Authors: Saraswat, S.K.; Digalwar, A.K.; Yadav, S.S.; Kumar, G.
Title: MCDM and GIS based modelling technique for assessment of solar and wind farm locations in India
Abstract: In the recent past, various factors have led to an increase in the use of renewable energy sources, among which, the depleting fossil fuel reserves, increasing fuel prices, and rising environmental concerns are the most prominent. With this increasing reliance on renewable energy sources, a proper assessment of the suitable sites becomes necessary for the optimum utilization of these resources. The present study investigates the spatial suitability of the solar and wind farms locations in India based on the technical, economic, and socio-environmental perspectives. The analysis is performed with the coupled use of the Geographical Information System (GIS) and Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) approaches. Analysis of present research work shows that 4.13% of the study area (133,874 km2) is highly suitable for the deployment of solar plants while 0.91% of the total area (29,457 km2) is highly suitable for the wind farms. The study further concludes that the Rajasthan state in India has the highest suitable land for the installation of solar plants (20,881 km2) as well as wind farms (6323 km2). The proposed model can be used for the development of policies related to renewable energy resources and the assessment of suitability of already sanctioned projects. © 2021 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Analytical hierarchy process; GIS; India; Multi-criteria decision making; Site suitability analysis; Solar plant; Wind farm
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric utilities; Fossil fuels; Proven reserves; Wind power; Environmental concerns; Fuel prices; Multi-criteria decision making; Renewable energy source; Solar and wind farms; Solar plant; Study areas; Use of renewable energies; Geographic information systems; assessment method; fossil fuel; GIS; installation; multicriteria analysis; numerical model; research work; solar power; wind farm; India; Rajasthan
Metodología: “Assessment of suitable sites for solar and wind farms involves conflicting issues and certain complexities which necessarily require an advanced decision-making approach. Among the available alternatives, the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) decision-making approach has a strong ability to deal with complex and conflicting problems with different criteria. The pair-wise comparison among the criteria and sub-criteria is done using the 9-point Satty fundamental linguistic scale. To make the pair-wise comparison, five experts are selected and asked to share their opinions using the provided linguistic scale. These linguistic weights were converted into the fuzzy geometric mean (GM) value using Buckley’s GM approach. These fuzzy geometric mean values were converted into the fuzzy weights, and further aggregated values of these fuzzy weights were given the weights or performance score of criteria. The AHP approach provided a good factor to check the consistency of the expert’s weight called the Consistency Ratio (CR). The weights of the factors were obtained from the F-AHP approach and based on the experts’ judgment collected from interviews.”

Authors: Xu, Y.; Li, Y.; Zheng, L.; Cui, L.; Li, S.; Li, W.; Cai, Y.
Title: Site selection of wind farms using GIS and multi-criteria decision making method in Wafangdian, China
Abstract: Site selection is one of the most significant decision making activities for development of wind energy resource. In this study, a novel method integrating Geographic Information System (GIS), interval Analytic Hierarchy Process (IAHP) and stochastic VIKOR was proposed to tackle site selection issue of wind farms in the Wafangdian region, China. Two major factors, i.e. biodiversity conservation and production safety, were utilized to determine feasible areas. Afterwards, the weight of evaluation criteria, including the social impact, economic benefit, terrain and eco-environment protection, were identified by using IAHP. Finally, the suitability indexes of various alternatives were calculated by stochastic VIKOR and their ranking was used to determine high-suitability areas for wind farm locations. The obtained results indicated that 30.2% of studied region were suitable for installing the wind-power facilities, but only 3.36% were determined to be highly suitable. By comparing the optimized results to the actual locations of existing wind farms, it was revealed that the ideological framework of this study were practical and effective in guiding site selection of wind farms, and they were also useful for applications in site selection of other renewable energy forms involving complex spatial analysis and multiple criteria evaluation, including solar, hydroelectric, geothermal and biomass. © 2020 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: China; GIS; IAHP; Site selection; Stochastic VIKOR; Wind energy

Metodología: En el artículo “Site selection of wind farms using GIS and multi-criteria decision making method in Wafangdian, China”, el proceso MCDM se desarrolla de manera secuencial y justificada, comenzando por la reducción del espacio de decisión mediante criterios de exclusión, ya que “the exclusion area is identified firstly based on three predefined restrictions in order to reduce the potential unreasonable evaluation”, incorporando explícitamente biodiversidad y seguridad productiva como filtros iniciales; posteriormente, los autores estructuran el problema en criterios y subcriterios y justifican el uso de IAHP porque “the interval numbers are capable of addressing the problem of which traditional fixed values inappropriate to reflect the comparison results among the criteria”, lo que permite manejar la incertidumbre inherente a los juicios expertos en la ponderación de criterios sociales, económicos, topográficos y ecoambientales; el cálculo de pesos se realiza paso a paso a partir de matrices de comparación por pares basadas en la escala de Saaty, destacando que “the magnitude of the weight reflects the degree of influence the criterion had on the overall objective”; una vez obtenidos los pesos, la evaluación de alternativas se efectúa mediante stochastic VIKOR, elegido porque “VIKOR is able to provide the compromise solution that is closest to the ideal and solves conflicting problems among multiple attributes”, incorporando variabilidad estocástica para reflejar diferencias entre 125 actores consultados, ya que “the scoring results are inadequately expressed as deterministic values”; finalmente, los índices de compromiso QiQ_iQi​ se utilizan para jerarquizar las ubicaciones y definir categorías de idoneidad, validando el enfoque al señalar que la comparación con parques eólicos existentes demuestra que “the ideological framework of this study were practical and effective in guiding site selection of wind farms”, cerrando así un proceso MCDM coherente, justificado metodológicamente y alineado con regulaciones ambientales y energéticas vigentes.“four groups of factors including social impact, economic benefit, terrain and ecological environment criteria are concerned in this study” y, a continuación, se detallan seis criterios específicos: “C1 – wind speed; C2 – slope; C3 – distance to nearest electricity grid; C4 – distance to main roads; C5 – protected bird areas; C6 – distance to urban areas”

Authors: Rekik, S.; El Alimi, S.
Title: Optimal wind-solar site selection using a GIS-AHP based approach: A case of Tunisia
Abstract: Constructing large wind power plants (WPPs) and solar photovoltaic plants (SPVPs) is a significant long-term investment. Due to the various conflicting factors involved in the selection process, determining the most appropriate locations prior to deploying such facilities is of paramount importance. In this paper, we propose a preliminary assessment of the most promising sites in Tunisia to host large-scale WPPs and SPVPs using geographical information systems (GIS) and multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM). A study of this kind, focusing on both resources, has not been conducted in Tunisia. An in-depth literature review has been conducted in order to determine suitability criteria as well as constraint factors. The analytical hierarchy process (AHP) is used to assign weights to the considered criteria; then, the final suitability maps are generated using the weighted overlay tool under ArcGis 10.8 software. The study findings indicate that there are large areas that are suitable for the deployment of large-scale WPPs and SPVPs, covering 3335 km2 (2.15 % of the total area) and 3815 km2 (2.57 %), respectively. The most suitable wind sites are scattered northwest to southwest, southeast, and to a lesser extent north. In contrast, ideal solar sites are mainly distributed in the central and southern regions of the country. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that these designated locations are capable of providing an estimated annual energy of 40.896 TWh and 781.83 TWh for wind and solar, respectively. As a result of the adopted model in this study, policymakers could be more proactive in developing solar and wind farms. This would increase the possibility of Tunisia achieving its 2030 target. © 2023 The Authors
Author Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; GIS; Multi-criteria decision making; Solar and wind energy; Tunisia
Index Keywords: Decision making; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Information use; Investments; Site selection; Solar power generation; Wind power; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Geographical information; Geographical information system; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Solar and wind energies; Solar photovoltaic plants; Tunisia; Wind power plants; Analytic hierarchy process
Metodología: En el artículo “Optimal wind-solar site selection using a GIS-AHP based approach: A case of Tunisia”, los autores describen paso a paso un proceso MCDM integrado GIS–AHP claramente estructurado: primero, “an in-depth literature review has been conducted in order to determine suitability criteria as well as constraint factors”, justificando así la identificación inicial de criterios a partir de trabajos previos y opinión experta ; posteriormente, se explica que “after consulting with experts and referring to related literature, climatology, topography, and accessibility were identified as the main factors”, estableciendo la jerarquía del problema y la necesidad de incorporar criterios heterogéneos para un problema espacial complejo ; en una segunda etapa, “the analytical hierarchy process (AHP) is used to assign weights to the considered criteria”, decisión que se justifica porque el AHP “is simple, easily implemented, capable of handling both quantitative as well as qualitative data, and considers the consistency and inconsistency of alternatives”, lo que lo hace adecuado frente a otros métodos más complejos para este tipo de selección territorial ; el procedimiento AHP se detalla mediante la construcción de matrices de comparación por pares usando la escala de Saaty, el cálculo de los pesos relativos y la verificación de consistencia, indicando que “if CR ≤ 0.10, the degree of consistency is acceptable”, lo cual garantiza la validez de los juicios expertos ; a continuación, los autores integran los pesos obtenidos en el entorno SIG señalando que “the final suitability maps are generated using the weighted overlay tool under ArcGis 10.8 software”, justificando así la combinación GIS–MCDM para transformar criterios ponderados en mapas espaciales de aptitud ; finalmente, el estudio aclara que el análisis se ejecuta en cuatro pasos (eliminación de áreas no aptas mediante restricciones, ponderación AHP, reclasificación de capas y superposición ponderada), lo que permite una evaluación sistemática y reproducible del problema de decisión espacial . En total, el modelo utiliza 9 variables/criterios para la evaluación (velocidad del viento, irradiación solar global, temperatura, pendiente, uso del suelo, orientación/aspecto, distancia a la red eléctrica, distancia a vías de transporte y distancia a áreas residenciales), lo cual se establece explícitamente cuando se indica que “wind speed, global solar irradiance (GHI), temperature, slope, aspect, landuse, proximity to the grid network, transport links, and residential areas were designated as criteria” .

Authors: Raza, M.A.; Yousif, M.; Hassan, M.; Numan, M.; Abbas Kazmi, S.A.
Title: Site suitability for solar and wind energy in developing countries using combination of GIS- AHP; a case study of Pakistan
Abstract: Climate change and scarcity of energy sources have put developing countries in a situation that demands emergency future planning for matured renewable technologies in order to continue sustainable progress and provide clean and cheap energy to masses. This paper provides a comprehensive structure of site suitability for utility scale solar PV and wind energy projects considering criteria critical to each technology while only using criteria with absolute necessity for varying scenarios incorporating Multi Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) along with Geographic Information System (GIS). A novel approach is also considered for far-off areas potentially deprived of electricity access for small scale solar PV implementation. A total of eight sub-criteria were considered in site selection process regarding wind and solar energy, analyzed using Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) in order to rank criteria depending on their importance to selection process. Resulting methodology was applied to case study of Pakistan, a developing country with growing climate change emergency and ongoing power crisis. End results showed 25.28% area suitable for utility scale solar PV farms, 5.93% area suitable for utility scale wind farms and 40.02% suitable area for small scale solar PV farms. © 2023 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Multi criteria decision analysis (MCDA); Optimal sites; Remote areas; Renewable energy; Solar PV; Wind farms
Index Keywords: Climate change; Decision making; Electric utilities; Energy policy; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Site selection; Solar energy; Wind power; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Case-studies; Multi criteria decision analyse; Multi-criteria decision analysis; Optimal site; Pakistan; Remote areas; Renewable energies; Solar PVs; Wind farm; Developing countries; alternative energy; analytical hierarchy process; climate change; developing world; GIS; multicriteria analysis; photovoltaic system; solar power; wind farm; wind power
Metodología: En este trabajo, los autores desarrollan un **proceso MCDM integrado con SIG** cuyo punto de partida es la definición explícita del problema de decisión, orientado a “*provide a comprehensive planning structure for site suitability of renewable energy farms implementation for developing countries*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, lo que justifica el uso de MCDA debido a la complejidad multivariable y a la necesidad de incorporar criterios técnicos, climáticos y espaciales; posteriormente, se realiza la **selección de criterios**, donde se establece que “*a total of eight sub-criteria were considered for each scenario from climatology, orography, technical and location criteria varying on the type of technology and scenario*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, confirmando explícitamente el uso de **ocho variables** por escenario; el **método MCDM elegido es el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)**, decisión que se justifica porque “*it provides answer to multivariable complex problems depending on expert opinion from decision makers*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, permitiendo estructurar el problema jerárquicamente; el proceso continúa con la **ponderación de criterios**, donde “*Application of AHP for weight distribution to considered criteria suggested priority weights*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, obtenidos mediante comparaciones pareadas basadas en literatura previa y juicio experto; posteriormente, los criterios ponderados son **integrados espacialmente en ArcGIS**, ya que “*Satellite driven data for criteria was further processed via ArcGIS (v.10.7) for spatial analysis and formation of final map layer after combination with priority weights obtained from AHP*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, justificando la agregación MCDM-SIG por su capacidad de representar visualmente la idoneidad espacial; finalmente, se genera el **ranking implícito de áreas aptas** mediante mapas de idoneidad, donde se reporta que “*Final suitability maps suggested 25.28%, 5.93% and 40.02% suitable areas for case 1, 2 and 3 after exclusion of restrictive criteria*” 1-s2.0-S0960148123001520-main, consolidando así un proceso MCDM secuencial, jerárquico y espacialmente explícito basado en **AHP con ocho criterios por escenario**, plenamente justificado por la naturaleza multivariable y territorial del problema de decisión.

Authors: Vinhoza, A.; Schaeffer, R.
Title: Brazil's offshore wind energy potential assessment based on a Spatial Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis
Abstract: Brazil is an emerging market for offshore wind energy, with its first projects having already applied for environmental permits. However, few studies have actually evaluated Brazil's offshore potential, and those analyses have focused on its gross resource potential. This paper thus aims to assess Brazil's offshore wind potential through a novel approach that allows for the evaluation and mapping of the potential in different levels (Gross, Technical and Environmental and Social) and highlights the most economically attractive areas for offshore wind deployment, following a Spatial Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis. Results for Brazil show 1688 GW of Gross Potential, 1064 GW of Technical Potential and 330 GW of Environmental and Social Potential. The least economically attractive areas are mainly in the Southeast region, while the most attractive ones are concentrated in the Northeast region. The top ten areas for offshore development are also highlighted, totaling 126 GW of capacity. In short, this paper addresses the knowledge gap towards a comprehensive potential assessment for Brazil and concludes that the country has a significant offshore wind potential that can be sustainably deployed if proper siting criteria are applied. The approach proposed here is replicable and capable of informing the planning of sustainable offshore wind development in other regions or countries. © 2021 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Brazil; GIS; Offshore wind potential; Offshore wind siting criteria; Spatial multi-criteria decision analysis; Suitability map
Index Keywords: Decision making; Offshore oil well production; Spatial variables measurement; Wind power; Brazil; Multi-criteria decision analysis; Off-shore wind energy; Offshore wind potential; Offshore wind siting criteria; Offshore winds; Spatial multi-criteria decision analyse; Suitability map; Wind potential; Geographic information systems
Metodología: el estudio aplica un **Spatial Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (SMCDA)** integrado con **AHP** para abordar un problema espacial multivariable, justificando su uso porque “*it can happen that some siting studies involve a wide set of variables, or factors, and the solution relies on a compromise between them*”, señalando que cuando existen variables espaciales “*the MCDA methodology can be blended with GIS software, resulting in the so-called Spatial Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (SMCDA)*” 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; el proceso se estructura jerárquicamente siguiendo AHP, donde “*the objective is to minimize the costs associated with offshore wind projects*”, los criterios corresponden a factores económicos y las alternativas son los puntos espaciales disponibles tras aplicar restricciones técnicas, ambientales y sociales 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; primero se justifica la **aplicación secuencial de restricciones** (no como factores ponderados) porque “*technical, environmental and social criteria are treated as constraints, leading to the exclusion of regions that do not comply with the stipulated thresholds*”, lo que permite evaluar el potencial en niveles (Gross, Technical, Environmental and Social) y reducir progresivamente el espacio de decisión antes del MCDM 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; posteriormente, el MCDM se aplica **solo a factores económicos**, ya que “*economic aspects are not considered to be exclusions, but factors that demand a compromise to reach a specific common purpose*”, lo que justifica el uso de ponderaciones en lugar de umbrales rígidos 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; el artículo utiliza **cuatro variables económicas**, explícitamente citadas como “*four were selected to be assessed in the present paper as economic factors: wind speed, water depth, distance to shore and distance to ports*” 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; estas variables se reclasifican en clases de aptitud comunes (“*reclassified as suitability scores: 0 – Unsuitable, 1 – Low, 2 – Medium, 3 – High*”) para hacerlas comparables 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; luego se realiza la **comparación por pares** con AHP porque “*comparison is based on experts’ judgment and knowledge and uses the fundamental scale proposed by Saaty*”, obteniendo una matriz de comparación, su normalización y los **pesos relativos** de cada criterio 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; la coherencia del juicio se valida calculando el **Consistency Ratio**, el cual resulta aceptable (“*the calculated CR is 0.007, indicating consistency*”), justificando la validez del proceso de ponderación 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main; finalmente, la agregación se realiza mediante una **suma ponderada** (“*the final value is calculated as the weighted sum of the suitability scores received in each economic factor*”), implementada en GIS para generar el mapa final de aptitud y el ranking espacial de alternativas 1-s2.0-S1364032121004731-main

Authors: Nadizadeh Shorabeh, S.N.; Firozjaei, H.K.; Karimi Firozjaei, M.K.; Jelokhani-Niaraki, M.; Homaee, M.; Nematollahi, O.
Title: The site selection of wind energy power plant using GIS-multi-criteria evaluation from economic perspectives
Abstract: The use of wind turbines can help progress towards economic and technological development, lower rates of fossil fuel consumption, decreased greenhouse emissions, and reduced side-effects of climate change. A successful mechanism for developing renewable energy worldwide is the guaranteed purchase of electricity generated from renewable energy sources. Accordingly, this study aims to integrate Geographic Information System-based Multi-criteria Evaluation (GIS-MCE) models with economic frameworks to estimate the optimal purchasing price for electricity produced by wind turbines. A total of 13 criteria maps were used and integrated using Ordered Weighted Averaging (OWA) as a type of MCE model. The criteria were initially normalized based on the minimum, and maximum values and weights were assigned to each criterion, using the Best-Worst method. The OWA model identified optimal site locations at various decision risk levels. The economic efficiency of wind turbines and the potential purchasing price of electricity from turbines were also assessed in terms of Net Present Value (NPV). The results show that Ardabil and Southern Khorasan provinces had the most significant areas in the very-suitable class for wind turbine installation (small/large scale). The purchasing prices for wind-generated electricity ranged from 0.047 to 0.182 US$ for large wind farms and 0.074 to 0.384 US$ for small wind plants. The highest electricity produced from large wind farms was found in Maragheh. © 2022 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Economic model; GIS; Multi-criteria evaluation; Optimal price; Wind energy
Index Keywords: Climate change; Electric power generation; Electric utilities; Fossil fuel power plants; Fossil fuels; Geographic information systems; Purchasing; Sales; Site selection; Wind turbines; Economic development; Economic models; Economic perspective; Energies (power); Multi-criteria evaluation; Multi-criterion evaluation; Optimal price; Ordered weighted averaging; Technological development; Wind farm; Wind power
Metodología: El proceso de toma de decisiones multicriterio se estructuró en fases secuenciales que iniciaron con la generación de capas espaciales mediante el algoritmo de vecino más cercano, donde "the nearest neighbor algorithm was used to obtain values of different pixels and create criteria maps". Tras normalizar los datos, se aplicó el Best-Worst Method (BWM) para la ponderación, justificando su elección frente a otros métodos debido a que "it uses fewer pairwise comparisons, and second, it has a higher consistency ratio". Para la fase de agregación, se utilizó el modelo Ordered Weighted Averaging (OWA), seleccionado específicamente porque "is capable of identifying optimal site locations at various decision risk levels" , lo que permitió a los autores "provide various scenarios based on different degrees of risk-taking or risk-aversion". Finalmente, la idoneidad espacial se vinculó con una evaluación económica donde "NPV was used to obtain the optimal purchasing price for electricity produced from small and large wind power plants". Respecto a la complejidad del modelo, se empleó un robusto conjunto de datos compuesto por un total de 13 variables, tal como se indica al precisar que "a total of 13 criteria maps were used and integrated using Ordered Weighted Averaging (OWA)"

Authors: Tercan, E.; Tapkin, S.; Latinopoulos, D.; Dereli, M.A.; Tsiropoulos, A.; Ak, M.F.
Title: A GIS-based multi-criteria model for offshore wind energy power plants site selection in both sides of the Aegean Sea
Abstract: Location selection for offshore wind farms is a major challenge for renewable energy policy, marine spatial planning, and environmental conservation. This selection constitutes a multi-criteria decision-making problem, through which parameters like wind velocity, water depth, shorelines, fishing areas, shipping routes, environmental protection areas, transportation, and military zones should be jointly investigated. The aim of the present study was thus to develop an integrated methodology for assessing the siting of bottom-fixed offshore wind farms in two different countries (with different legal, political, and socio/economic characteristics). Our methodology combined multi-criteria decision-making methods and geographical information systems and was implemented in Cyclades (Greece) and in the sea area of İzmir region (Turkey). Experts used fuzzy sets and linguistic terms to achieve more consistent and independent rankings and results. In the Turkish region, the results showed that 519 km2 (10.23%) of the study area is suitable for offshore wind farms, while in the Greek region, only 289 km2 (3.22%) of the study area was found to be suitable. This spatial suitability analysis may contribute to provide some useful recommendations for the spatial marine planning at the regional scale, as well as for the preliminary assessment of new offshore wind farms in both countries. © 2020, Springer Nature Switzerland AG.

Metodología: El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo a través de cuatro etapas fundamentales: inicialmente se realizó «the exclusion of the areas that are incompatible with the development of offshore wind farms» , justificando este paso en que dichas zonas «are either protected or utilized for other human activities... or unsuitable for offshore wind development due to physical and technical reasons». Posteriormente, se procedió a la evaluación de las áreas restantes mediante el método Buckley FAHP para calcular los pesos de importancia de los criterios , técnica que fue preferida para evitar limitaciones como el «irrational zero weight in criteria selection» y bajo la premisa de que «fuzzy numbers can better reflect expert judgment... and fuzzy numbers make it easier for decision-makers to evaluate to achieve the main goal». En la tercera fase, se aplicó el modelo para obtener el índice de idoneidad (SI) utilizando la técnica de Weighted linear combination (WLC) , cuya elección se justifica porque en este enfoque «each parameter (criterion) is multiplied by a certain weight and the final results are summed up... to obtain the result suitability index». Para estructurar este análisis, se emplearon un total de 9 variables críticas, citando que «nine almost identical criteria were used in both study areas (countries)» , las cuales abarcaron factores técnicos, económicos, ambientales y sociales.

Authors: Taoufik, M.; Fekri, A.
Title: GIS-based multi-criteria analysis of offshore wind farm development in Morocco
Abstract: This investigation focuses on offshore wind resource assessment in Morocco using an integrated methodology consisting of Geographic Information System and the Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process. Following the published literature and regional necessities, a set of suitability criteria is defined including eight different site selection criteria and six exclusion factors. A weighted overlay analysis is conducted in order to map the ideal locations for offshore wind energy development in Morocco. The study results highlight a wide offshore area that is most suited for wind farm development. Three high suitable areas are shortlisted within the Atlantic Ocean boundary of Morocco, in proximity to the cities of Essaouira, Boujdour, and Dakhla. The three selected areas are capable of generating around 13.3 GW of electrical power. The suggested methodology can be of immense aid for locating the most suitable areas for offshore wind farms in different regions having equivalent geo-factors. © 2021
Author Keywords: Fuzzy AHP; GIS; MCDA; Morocco; Offshore; Suitability map; Wind farm
Index Keywords: Electric power system interconnection; Geographic information systems; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Site selection; Fuzzy AHP; MCDA; Morocco; Multicriteria analysis; Offshore wind resources; Offshores; Suitability map; Wind farm; Wind farm development; Wind resource assessment; Electric utilities
Metodología: El proceso MCDM se estructuró mediante una metodología integrada de SIG y el Proceso Jerárquico Analítico Difuso (Fuzzy-AHP), que comenzó con la definición de «a set of suitability criteria... including eight different site selection criteria and six exclusion factors». El procedimiento se ejecutó paso a paso iniciando con «Step 1: Draw the hierarchical chart to determine the desired solution» , seguido por «Step 2: The development of judgment matrices by pairwise comparisons according to Saaty’s rating scale» , «Step 3: Defuzzifying the fuzzy pairwise comparison matrices» , «Step 4: Calculating the Consistency Rate (C.R)» , «Step 5: Checking if C.R < 0.1» y finalmente «Step 6: Calculating weights of the pairwise comparison matrices». Los autores justificaron el uso de la lógica difusa explicando que, en el AHP básico, las «imprecisions stemming from individual subjective judgments could introduce errors» , por lo que el enfoque Fuzzy-AHP es superior al utilizar un rango de valores para «integrating decision maker uncertainties». Para la obtención de los resultados finales, se realizó un «weighted overlay analysis... in order to map the ideal locations» , tras haber reclasificado todos los datos en una «common-scale that ranges between 0 and 5». El estudio integró un total de 14 variables, citando explícitamente el uso de «eight different site selection criteria and six exclusion factors»

Authors: Genç, M.S.; Karipoğlu, F.; Koca, K.; Azgin, Ş.T.
Title: Suitable site selection for offshore wind farms in Turkey’s seas: GIS-MCDM based approach
Abstract: Offshore wind energy resources are not fully exploited renewable energy resources until now and could play a crucial role in mitigating the impacts of climate change by the generation of renewable electricity. Planning processes such as technical, social, environmental, various agents, and political concerns are necessary for the development of offshore wind energy projects. The objective of this study was to assess the comprehensive feasibility of a desired offshore wind power plant applying Geographical Information Systems (GIS) and Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) guidance for the coastal area of Turkey. Furthermore, EMODnet (the European Marine Observation and Data Network) was employed for data acquisition to unlock fragmented and hidden marine data resources and to facilitate investment in sustainable coastal and offshore activities. For the determination of potential site with Multi-Criteria Decision Maker Method, 3 main criteria Technical (C1), Environmental (C2), and Social (C3), and 13 sub-criteria were determined. Based on these criteria, the suitability map was created by using all criteria map layers with their buffer zones. The final map indicated that %1.38 (3294.8 km2) of Turkey Seas was suitable for offshore wind farms. The most suitable region was determined in the Marmara Sea with 1194 km2. The Aegean Sea, the Black Sea, and the Mediterranean Sea were following the Marmara Sea in terms of the huge suitable regions respectively. It was apparent that the growth of offshore wind farms in Turkey would increase if the supporting mechanism and the necessary legislation were ensured. © 2021, The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature.
Author Keywords: EMODnet; Feasible site selection; Geographic information system (GIS); Offshore wind farm; Renewable energy

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para la selección de sitios se llevó a cabo siguiendo una metodología estructurada en cuatro etapas: «(i) defining offshore wind farms criteria, (ii) data collection and processing... (iii) determine restrictions which are important for suitable site selection and defining buffer zones of restrictions, (iv) in the last stage to indicate suitable sites and locations for offshore wind power installation». Los autores justificaron el uso de técnicas MCDM argumentando que son «tools that allow the best choice to be made from multiple and concurrent criteria» , especialmente cuando «economic considerations are complemented with environmental and social considerations, leading to multiple-criteria decision-making being used to deal with conflicting decision problems». Una particularidad metodológica fue que «all weights for criteria were accepted equal to 1», decisión justificada por la «lack of offshore wind power plant applications in Turkey». Para la ejecución del modelo, se definieron un total de 16 variables, citando que «3 main criteria Technical (C1), Environmental (C2), and Social (C3), and 13 sub-criteria were determined»  para evaluar la idoneidad del terreno.

Authors: Sotiropoulou, K.F.; Vavatsikos, A.P.
Title: Onshore wind farms GIS-Assisted suitability analysis using PROMETHEE II
Abstract: The increased use of renewable energy will reduce greenhouse gas emissions and comply with the European Union's commitment under the 2015 Paris Agreement on Climate Change. Wind energy generation contributes to the attainment of the Union's objectives because it is a mature and cost-effective national investment. Moreover, wind energy is part of the transition towards a circular economy primarily by producing clean renewable energy with a low environmental impact. The appropriate selection of both materials and sites is critical to ensure efficiency and low local impact. The selection of a wind farm installation site is a spatially complex decision-making problem that aims to evaluate alternative locations using planning and environmental restrictions. This paper introduces a new decision-making framework that combines geographic information systems (GIS) technology and PROMETHEE II, a multi-attribute decision making (MADM) method, to enable the GIS-assisted wind farm suitability analysis. The proposed framework can be used as a strategic planning tool that can help energy planners, wind developers, and decision and policy makers at the national and regional levels to accurately determine and predict the most efficient locations for wind farm development. The framework is illustrated through a case study of the Thrace region of north-eastern Greece. © 2021
Author Keywords: Geographic information systems (GIS); Multicriteria decision analysis (MCDA); PROMETHEE II method; Suitability analysis; Wind farm siting
Index Keywords: Climate change; Cost effectiveness; Decision theory; Electric utilities; Gas emissions; Geographic information systems; Greenhouse gases; Information systems; Information use; Investments; Site selection; Wind power; Energy; Geographic information; Geographic information system; Multicriterium decision analyse; Onshore winds; PROMETHEE II method; PROMETHEE-II; Suitability analysis; Wind farm; Wind farm siting; Decision making; alternative energy; economic system; European Union; GIS; multicriteria analysis; wind farm; wind power; Greece; Thrace
Metodología: El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando sistemas de información geográfica (GIS) con el método PROMETHEE II, estructurado en una serie de pasos que comienzan con la «identification of areas for wind farm siting based on their wind potential», seguido por la especificación de «constraints and decision criteria» para crear los mapas correspondientes1. Debido a las «computational limitations» que impiden aplicar PROMETHEE directamente sobre cada celda de un mapa raster, los autores justificaron la creación de un «sample dataset... forming a fishnet of points» para representar uniformemente el área2. Posteriormente, se implementó PROMETHEE II para estimar los «net preference flows $(\phi_{net})$» en los puntos muestreados y se utilizó la técnica de Interpolación de Distancia Inversa (IDW) para «generate the net preference flow surface for the entire examined area»3. Esta metodología se justifica porque, a diferencia de los modelos aditivos comunes, en los métodos de superación (outranking) «alternatives standardisation is performed based on the difference in their performances... and not to their absolute values», lo que permite realizar análisis de idoneidad absoluta y relativa en paralelo4. El estudio empleó un total de 18 variables (atributos), citando que «the decision model considers the design, development, environmental, and safety-related siting decision criteria and constraints measured by 18 attributes»5.

Authors: Imam, A.A.; Abusorrah, A.; Marzband, M.
Title: Potentials and opportunities of solar PV and wind energy sources in Saudi Arabia: Land suitability, techno-socio-economic feasibility, and future variability
Abstract: Solar and wind energy sources hold significant potential to meet the escalating energy demand in Saudi Arabia sustainably. This research aims to assess the feasibility and prospects of deploying solar photovoltaic (PV) and wind energy systems in Saudi Arabia (SA). The study adopts a comprehensive approach, encompassing spatial analysis of land suitability, techno-socio-economic feasibility studies, and prediction of renewable energy (RE) resource variability and trends using machine learning techniques. Geographical Information System (GIS) spatial analysis is employed to identify suitable areas for establishing solar PV and wind farms based on multi-criteria evaluation methods. Techno-socio-economic feasibility analysis of solar PV and wind energy systems, under various configurations and scenarios, is conducted using the System Advisor Model (SAM) tool. Furthermore, a Support Vector Machine (SVM) machine learning algorithm is developed and deployed to forecast Global Horizontal Irradiation (GHI), wind speed, and influential weather parameters at different locations in SA. The projections of these variables derived from global climate models are utilized to analyze prospects and variabilities. The land suitability analysis identified four optimal locations for large-scale solar PV fields in Tabuk, Al Madinah, Makkah, and Riyadh provinces. Additionally, four promising wind farms were found in Al Madinah, Makkah, Riyadh, and Eastern provinces. The analysis also identified one location in Al Jouf province suitable for hybrid systems combining solar PV and wind energy. The techno-economic assessment revealed that wind farms performed best overall, achieving a capacity factor of 42.6 % in Al Madinah province. Although current tariffs render projects economically unviable, solar PV, wind energy, and hybrid solar PV-wind technologies are economically feasible in SA at Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) rates above $32.8/MWh, $26.1/MWh, and $50.6/MWh, respectively. The social development analysis estimated potential job creation from solar PV and wind energy deployment under different scenarios from 2020 to 2060, indicating that more ambitious climate targets could translate to millions of renewable energy jobs. The SVM model predicted solar irradiance with an R-squared value of 0.893. The CMIP6 model was then used to project the GHI for SA in 2049, suggesting an increase of approximately 19 %. Wind speed is also expected to rise roughly 5 % over the same period. The integration of GIS spatial analysis, techno-socio-economic modeling, and machine learning-based forecasting provides comprehensive insights into harnessing solar and wind energy in SA. This study facilitates evidence-based planning and risk assessment, crucial for a sustainable energy transition. The insights and roadmap derived from this research can inform policy frameworks, support the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UNSDGs), and attract private investments for RE development in SA. Consequently, this study establishes solar and wind energy as viable and promising solutions for meeting SA's growing energy demands sustainably, while minimizing the associated environmental impact. © 2024 The Authors

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se estructuró en un marco integral de seis etapas que combina análisis espacial, modelado tecno-económico y aprendizaje automático. La fase de selección de sitios se basó en un enfoque de evaluación multicriterio asistido por GIS, el cual comenzó con la «identification of site selection criteria» y la «data collection and GIS-based processing». Los autores utilizaron el método AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) para la asignación de pesos, justificando su elección en la «capability to handle complex decision-making problems with multiple criteria» y su habilidad para proporcionar un «structured approach to decision-making by breaking down the problem into a hierarchy». Para obtener los resultados espaciales, se empleó la técnica de Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) para integrar las capas de criterios ponderados, mientras que las restricciones se aplicaron mediante «Boolean Logic Restrictions Integration» usando puertas lógicas AND para generar el mapa de idoneidad final. El estudio es notable por integrar además un análisis tecno-económico mediante el software SAM y un modelo de pronóstico basado en Support Vector Machine (SVM) para predecir la variabilidad futura de los recursos. En total, el modelo de idoneidad consideró un conjunto robusto de variables, citando que se evaluaron «various evaluation criteria» que abarcan dimensiones técnicas, económicas, ambientales y sociales, incluyendo niveles de irradiación solar, velocidades del viento, restricciones de uso de suelo y proximidad a la infraestructura.

Authors: Caceoğlu, E.; Yildiz, H.K.; Oğuz, E.; Huvaj, N.; Guerrero, J.M.
Title: Offshore wind power plant site selection using Analytical Hierarchy Process for Northwest Turkey
Abstract: This study presents a quantitative methodology for offshore wind power plant site selection in Northwest Turkey using Geographical Information Systems (GIS) and Analytical Hierarchy Process, which is one of the Multi-Criteria Decision Making methods. A comprehensive literature review is conducted to identify more than 17 site selection criteria and required GIS data were collected accordingly. Site selection criteria are classified as decision and exclusion criteria. Decision criteria along with their suitable limits and exclusion criteria are evaluated in GIS resulting in five alternative suitable sites. Alternative site pairwise comparison matrices are formed in terms of each decision criterion. In the meantime, a hierarchy of decision criteria is formed, and a questionnaire comparing the relative importance of decision criteria is conducted on national and international experts. Electricity grid connection, offshore average wind speed at 100 m height and environmental concerns are found to be the most important decision criteria. Relative importance of decision criteria and alternative site comparisons are combined in two different analyses, namely “scenario analysis” and “consensus analysis”. Out of the five alternative sites, Bozcaada is concluded to be the most suitable site, and the second-best alternative is Kıyıköy. © 2022 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Analytical hierarchy process; Geographical information systems; Multi-criteria decision making; Offshore wind energy; Site selection

Metodología: El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), estructurado en pasos secuenciales que comenzaron con «identifying the criteria through a detailed literature review», seguido por la categorización en «decision and exclusion criteria». Los autores justificaron el uso de AHP señalando que es una herramienta que «helps decision-makers to solve complex problems with multiple criteria by prioritizing alternatives and making the best decision», permitiendo que «relative weights of the decision criteria are calculated via pairwise comparison matrices» para reducir la subjetividad. Una fase crítica del proceso fue la «reclassification of each decision criterion to a common scale from 1 to 5», lo que permitió la agregación final mediante la técnica de Weighted Overlay Analysis para generar el «suitability map». El estudio empleó un total de 17 variables, citando que se identificaron «more than 17 site selection criteria», divididas específicamente en «7 decision criteria» (como velocidad del viento y profundidad) y «10 exclusion criteria» (como rutas de navegación y áreas protegidas) para garantizar la viabilidad técnica y ambiental del proyecto.

Authors: Díaz, H.; Guedes Soares, C.G.
Title: A multi-criteria approach to evaluate floating offshore wind farms siting in the canary islands (Spain)
Abstract: The study presents a methodology for floating wind farms site selection with a Canary Islands case study. The frame combines geographical information systems (GIS) and multiple criteria decision methods (MCDMs). First, the problematic areas for the installation of the turbines are identified through a GIS database application. This tool generates thematic layers representing exclusion criteria. Then, at the second stage of the study, available maritime locations are analyzed and ranked using the analytical hierarchy process (AHP), based on technical, economic, and environmental aspects. AHP’s technique guarantee the elimination of the judgment’s subjectivity. The study also compared the solutions of the AHP technique with other methods, such as Preference Ranking Organization METHod for Enrichment of Evaluations (PROMETHEE), ELimination Et Choix Traduisant la Realité (ELECTRE III), Technique for Order Preferences by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) and Weighted Sum Algorithm (WSA(). The main result of this study is the creation of a realistic and objective overview of floating offshore wind farm site selection and the contribution to minimize the environmental impacts and to reduce the social conflicts between stakeholders. © 2021 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.
Author Keywords: Canary Islands; Floating wind farm; Multicriteria decision making; Offshore wind; Site selection; Spatial energy planning
Index Keywords: Electric utilities; Environmental impact; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Offshore oil well production; Site selection; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Environmental aspects; Ideal solutions; Multi-criteria approach; Multiple-criteria decisions; Preference ranking; Social conflicts; Weighted sum algorithm; Offshore wind farms
Metodología: El proceso MCDM se desarrolló bajo un enfoque híbrido que combinó GIS con los métodos AHP y TOPSIS, estructurado en tres fases principales: «the first step consists of a GIS analysis to identify the feasible areas... the second step focuses on determining the criteria weights using the AHP... and the final step applies the TOPSIS method to rank the potential locations». Los autores justificaron el uso de esta combinación señalando que el AHP permite «structuring the problem into a hierarchy and evaluating the relative importance of criteria», mientras que TOPSIS fue elegido para la etapa de clasificación final debido a su capacidad para encontrar la alternativa que está «the shortest distance from the positive ideal solution and the farthest distance from the negative ideal solution». Además, se justificó el uso de una «multi-criteria approach» para abordar la complejidad de las instalaciones flotantes, donde las restricciones técnicas y ambientales son críticas. El estudio utilizó un total de 13 variables, citando que el modelo se basó en «the definition of 13 criteria (7 restriction and 6 assessment criteria)», los cuales incluyeron desde la velocidad del viento y la batimetría hasta la proximidad a aeropuertos y áreas de patrimonio natural.

Authors: Zalhaf, A.S.; Elboshy, B.; Kotb, K.M.; Han, Y.; Almaliki, A.H.; Aly, R.M.H.; Elkadeem, M.R.
Title: A high-resolution wind farms suitability mapping using gis and fuzzy ahp approach: A national-level case study in Sudan
Abstract: Wind energy is one of the most attractive sustainable energy resources since it has low oper-ation, maintenance, and production costs and a relatively low impact on the environment. Identifying the optimal sites for installing wind power plants (WPPs) is considered an important challenge of wind energy development which requires careful and combined analyses of numerous criteria. This study introduces a high-resolution wind farms suitability mapping based on Fuzzy Analytical Hierarchy Process (FAHP) and Geographic Information System (GIS) approaches considering technical, environmental, social, and spatial aspects, representing eight different criteria. First, a multi-criteria decision-making analysis based on the FAHP method is employed to assign appropriate weights for the addressed criteria with respect to their relative importance. Since the traditional AHP method, which was found employed in the majority of the relative case-studies, is not efficient in dealing with uncertainty when experts use a basic scale (0 to 1) for their assessments, the FAHP provides more flexible scales through the utilized fuzzy membership functions and the natural linguistic variables. Consequently, this helps to facilitate the assessments made by experts and increases the precision of the obtained results (weights). Next, the high-resolution GIS is used to carry out a spatial analysis and integrate various factors/criteria throughout the proposed index to produce the final suitability map and identify the unsuitable areas. The presented study emphasizes investigating the lightning strike flash rate due to its significant influences on the wind turbine’s safety and operation, yet this crucial factor has been seldomly investigated in previous studies. The obtained findings revealed that the wind speed, the land slope, and the elevation had the highest weighted criteria with 33.1%, 24.8%, and 12.2%, respectively. Besides, the final-developed suitability map revealed that 23.22% and 8.31% of the Sudanese territory are of high and very high suitability, respectively, for wind farms installation which are considered sufficient to cover the electricity needs. The difficulty of acquiring real data and resources for the addressed location was the main challenge of the presented work. The work outlook addresses the suitability mapping of hybrid photovoltaic-wind turbine energy systems, which will require addressing new and significant criteria in the applied methodology. © 2021 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.
Author Keywords: Fuzzy Analytical Hierarchy Process (FAHP); Geographic Information System (GIS); Multi-criteria decision making; Suitability analysis; Wind farms
Index Keywords: alternative energy; electricity; GIS; spatial analysis; wind power; Sudan
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con la metodología Fuzzy AHP (FAHP), estructurándose en un procedimiento que comenzó con «identifying the criteria through a detailed literature review» para luego clasificarlos en «decision and exclusion criteria». Los autores justificaron el uso de la lógica difusa dentro del AHP señalando que el método tradicional «is not efficient in dealing with uncertainty when experts use a basic scale», mientras que el FAHP permite manejar la ambigüedad mediante «triangular fuzzy numbers» que capturan mejor el juicio humano. El flujo de trabajo continuó con la «determination of criteria weights using the fuzzy AHP method» y un «weighted overlay analysis» para combinar las capas espaciales, justificando que esta integración técnica es fundamental para «solve complex problems with multiple criteria by prioritizing alternatives». El estudio consideró un total de 19 variables, citando explícitamente la división entre «eleven constraint factors and eight evaluation factors», lo que permitió generar un mapa de idoneidad de alta resolución.

Authors: Cunden, T.S.M.; Doorga, J.; Lollchund, M.R.; Rughooputh, S.D.D.V.
Title: Multi-level constraints wind farms siting for a complex terrain in a tropical region using MCDM approach coupled with GIS
Abstract: This paper presents a Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) process together with Geographic Information System (GIS) to analyse multiple constraints that affect the siting of wind farms. Firstly, exclusion zones are identified and are removed from the potential wind farm placement regions of the wind resource map layer. A set of nine selected evaluation criteria are weighted using the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) in order to quantify their impacts on the placements of the wind farms. Constraints such as buffer zones and suitability scores are developed. Using these constraints, raster layers including the proximity to road network and high voltage transmission lines are created. Theses layers are then superimposed on raster layers comprising of high-resolution terrain DEM and raster dataset for wind speed and direction. The combination of the raster layers is done using the Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) technique to yield a wind resource map highlighting the regions for wind farms placements, classified according to their potential wind power density. As a case study, the methodology is applied to the island of Mauritius which has a complex topography. © 2020 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Decision criteria; GIS; MCDM; Wind farm siting

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) y la técnica de Weighted Linear Combination (WLC). El procedimiento se estructuró en una secuencia lógica que comenzó con la «identification of exclusion zones» , seguido por la «selection of nine evaluation criteria» que fueron ponderados mediante AHP , y finalizó con la «combination of the raster layers... using the Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) technique» para generar el mapa de idoneidad final. Los autores justificaron el uso del enfoque MCDM argumentando que proporciona un medio sustancial para «handle the shortcomings of GIS in analysing cases involving complex criteria and objectives». El estudio empleó un total de 19 variables, divididas en diez criterios de exclusión (como «airport, settlement areas, nature reserve/park») y nueve criterios de evaluación (como «wind speed, slope, and proximity to road network»)

Authors: Asadi, M.; Pourhossein, K.; Mohammadi-ivatloo, B.
Title: GIS-assisted modeling of wind farm site selection based on support vector regression
Abstract: The optimal siting of wind farms is a multi-attribute problem, and different objectives and constraints like economic, environmental, social, and technical should be taken into account. The widely-used category of site selection methods, multi-criteria decision making (MCDM), suffers from local scoring despite its numerous advantages. In this paper, support vector regression (SVR) is implemented to add the global scoring capability to MCDM by using a global-scale span of decision criteria. Here, the weighting of the decision criteria is obtained by the analytic hierarchy process (AHP), and then an SVR is utilized to appoint a score to any candidate site location. The proposed procedure has been used to study potential wind sites in Iran. In this regard, the study was conducted through three scenarios based on the natural and artificial potential of candidate sites. This study reveals that there are several vast areas, especially in eastern Iran, having high wind power density and flat terrain that are ready to be utilized as an endless source of clean energy provided that proper investment is made in power transmission lines and road networks. © 2023
Author Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process (AHP); Cleaner production; Geographic information system (GIS); Site selection; Support vector machine (SVM); Wind energy potential
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Decision making; Electric power transmission; Electric power transmission networks; Electric utilities; Hierarchical systems; Investments; Site selection; Support vector machines; Wind power; Cleaner production; Geographic information system; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Support vector machine; Support vector regressions; Support vectors machine; Wind energy potential; Wind farm; Geographic information systems
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se diseñó mediante una arquitectura híbrida que integra GIS, AHP y Support Vector Regression (SVR), con el fin de superar las limitaciones de la puntuación local. El procedimiento se ejecutó en etapas definidas: «firstly, all the criteria and sub-criteria for the wind farm site selection were determined», seguido por el cálculo de pesos mediante AHP, donde se justifica que este método permite «the weighting of the decision criteria is obtained by the expert’s knowledge and AHP method». La innovación técnica radica en que, tras obtener el índice de idoneidad (SI) inicial, se implementó SVR para añadir una «global scoring capability», justificando que mientras los métodos MCDM tradicionales sufren de «local scoring», el uso de SVR permite que «the suitability of the target site is determined relative to the global-scale values of the criteria». Finalmente, se utilizaron técnicas GIS para la «standardization and overlaying of layers». El estudio empleó un total de 10 variables (6 criterios de evaluación y 4 factores de exclusión), citando que la selección se basó en que «the optimal siting of wind farms is a multi-attribute problem, and different objectives and constraints like economic, environmental, social, and technical should be taken into account».

Authors: Gkeka-Serpetsidaki, P.; Tsoutsos, T.
Title: A methodological framework for optimal siting of offshore wind farms: A case study on the island of Crete
Abstract: Land-use conflicts, along with the increasing demand for green energy and the unexploited offshore wind potential, intensify the necessity of the development of Offshore Wind Farms (OWFs). Their sustainable siting remains still a research gap to be further analysed due to: (i) the need of the insular energy self-efficiency; the great distances from the mainland); (ii) the freeing of land; and (iii) the high unexploited offshore wind potential. The Analytical Hierarchy Process method and Geographic Information Systems incorporating 14 exclusion and 16 evaluation criteria are employed tο identify sustainable siting. This research integrates the opinions of a wide range of local stakeholders and experts, considering the insular specific characteristics of Crete. The high suitable marine areas are evaluated according to their energy capacity, assessed with seven different commercially available models. Most respondents estimate that the distance from environmental interest areas is the most critical criterion. The analysis proved that the final available areas for OWFs siting are 205.5 km2; 126.25 km2 of high suitability with a high potential of 620–900 MW. Since the maximum annual energy demand of the island amounted to 676 MW (2019) this demand could actually be overcovered by four main OWFs near the island. © 2021 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Islands; Multi-Criteria Decision-Making; Offshore Wind Farms; Social acceptance; Sustainable siting

Metodología: El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), estructurándose en tres etapas secuenciales: «(i) selection of the criteria... (ii) weighting of the evaluation criteria... and (iii) identification of the sustainable siting for the OWFs». Los autores justificaron el uso de esta metodología bajo la premisa de que permite «handling conflicting criteria, such as environmental, technical, economic and social criteria», logrando que «the decision-maker structures the decision problem into a hierarchy of different levels». Para determinar la importancia de cada factor, se utilizó la escala de Saaty para las comparaciones pareadas, citando que «the relative weights of the criteria were calculated based on the responses from the questionnaires of the different groups of stakeholders and experts». Finalmente, se aplicó un «weighted linear combination (WLC)» para superponer las capas y obtener el mapa de idoneidad. El estudio empleó un total de 30 variables, citando explícitamente el uso de «14 exclusion and 16 evaluation criteria» para garantizar un análisis exhaustivo y sostenible.

Authors: Barzehkar, M.; Parnell, K.E.; Mobarghaee Dinan, N.; Brodie, G.
Title: Decision support tools for wind and solar farm site selection in Isfahan Province, Iran
Abstract: Abstract: Optimizing the location of wind and photovoltaic solar power plants is a significant environmental management problem. The effectiveness of the site selection process for renewable energy systems (RES) could be strengthened by flexible spatial and environmental planning strategies using decision support systems (DSS) to critically identify the most productive, environmentally friendly and acceptable sites for the production of sustainable and reliable wind and solar energy. This study discusses hybrid DSS, using multi-criteria evaluation based on the analytical hierarchy process (AHP), a geographical information system (GIS), fuzzy logic, and a weighted linear combination (WLC) approach to determine optimal locations for renewable energy generation infrastructure. In the first stage, the most decisive factors for evaluating the site suitability were identified, based on experts’ opinions. Next, raster layers of ecological and socioeconomic sub-criteria were prepared GIS software. After incorporating the raster maps of each parameter, fuzzy membership functions were applied to normalize each raster layer between 0 and 1. The relative weights of different indicators were calculated using super decision software. Prioritizing vital elements were performed using AHP. In the final stage, the WLC approach was utilized to amalgamate layers in the GIS environment, which afforded the final site suitability maps. In Isfahan Province, Iran, 26% of the land area was found to be highly suitable for solar farms with 18% being highly suitable for wind farms. The results illustrate that using and comparing the results from combinations of computer-based DSS are more likely to result in better decisions than using individual DSS tools for the determination of the most suitable sites for RES location. Metodología: El proceso MCDM para la selección de sitios de energía renovable en la provincia de Isfahan se ejecutó mediante un sistema de apoyo a la decisión (DSS) que integró GIS, lógica difusa y el método AHP. El procedimiento se dividió en etapas claras: primero, «the most decisive factors for evaluating the site suitability were identified, based on experts’ opinions», seguido por la estandarización de criterios donde «fuzzy logic was applied to the initial datasets to standardise the varied criteria into a common scale of 0 to 255». Los autores justificaron el uso de la lógica difusa explicando que los métodos booleanos tradicionales son demasiado rígidos, mientras que el enfoque difuso permite que «the range of potential values of each criterion can be included in the analysis», proporcionando una evaluación más matizada de la idoneidad. Posteriormente, se utilizó el método AHP para la ponderación, justificando que este permite «reducing the complexity of the decision-making process by breaking the problem down into a hierarchical structure». Finalmente, se aplicó la técnica de Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) para generar los mapas finales, citando que «WLC is one of the most common methods for the combination of criteria in GIS-based MCDM». El estudio integró un total de 25 variables, distribuidas en «13 factors and 12 constraints» para el caso solar, y un conjunto similar para el eólico, asegurando que se cubrieran dimensiones ecológicas, técnicas y socioeconómicas.

Authors: Nagababu, G.; Puppala, H.; Pritam, K.; Kantipudi, M.P.
Title: Two-stage GIS-MCDM based algorithm to identify plausible regions at micro level to install wind farms: A case study of India
Abstract: Efficiency of the installed wind farms is location-specific. Various research works used the concepts of Geographical Information Systems (GIS) and Multi-criteria-techniques (MCDM) to identify suitable locations. However, research works addressing the micro-level site selection are limited. This study proposes a two-stage GIS-MCDM based algorithm that can identify the plausible regions for installing wind farms at the microscopic level. The developed tool, built on the philosophy of fuzzy Analytical Hierarchy Process (FAHP) and Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), differentiates the regions based on technological, economic, social, and environmental aspects. For demonstration purposes, India is chosen as a study region, implying national-level analysis as stage-1 and state-wise analysis as stage-2. Results suggest that the suitable area for wind farm development in India is approximately 1805131 km2, out of which about 650 km2 is considered as highly suitable, and the following best has 330321 km2. The most suitable locations are in the western and southern parts of India, mainly in Gujarat and Tamil Nadu states. These findings of stage-2 present the hierarchy of plausible regions within each state. The developed tool is the first of its kind, help the decision-maker to extend it for siting solar farms and other energy sources. © 2022
Author Keywords: FAHP; GIS; Site suitability analysis; TOPSIS; Wind energy

Metodología: El proceso MCDM se ejecutó a través de un algoritmo de dos etapas que integra GIS, Fuzzy AHP (FAHP) y TOPSIS, diseñado para «identify the plausible regions for installing wind farms at the microscopic level». En la primera etapa, se realizó un análisis macro mediante GIS para filtrar áreas mediante «exclusion criteria (binary constraints)» y se determinaron los pesos de los criterios usando FAHP, justificando que este método «extinguishes the ambiguity and uncertainty in the decision-makers' opinion» al utilizar números difusos en lugar de valores exactos. La segunda etapa consistió en un análisis micro donde se aplicó el método TOPSIS para clasificar los sitios potenciales, justificando su uso porque «it chooses the alternative that has the shortest distance from the positive ideal solution and the longest distance from the negative ideal solution», lo que permite una discriminación más fina entre sitios altamente calificados. El estudio empleó un total de 13 variables, citando explícitamente la evaluación de «ten assessment criteria» (como velocidad del viento, densidad de potencia y elevación) y la aplicación de «three exclusion criteria» para delimitar las zonas técnicamente viables.

Authors: Sotiropoulou, K.F.; Vavatsikos, A.P.; Botsaris, P.N.
Title: A hybrid AHP-PROMETHEE II onshore wind farms multicriteria suitability analysis using kNN and SVM regression models in northeastern Greece
Abstract: Wind energy presents a high growth potential in the EU as an emission reduction strategy and to achieve the climate neutrality goal by 2050. Wind farms suitability analysis is one of the primary goals in the spatial planning of wind energy developments. This research paper introduces a hybrid spatial multicriteria GIS-based framework that combines Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), PROMETHEE II and Machine Learning algorithms to determine and predict the most efficient onshore wind farm locations by generating suitability index mappings. The methodology allows to overcome PROMETHEE II limitations in raster driven suitability analysis, utilizing machine learning regression methods as the k Nearest Neighbor and Support Vector Machines to predict a graduating mapping of suitability index for wind farm locations in northeastern Greece. The best configured models presented a RMSE of 0.0344 and 0.0154 respectively, indicating a quite high predictive performance. Suitability results indicate that 56.10 % of the feasible locations in the Thrace area present a positive outranking character for the kNN model and 56.79 % for the SVR model. The proposed framework, enriched by PROMETHEE II capabilities, assists energy and spatial planners in identifying suitable sites for wind farm siting and enables rational decision making that enhances efficient wind energy investments. © 2023 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Geographic information systems (GIS); k Nearest Neighbor; Machine learning; PROMETHEE II outranking method; Support vector regression; Wind farm suitability analysis

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló mediante un marco espacial híbrido que integra GIS, AHP, PROMETHEE II y algoritmos de Machine Learning, estructurado para superar las limitaciones computacionales de los métodos de superación en formatos ráster. El procedimiento comenzó con la «identification of the criteria and the determination of their relative weights through the AHP method», justificando su uso para estructurar la jerarquía de decisión y obtener pesos consistentes. Posteriormente, se aplicó el método PROMETHEE II, justificando que, a diferencia de los modelos aditivos, este «performs an outranking analysis based on the differences between the alternative’s performances» y permite «the implementation of the preference functions for each criterion». Debido a la imposibilidad de aplicar PROMETHEE II a cada celda de un mapa ráster, se utilizó un «sample dataset» para entrenar modelos de k-Nearest Neighbor (kNN) y Support Vector Regression (SVR), citando que estos modelos permiten «predict and generate the suitability index mappings for the entire examined area». El estudio empleó un total de 14 variables, mencionando explícitamente el uso de «fourteen (14) criteria and constraints» que abarcan factores técnicos, económicos, ambientales y sociales.+

Authors: Ahmadi, S.H.R.; Noorollahi, Y.; Ghanbari, S.; Ebrahimi, M.; Mirshojaeian Hosseini, H.; Foroozani, A.; Hajinezhad, A.
Title: Hybrid fuzzy decision making approach for wind-powered pumped storage power plant site selection: A case study
Abstract: Combining wind power with pumped-storage systems is trustworthy for reducing the unreliability of wind energy, caused by the variable nature of the wind for contributing to the grid's peak shaving. Locating suitable sites for constructing both wind parks and pumped-storage systems for peak shaving is a delicate problem-solving. For solving such problems, a wide range of technical, financial, and environmental criteria should be taken into account. A two-step decision framework combining GIS, Analytic Network Process (ANP) and fuzzy VIKOR are proposed in this paper to solve the problem for locating the suitable sites for constructing a wind-powered pump storage plant. In the first step, a GIS database is developed to locate a handful potential sites within the study area. As there are different and even conflicting criteria involved with site selection problem. In the second step, we used an MCDM approach of fuzzy analytic network process (ANP) and fuzzy VIKOR to rank these potential sites. To establish an evaluation system, four major clusters of criteria are introduced (wind farm, water reservoir, water pump and power production) with more addition related sub-criteria. Comprehensive guidelines are formulated through a hierarchical procedure so a data-driven, multi-aspect decision framework could be presented with the goal of improving the benefits of integrated renewable energies. The results show that, the Gilane-Gharb dam is the most promising site within the study area. This site provides a net area of 13.2 km2 for constructing a wind farm with an approximate power capacity of 31 MW. The proposed procedure in this is a framework that are provide a theoretical reference for resolving any multicriteria decision-making problem by introducing the appropriate criteria. © 2020 Elsevier Ltd

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se diseñó mediante una metodología híbrida que combina GIS, Fuzzy ANP (FANP) y Fuzzy VIKOR, estructurada para abordar la selección de sitios para sistemas combinados de energía eólica y almacenamiento por bombeo. El procedimiento se ejecutó en etapas secuenciales: primero, se realizó un «pre-screening of potential sites using GIS» mediante el uso de «Boolean logic (AND operator)» para excluir áreas restringidas; posteriormente, se aplicó el método Fuzzy ANP para determinar los pesos de los criterios, justificando su elección en que, a diferencia del AHP, el ANP «can consider the inner and outer dependencies between criteria and alternatives», lo cual es crucial dado que «site selection is a complex problem and criteria are often related to each other». En la fase final, se utilizó el método Fuzzy VIKOR para clasificar las alternativas, fundamentando su uso en la capacidad de este método para proporcionar una «compromise solution... with a maximum 'group utility' and a minimum 'individual regret'». El estudio empleó un total de 14 variables, citando que la evaluación se basó en «14 important and effective criteria» clasificados en dimensiones técnicas, económicas y geográficas.

Authors: AlBraheem, L.; AlAwlaqi, L.
Title: Geospatial analysis of wind energy plant in Saudi Arabia using a GIS-AHP technique
Abstract: Wind energy is one of the most desirable sustainable energy alternatives because of its low operating, maintenance, and production costs, as well as its low environmental impact. Selecting suitable locations for wind power farms is a significant difficulty in wind energy development, requiring careful and coordinated evaluations of various factors. This research presents a wind farm site suitability analysis based on the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) and Geographic Information System (GIS) techniques, which consider technical, economic, environmental, and social considerations, reflecting five criteria. First, a Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) analysis using the AHP approach is applied to give suitable weights to the addressed criteria based on their relative importance. A GIS is utilized to conduct a geospatial analysis and integrate multiple criteria throughout the suggested index to create the final suitability map and indicate unsuitable locations. The final suitability map consists of four classes ranging from 1 to 4, whereas 4 represents the highly suitable sites in Saudi Arabia. The results show that the total land area of extremely suitable is 48,995 km 2, and the very suitable (class 3) is 197,800 km 2. The exact new suitable locations of class 4 with their total land area and coordinates are proposed in this study. The region that represents the largest total land area of suitability class 3 is Riyadh, which consists of 65,989.499 km 2. Whereas the largest land area of suitability class 4 is Al Mantiqah Al Sharqiyah consisting of 14,553.57 km 2. Finally, the currently announced wind projects in Saudi Arabia are mentioned and further support the findings of this research. © 2023 The Author(s)
