Metodología: El proceso MCDM para la evaluación de la energía eólica en Arabia Saudita se ejecutó integrando Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con la técnica Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), siguiendo un flujo de trabajo que comenzó con la «identification of decision and exclusion criteria based on technical, economic, environmental, and social considerations». Los autores justificaron el uso de AHP afirmando que es una herramienta robusta que permite a los tomadores de decisiones «solve complex problems with multiple criteria by prioritizing alternatives and making the best decision» a través de una estructura jerárquica. El procedimiento incluyó la creación de «pairwise comparison matrices» para calcular los pesos relativos, asegurando la validez del modelo mediante el «Consistency Ratio (CR)», justificando que un valor menor a 0.1 confirma que los juicios son consistentes y no aleatorios. Para la fase de integración, se utilizó la técnica de Weighted Overlay Analysis, donde «each decision criterion was reclassified to a common scale from 1 to 10» antes de ser multiplicado por su peso correspondiente. El estudio empleó un total de 10 variables, citando específicamente el uso de «five decision criteria» (como velocidad del viento, elevación y pendiente) y «five exclusion criteria» (incluyendo áreas urbanas, aeropuertos y sitios industriales) para delimitar las zonas óptimas.

Authors: Spyridonidou, S.; Sismani, G.; Loukogeorgaki, E.; Vagiona, D.G.; Ulanovsky, H.; Madar, D.
Title: Sustainable spatial energy planning of large-scale wind and pv farms in israel: A collaborative and participatory planning approach
Abstract: In this work, an innovative sustainable spatial energy planning framework is developed on national scale for identifying and prioritizing appropriate, technically and economically feasible, environmentally sustainable as well as socially acceptable sites for the siting of large-scale onshore Wind Farms (WFs) and Photovoltaic Farms (PVFs) in Israel. The proposed holistic framework consists of distinctive steps allocated in two successive modules (the Planning and the Field Investigation module), and it covers all relevant dimensions of a sustainable siting analysis (economic, social, and environmental). It advances a collaborative and participatory planning approach by combining spatial planning tools (Geographic Information Systems (GIS)) and multi-criteria decision-making methods (e.g., Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP)) with versatile participatory planning techniques in order to consider the opinion of three different participatory groups (public, experts, and renewable energy planners) within the site-selection processes. Moreover, it facilitates verification of GIS results by conducting appropriate field observations. Sites of high suitability, accepted by all participatory groups and field verified, form the final outcome of the proposed framework. The results illustrate the existence of high suitable sites for large-scale WFs’ and PVFs’ siting and, thus, the potential deployment of such projects towards the fulfillment of the Israeli energy targets in the near future. © 2021 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló bajo un enfoque colaborativo y participativo que integró GIS con el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), estructurándose en cuatro fases principales: «(i) selection of the study area, (ii) identification of the exclusion and the evaluation criteria, (iii) determination of the criteria’s weights, and (iv) combination of the criteria». Los autores justificaron esta metodología destacando la importancia de un «participatory planning approach» para abordar conflictos de uso de suelo, permitiendo que los pesos de los criterios fueran determinados mediante el consenso de expertos y partes interesadas. El procedimiento incluyó la aplicación de «exclusion criteria» para delimitar las áreas factibles y el uso de comparaciones pareadas en AHP para priorizar los factores de evaluación. Finalmente, se utilizó la técnica de Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) en un entorno GIS para superponer las capas y clasificar la idoneidad del terreno. El estudio empleó un total de 26 variables, desglosadas en «14 exclusion criteria and 12 evaluation criteria» (como velocidad del viento, radiación solar, pendiente y proximidad a la red eléctrica), asegurando una planificación espacial sostenible y socialmente aceptable.

Authors: Yousefi, H.; Ghanbari Motlagh, S.G.; Montazeri, M.
Title: Multi-Criteria Decision-Making System for Wind Farm Site-Selection Using Geographic Information System (GIS): Case Study of Semnan Province, Iran
Abstract: Selecting the best place for constructing a renewable power plant is a vital issue that can be considered a site-selection problem. Various factors are involved in selecting the best location for a renewable power plant. Therefore, it categorizes as a multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) problem. In this study, the site selection of a wind power plant is investigated in a central province of Iran, Semnan. The main criteria for classifying various parts of the province were selected and pairwise compared using experts’ opinions in this field. Furthermore, multiple restrictions were applied according to local and constitutional rules and regulations. The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) was used to weigh the criteria, and according to obtained weights, wind speed, and slope were the essential criteria. Moreover, a geographic information system (GIS) is used to apply the weighted criteria and restrictions. The province’s area is classified into nine classes according to the results. Based on the restrictions, 36.2% of the total area was unsuitable, mainly located in the north part of the province. Furthermore, 2.68% (2618 km2) and 4.98% (4857 km2) of the total area are the ninth and eightieth classes, respectively, which are the best locations for constructing a wind farm. The results show that, although the wind speed and slope are the most essential criteria, the distance from power facilities and communication routes has an extreme impact on the initial costs and final results. The results of this study are reliable and can help to develop the wind farm industry in the central part of Iran. © 2022 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se ejecutó mediante la integración de Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) y el método Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), estructurándose en una metodología de dos etapas para identificar los sitios óptimos en la provincia de Semnan. El procedimiento comenzó con la «selección de los criterios y la comparación por pares utilizando las opiniones de expertos en el campo» , seguido por la «aplicación de múltiples restricciones de acuerdo con las leyes y regulaciones locales y constitucionales». Los autores justificaron el uso de AHP por ser uno de los métodos más «esenciales y ampliamente utilizados en MCDM» para resolver problemas de selección de sitios donde intervienen factores contradictorios. Para la fase de análisis espacial, se empleó GIS con el fin de «aplicar los criterios ponderados y las restricciones» , lo que permitió clasificar el área de la provincia en nueve categorías de idoneidad. En total, el modelo consideró 14 variables principales, divididas en 7 criterios de evaluación (como la velocidad del viento, la pendiente y la distancia a infraestructuras eléctricas) y 7 tipos de restricciones (incluyendo áreas urbanas, zonas protegidas ambientalmente y rutas de comunicación). Los resultados determinaron que, aunque la velocidad del viento y la pendiente son los criterios con mayor peso relativo, la «distancia a las instalaciones eléctricas y las rutas de comunicación tiene un impacto extremo en los costos iniciales».

Authors: Karipoğlu, F.; Genç, M.S.; Koca, K.
Title: Determination of the most appropriate site selection of wind power plants based geographic information system and multi-criteria decision-making approach in develi, Turkey
Abstract: Wind power has major benefits including providing for an increasing energy demand while tackling climate change problems. Detailed planning processes such as technical, social, environmental, various agents, and political concerns are essential for the development of wind energy projects. The objective of the present study is to develop a visualization that combines Geographic Information System (GIS) and Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) and implementation for Kayseri, Develi in Turkey as a case study. For the analyzes, CORINE CLC 2000 and other data sources were employed for data acquisition to unlock fragmented and hidden onshore data resources and to facilitate investment in sustainable coastal and inland activities. Several factors were determined in the wind power plant installations such as wind potential, roads, water sources, and these factors were analyzed based on their buffer zones. After detailed analyses, sites near the Havadan (7.87 MW) and Kulpak (9.22 MW) villages were found to be the most suitable locations for the installation of a potential onshore wind farm. The method suggested in this study can be used to analyze the suitability of any region at the regional level for onshore wind power plant and the results of the study can be used to develop based on public perception, renewable energy policies, energy political rules. © 2021, Aalborg University press. All rights reserved.

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para la determinación del sitio más apropiado en Develi, Turquía, se llevó a cabo siguiendo una metodología estructurada en cuatro etapas principales: «(i) defining wind power plants criteria, (ii) data collection and processing... (iii) determine restrictions which are important for suitable site selection and defining buffer zones of restrictions, (iv) in the last stage to indicate suitable sites and locations for wind power installation». Los autores justificaron el uso de técnicas MCDM argumentando que son herramientas que permiten realizar la mejor elección basándose en criterios concurrentes, especialmente cuando las «economic considerations are complemented with environmental and social considerations». Una característica particular de este estudio fue que «all weights for criteria were accepted equal to 1», decisión fundamentada en que el objetivo principal era la visualización de áreas factibles mediante el análisis espacial. Para la ejecución del modelo, se definieron un total de 13 variables, divididas en «three main criteria (technical, environmental, and social) and 10 sub-criteria», las cuales fueron procesadas mediante herramientas GIS para identificar las localizaciones óptimas.

Authors: Li, M.; Xu, Y.; Guo, J.; Li, Y.; Li, W.
Title: Application of a GIS-based fuzzy multi-criteria evaluation approach for wind farm site selection in China
Abstract: The development and utilization of wind energy has alleviated the problems of energy shortage and environmental pollution; however, it caused many negative impacts due to suboptimal site selections. This study proposes an innovative method integrating Geographic Information System (GIS), fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (FAHP), and fuzzy VIšekriterijumsko KOmpromisno Rangiranje (VIKOR) for site selection of wind farms in the Wafangdian region, China. The uncertainties caused by subjective judgments of the stakeholders were tackled by the FAHP method firstly, where weight values of six criteria were identified. Next, the fuzzy VIKOR method and GIS tool were used to generate the Qi value of each location for ranking their appropriate degrees for wind energy development. The results demonstrated that the middle and upper parts of the studied area are suitable for the exploitation of wind energy, while the central and eastern areas are unfavorable. The influences exerted by various weight combinations and climate change on a site suitability assessment were examined. The resulting comparison with existing wind farms reflected the practicability and reliability of the proposed method; the estimation of climate change impacts on site selection provided the suggestion and support of a long-term plan for wind power development, and even the energy structure adjustment scheme adapted to climate change. © 2020 by the authors. Metodología: El proceso MCDM para la selección de sitios de energía eólica en la región de Wafangdian se llevó a cabo mediante una metodología híbrida que integra GIS, Fuzzy AHP (FAHP) y Fuzzy VIKOR. El procedimiento se estructuró en tres fases: primero, la identificación de criterios y la recopilación de datos espaciales; segundo, el cálculo de pesos mediante FAHP para manejar la incertidumbre; y tercero, la clasificación de alternativas utilizando Fuzzy VIKOR. Los autores justificaron el uso de la lógica difusa (Fuzzy) argumentando que permite «tackle the uncertainties caused by subjective judgments of the stakeholders» y que el método VIKOR es ideal para este problema ya que busca una «compromise solution... that is the closest to the ideal solution». Para la ejecución del modelo, se definieron un total de 10 variables, divididas en 6 criterios de evaluación (como velocidad del viento, elevación y distancia a la red eléctrica) y 4 criterios de restricción (incluyendo áreas residenciales, cuerpos de agua y zonas forestales), los cuales fueron procesados en un entorno GIS para generar los mapas de idoneidad final.

Authors: Demir, G.; Riaz, M.; Deveci, M.
Title: Wind farm site selection using geographic information system and fuzzy decision making model
Abstract: As the demand for renewable energy sources increases, finding the right places to install wind turbines becomes more and more important. The goal of this research is to create and implement a technique that uses geographic information system (GIS) technology to discover appropriate wind farm locations utilizing multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) approaches. The complexity of this decision-making process, which includes multiple criteria and uncertainty, requires the use of advanced techniques. Fuzzy MCDM methods provide a framework for evaluating and prioritizing potential wind farm sites, taking into account subjective judgments and linguistic terms. In this article, Fuzzy Stepwise Weight Evaluation Ratio Analysis (F-SWARA) is preferred for prioritizing and ranking the criteria in the wind farm installation, while Fuzzy Measurement Alternatives and Ranking by Compromise Solution (F-MARCOS) are used to determine the most suitable location for the wind farm. A database of alternatives and criteria was created using GIS, which was converted into a fuzzy decision matrix via triangular fuzzy numbers. In order to make this evaluation, Sivas province, located in the middle of Turkey, was chosen as the study area. Results obtained show that 36,5% of the whole study area is very suitable for wind farm, and Gürün and Kangal districts are suitable for wind farm. According to the result of F-SWARA method used to evaluate the criteria, wind speed is the most important criteria with a weight of 0,45039. According to the F-MARCOS method used for wind farm site selection, Ulaş district was determined the most suitable location. Furthermore, a sensitivity analysis was performed to test the robustness of the proposed methodology and the results revealed that the proposed integrated MCDM framework is feasible. © 2024 The Author(s)

Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con un enfoque de decisión difuso que combina los métodos Fuzzy SWARA (Simplified Wisdom Assessment and Rating Analysis) y Fuzzy MARCOS (Measurement of Alternatives and Ranking according to COmpromise Solution). El procedimiento se estructuró en fases críticas: primero, se realizó una «screening stage using GIS» para eliminar áreas con restricciones legales o técnicas; posteriormente, se aplicó Fuzzy SWARA para determinar la importancia de los criterios, justificando que este método permite «the participation of multiple experts in the weight calculation process» de forma eficiente. En la fase final, se utilizó el método Fuzzy MARCOS para clasificar los sitios potenciales, fundamentando su elección en que «it considers the ideal and anti-ideal solutions at the beginning of the formation of the initial decision matrix», lo que reduce la incertidumbre en el ranking final. El estudio integró un total de 14 variables, desglosadas en «six main criteria» (económicos, técnicos, ambientales, sociales, etc.) y un conjunto de criterios de exclusión procesados mediante lógica booleana en el GIS.

Authors: Şahin, G.; Koc, A.; Van Sark, W.G.J.H.M.
Title: Multi-criteria decision making for solar power - Wind power plant site selection using a GIS-intuitionistic fuzzy-based approach with an application in the Netherlands
Abstract: The development of a country cannot be realized only through the amount of energy it produces and its industrialization. In a country where its people are left homeless and poor, and its cultural and natural riches are destroyed, the electricity produced is not a measure of development on its own. Development and progress must be considered from a holistic perspective that includes the country's geographical structure, all its living creatures, culture, urban and social structure as a whole. In this respect, the transition to renewable energy is imperative. One of the most widely used renewable energies in the Netherlands is solar and wind energy. For these power plants, site selection is an important factor in reducing the installation cost of the wind and solar power plant and achieving maximum efficiency during operation. This paves the way for the study of a site selection problem. In this study, we first investigate possible locations for solar-wind power plant installation for 12 regions of the Netherlands, namely Noord Holland, Gelderland, Friesland, North Brabant, Drenthe, Groningen, Zeeland (Middelburg), Utrecht, Zuid Holland, Limburg, Over Ijssel and Flevoland, using GIS as a mapping method, and then apply a Intuitionistic fuzzy-based approach to the problem to obtain the optimal locations for both solar and wind energy. Furthermore, the results of two methods (GIS and Intuitionistic fuzzy-based approach) are compared to obtain more accurate results. The results show that 35317.2 km2 is suitable for solar power plant and 34844.5 km2 is suitable for wind turbine, but only 34875.8 km2 is suitable for solar-wind power plan installation. © 2024 The Authors
Author Keywords: Ecological criteria; Geographical information systems (GIS); Intuitionistic fuzzy; Multi-criteria decision-making; Solar power plant siting map; Solar-wind optimal siting map; Wind power plant siting map
Index Keywords: Behavioral research; Decision making; Developing countries; Fuzzy sets; Geographic information systems; Maps; Site selection; Solar energy; Wind power; Ecological criteria; Geographical information; Geographical information system; Intuitionistic fuzzy; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Solar power plant siting map; Solar-wind optimal siting map; Solar/wind; Wind power plant siting map; Wind power plants; Solar power plants
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se basó en un enfoque espacial que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con una metodología de lógica difusa intuicionista (Intuitionistic Fuzzy), específicamente mediante el método IF-AHP. El procedimiento se estructuró para identificar sitios óptimos tanto para plantas solares como eólicas de manera independiente y combinada, justificando el uso de la lógica difusa intuicionista porque permite manejar la incertidumbre y la subjetividad de los expertos de manera más robusta que los métodos difusos tradicionales al incorporar grados de «membership, non-membership, and hesitation». Los autores justificaron este marco holístico señalando que el desarrollo de un país no puede medirse únicamente por la cantidad de energía producida, sino que debe considerar la «geographical structure, all its living creatures, culture, urban and social structure as a whole». El estudio consideró un total de 15 variables de evaluación (desglosadas en criterios técnicos, sociales, ecológicos y económicos), citando explícitamente factores como el recurso energético (viento/sol), la pendiente del terreno y la proximidad a infraestructuras, además de una fuerte prioridad por los criterios ecológicos como las áreas Natura 2000 y reservas naturales.

Authors: Yildiz, S.S.
Title: Spatial multi-criteria decision making approach for wind farm site selection: A case study in Balıkesir, Turkey
Abstract: This study presents a spatial multi-criteria decision making (SMCDM) approach based on geographic information system (GIS) for determining the sites that have potential for wind farm installation in Balıkesir, Turkey. Topographical, structural, climatic, and environmental criteria consisting of wind speed, land cover type, slope of terrain, and distance from substations, road networks, settlements, fault lines, electricity grid, and seaports are considered in the approach. Weights, derived from evaluation involving the use of the analytical hierarchy process (AHP) questionnaire, and scored criteria maps, are used in creation of a wind farm suitability map. The results of the study demonstrate that 2.34 % of the total area under consideration is in the most suitable class, while 9.34 % is in the next most suitable class. The study introduces the seaport criterion for the first time in onshore wind turbine site selection. The findings show that considering the seaport criterion provides a more realistic approach of feasible wind turbine site selection. This is clearly seen in the 13 % decrease in the most suitable area. When the wind farm suitability map is evaluated on a district scale, Bandırma takes first place compared to other districts. A sensitivity analysis performed with equal criteria weights indicates that the results are generally reliable. © 2023 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: AHP; Carbon footprint; GIS; Site selection; SMCDM; Wind farm
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric substations; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Sensitivity analysis; Site selection; Wind; Wind power; Wind turbines; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Case-studies; Farm sites; Land-cover types; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Spatial multi-criteria decision making; Wind farm; Wind speed; Carbon footprint
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), estructurándose en una metodología espacial de toma de decisiones (SMCDM) que prioriza la precisión logística y técnica. El procedimiento comenzó con la «determination of the appropriate criteria» mediante una revisión detallada de estudios previos y características locales , seguido por la «evaluation involving the use of the analytical hierarchy process (AHP) questionnaire» para derivar los pesos de importancia relativa. Los autores justificaron el uso de este enfoque destacando que permite analizar «varying parameters associated with the economic efficiency and land use characteristics of the power plants in relation to the natural environment and to social impact». Una innovación clave fue la inclusión de la «distance from seaports as a criterion», justificando que proporciona un «more realistic approach» al reducir los costos de transporte y la huella de carbono asociada. El modelo empleó un total de 9 variables de evaluación (incluyendo velocidad del viento, tipo de cobertura del suelo, pendiente del terreno y distancias a subestaciones, redes de carreteras, asentamientos, líneas de falla, red eléctrica y puertos marítimos) , las cuales fueron procesadas mediante funciones GIS de «Near, point to polygon and reclassify» para generar el mapa final de idoneidad.

Authors: Bahaj, A.S.; Mahdy, M.; Alghamdi, A.S.; Richards, D.J.
Title: New approach to determine the Importance Index for developing offshore wind energy potential sites: Supported by UK and Arabian Peninsula case studies
Abstract: A multi-criteria decision-making analysis linked to a Geographical Information System was developed to solve the spatial siting for offshore wind farms taking into account appropriate conflicting factors/constraints. A new approach is presented to solve the conflicting factors by determining the Importance Index (I) for offshore wind farms. This is based on a newly defined parameter Representative Cost Ratio (RCR) facilitating the comparison process. The method compares factor pairs and overcomes the issue where the evaluation of “alternatives” and “criteria”, conducted by a number of experts result in reduced accuracy, coherence, and making the process time-consuming. The approach is tested through two case studies (i) UK deployed projects and (ii) determining the offshore wind energy potential around the Arabian Peninsula at scale. The presented method circumvents the literature-highlighted shortcomings with the advantage of considering all restrictions/constraints together at the start of the analysis, arriving at a signally combined Boolean Mask. RCR compares factor pairs to interpret the relationship between Importance Index scale (1–9) and its descriptors. Results from both case studies provided excellent outcomes, confirming the robustness of the RCR approach and its global applicability in addressing the spatial planning of offshore wind farms. © 2020 The Authors
Author Keywords: GIS/MCDA; Offshore wind; Offshore wind constraints; Representative cost ratio; Suitability maps; UK and Arabian Peninsula offshore wind areas
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric utilities; Offshore oil well production; Cost ratio; Importance index; Multi criteria decision making; New approaches; Off-shore wind energy; Offshore winds; Process time; Spatial planning; Offshore wind farms; accuracy assessment; cost analysis; GIS; index method; spatial planning; wind farm; wind power; Arabian Peninsula; United Kingdom
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló vinculando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) con un análisis de toma de decisiones multicriterio para resolver el emplazamiento espacial de parques eólicos marinos, introduciendo un nuevo enfoque basado en el parámetro Representative Cost Ratio (RCR), ademas de usar AHP antes de aplicar el RCR1. Este método se utiliza para determinar el Importance Index ($I$), fundamentando su aplicación en la capacidad de resolver factores en conflicto y superar las limitaciones de la evaluación tradicional por expertos, que a menudo resulta en una «reduced accuracy, coherence, and making the process time-consuming»2. Los autores justificaron la robustez del modelo señalando que este permite «considering all restrictions/constraints together at the start of the analysis», generando una «signally combined Boolean Mask» para filtrar las áreas no aptas3. El procedimiento se estructuró comparando pares de factores mediante RCR para interpretar la relación entre la escala de importancia y sus descriptores, lo que facilita un proceso de comparación más objetivo y preciso4. El estudio evaluó la idoneidad mediante el uso de diversas variables técnicas y de restricción; se mencionaron factores de evaluación como la velocidad del viento, la profundidad del agua, la distancia a la costa y la distancia a la red eléctrica5. En cuanto a las restricciones, el modelo integró una amplia gama de capas espaciales, incluyendo límites marinos, áreas de ejercicios militares, zonas de pesca, naufragios protegidos y parques eólicos existentes6. Esta metodología fue validada a través de dos casos de estudio —proyectos desplegados en el Reino Unido y el potencial eólico en la Península Arábiga—, demostrando su eficacia para abordar la complejidad de la planificación espacial marina a gran escala7

Authors: Nasery, S.; Küçük Matcı, D.K.; Avdan, U.
Title: GIS-based wind farm suitability assessment using fuzzy AHP multi-criteria approach: the case of Herat, Afghanistan
Abstract: The negative impacts of fossil fuel and nuclear energy for generating electricity on the environment have created a need to find and use clean, renewable, and sustainable energy resources such as wind, solar, and geothermal resources. This study aims to locate the best suitable locations for siting wind farms in Herat province/Afghanistan with a geographic information system (GIS)–based multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) method. The most prominent factors affecting wind farm location considered in this study are annual average wind speed, slope, distance from residential areas, distance from power transmission lines, distance to roads, and land cover/land use (LULC). Equal buffer zones are created using the policies and regulations of different countries to prevent security disruptions around roads, residential areas, and existing power transmission lines. The fuzzy analytical hierarchy process (fuzzy AHP) is used to derive weights for each criterion, considering their relative importance. The final suitability map is generated using a weighted overlay tool in the ArcGIS Pro environment. The results show that 2.9% of the study area is very highly and 12.4% is highly suitable for siting wind farms. Four sites with areas of 537.10 km2, 188.81 km2, 166.97 km2, and 16.40 km2 located in the northeast, north, and northwest of Herat city, are concluded to be the most suitable areas for constructing wind farms. These areas are both near the town and at a close distance to power transmission lines. © 2021, Saudi Society for Geosciences.
Author Keywords: fuzzy AHP; Geographic information system; Herat/Afghanistan; Wind farm suitability
Index Keywords: alternative energy; analytical hierarchy process; ArcGIS; assessment method; detection method; energy policy; fossil fuel; general policy and legislation; GIS; multicriteria analysis; wind farm; Afghanistan; Herat
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se llevó a cabo integrando Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método Fuzzy AHP (FAHP), estructurándose para localizar los sitios más adecuados para parques eólicos en la provincia de Herat. El procedimiento se dividió en fases clave: primero, la identificación de los factores decisivos; segundo, la creación de zonas de amortiguamiento o «equal buffer zones» basadas en regulaciones internacionales para garantizar la seguridad en carreteras, áreas residenciales y líneas de transmisión; y tercero, la derivación de pesos mediante FAHP. Los autores justificaron el uso de la lógica difusa (Fuzzy) argumentando que permite «meet the requirements for uncertain and indeterminate pairwise comparisons», ya que en problemas de esta complejidad a menudo resulta imposible realizar juicios precisos mediante comparaciones binarias tradicionales. Además, señalaron que el FAHP ofrece resultados más robustos que el AHP clásico al reflejar mejor el estilo de pensamiento humano y manejar la vaguedad inherente a las opiniones de los expertos. Finalmente, se empleó la herramienta Weighted Overlay para integrar las capas espaciales y generar el mapa de idoneidad final. El estudio consideró un total de 6 variables principales de evaluación, que incluyen la velocidad media anual del viento, la pendiente del terreno, el uso de suelo (LULC) y las distancias a áreas residenciales, carreteras y líneas de transmisión eléctrica.

Authors: Asadi, M.; Pourhossein, K.
Title: Wind farm site selection considering turbulence intensity
Abstract: Selecting a suitable site is a critical step in wind energy projects. In this regard, using appropriate decision criteria plays a significant role. Turbulence intensity is one of the main characteristics of airflow that affects the power production and lifespan of wind turbines. Therefore, it is considered as a wind site selection criterion in this paper. The present paper formulates the effect of turbulence on both power production and lifespan of wind turbines via an equivalent wind power density (WPD<inf>eq</inf>) function, to use it in the wind site selection process. To implement the proposed methodology, 88 wind sites in Iran have been studied. The results reveal the significant effect of turbulence on the site selection process as some sites have changed their rank up to 8 grades considering turbulence intensity in the siting process. To consider the turbulence intensity as an independent criterion to use it in multi-criteria decision-making methods, its relative importance (weight) have to be calculated. Sensitivity analysis shows that, on average, the relative importance of turbulence intensity over wind power density can be approximated as 1/5. Researchers can quickly consider turbulence intensity in siting process incorporating its weight in the pairwise comparison matrix of multi-criteria decision–making. © 2021
Author Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process (AHP); Geographical information system (GIS); Sensitivity analysis; Turbulence intensity; Wind farm siting; Wind power density (WPD)
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Decision making; Electric power system interconnection; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Sensitivity analysis; Site selection; Turbulence; Wind turbines; Farm sites; Geographical information system; Life span; Power production; Turbulence intensity; Wind farm; Wind farm siting; Wind power density; Wind power; airflow; detection method; performance assessment; sensitivity analysis; site selection; turbulence; wind farm; wind power; Iran
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se centró en la evaluación de sitios eólicos integrando un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS) y el método Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), con un enfoque innovador que incorpora la intensidad de la turbulencia1111. El procedimiento comenzó con la «selección de un conjunto adecuado de criterios» basados en la opinión de expertos y el uso de GIS para generar «capas ráster relacionadas con los criterios de decisión» y extraer valores para las estaciones evaluadas2222. Los autores justificaron la inclusión de la intensidad de la turbulencia como un criterio clave, argumentando que es una de las características principales del flujo de aire que «afecta la producción de energía y la vida útil de las turbinas eólicas»33333. Para el pesaje de los factores, se utilizó el método AHP, fundamentando su uso en que permite «tomar la mejor decisión cuando se enfrentan múltiples problemas y criterios de decisión» mediante una matriz de comparación por pares validada por una «relación de consistencia (CR)» menor a 0.1444444444. El estudio empleó un total de 7 variables, destacando que la integración de la turbulencia permitió formular una función de «densidad de potencia eólica equivalente ($WPD_{eq}$)» para una evaluación más precisa y realista del recurso555555555.

Authors: Gil-Garcia, I.C.; Ramos-Escudero, A.; Molina-García, Á.; Fernández – Guillamón, A.
Title: GIS-based MCDM dual optimization approach for territorial-scale offshore wind power plants
Abstract: Despite the relevant potential of offshore resource to substantially mitigate the effects of climate change through the generation of renewable electricity, the full exploitation of such wind resource currently remains incomplete. To promote offshore wind energy projects, it is crucial to engage in comprehensive planning processes that encompass technical, social, environmental, stakeholder, and political considerations. Within this framework, this paper presents a combined approach based on Geographical Information Systems (GIS) and Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) methodology to optimize the selection of offshore wind farm locations. The proposed methodology includes annual net electricity production as a critical factor in the decision-making process, which has been previously optimized with regard to the wake effect. Subsequently, the determination of potential locations involves key criteria such as technical feasibility, environmental impact, economic viability, and power generation potential. This MCDM-GIS approach was applied and evaluated in a case study along the Spanish coastline, considering 92 initial alternatives. The results indicate that the offshore wind energy targets established for Spain in 2030 and 2050 should be reevaluated, as the optimal utilization of the available area represents only 16% of the total potential area. Based on the aforementioned criteria, a suitability map was generated by integrating all relevant map layers, including their respective buffer zones. © 2023
Author Keywords: GIS; MCDM; Net electricity generation; Offshore wind plant; Optimal location
Index Keywords: Climate change; Decision making; Energy policy; Environmental impact; Geographic information systems; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Power generation; Electricity-generation; Geographical information; Geographical information system; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Net electricity generation; Offshore wind plant; Offshore winds; Optimal locations; Wind plant; Location
Metodología: El proceso MCDM para este estudio se desarrolló mediante un enfoque de optimización dual que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con una metodología híbrida que combina el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) y el método VIKOR. El procedimiento se distinguió por la inclusión de la Producción Neta Anual de Electricidad (ANEP), optimizada considerando el efecto estela (wake effect), como un criterio de decisión fundamental. Los autores justificaron el uso de AHP para calcular los pesos de importancia, destacando su capacidad para estructurar problemas complejos, y emplearon VIKOR para la clasificación de alternativas, fundamentando su elección en la aptitud de este método para determinar una «solución de compromiso» que equilibra la utilidad del grupo y el pesar individual. Para la ejecución del modelo, se evaluaron un total de 92 ubicaciones iniciales a lo largo de las costas españolas, utilizando 8 criterios de selección clave: velocidad del viento, profundidad del agua, altura significativa de las olas, distancia a la costa, distancia a la red eléctrica, impacto ambiental, producción neta de electricidad y un índice de viabilidad económica. El estudio justifica este marco argumentando que permite una «planificación integral» que supera las limitaciones de los análisis puramente técnicos al incorporar factores económicos y ambientales de manera simultánea.

Authors: Li, Z.; Tian, G.; El-Shafay, A.S.
Title: Statistical-analytical study on world development trend in offshore wind energy production capacity focusing on Great Britain with the aim of MCDA based offshore wind farm siting
Abstract: Present study is a statistical-analytical analysis about the offshore wind energy plants development. As a multi objective or multi step study, in the first step the last 20 year development trend of top 10 pioneering countries in offshore wind energy production capacity studied to classify the countries based on their activity level in development path. Based on results the Great Britain (GB) can be nominated as the most active country from the offshore wind energy production capacity development point of view. But in order to access a real rank of countries based on offshore wind energy per capita, a more reliable index defined with dividing the offshore wind energy production capacity by population of countries. Based on introduced index the condition of GB with per capita of 123.5 Watt/person is not good enough compared to countries like Denmark, Belgium, Sweden and Netherlands with lower production capacity but higher per capita of 293.6, 194.4, 184.7 and 145.9 Watt/person respectively. Considering the island type and geographical position of GB and also having the highest development rate of offshore wind energy production capacity between top 10 countries in last 20 years, as the second step an analytical study conducted to find the best geographical coordinates for new offshore wind energy plant installation and enhancing production capacity. Blending multi criteria decision analysis (MCDA) with GIS software that is called special multi criteria decision analysis (SMCDA) is the method that is applied for offshore wind farm siting. Wind energy density, wind speed, water depth and distance from shore are the four main selected criteria for offshore wind farm siting. Next to for main criteria, the locations of installed or under construction offshore wind farms and also efficiency of offshore wind farms in various months and seasons are the other criteria for selecting suitable siting. In addition to above mentioned criteria, the suitability analysis for offshore wind farm siting applied in four different altitudes and also in six various areas around the GB. Based on results the northern (area#1), north western (some parts of area#6), north eastern (some parts of area#2) and some parts of western waters (area#5) are the best areas for installing offshore wind farms based on suitability map results in all four altitudes. © 2022
Author Keywords: GIS; Great britain; MCDA; Offshore wind energy; Offshore wind farm; Site selection
Index Keywords: Blending; Decision making; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Offshore oil well production; Site selection; Wind; Analytical analysis; Analytical studies; Development trends; Great Britain; Multi-criteria decision analysis; Off-shore wind energy; Per capita; Plant development; Production capacity; Wind energy production; Offshore wind farms
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "Statistical-analytical study on world development trend in offshore wind energy production capacity focusing on Great Britain with the aim of MCDA based offshore wind farm siting", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en las aguas territoriales de Gran Bretaña. El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso integrando un análisis estadístico de tendencias globales con un modelo de Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) y el método Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP). La metodología comenzó con una fase de exclusión para delimitar el área de estudio factible, eliminando zonas restringidas por "shipping density, pipelines and cables, and protected areas (like SACs, SPAs, and MCZs)". Posteriormente, se aplicó el método AHP para determinar los pesos de importancia de los criterios de evaluación, basándose en comparaciones por pares para cuantificar la relevancia relativa de cada factor, justificando esta elección al señalar que "MCDA is a systematic method that allows decision-makers to evaluate complex problems involving multiple, often conflicting, objectives and criteria". El paso final consistió en la generación de un índice de idoneidad mediante una superposición ponderada para identificar los sitios con mayor potencial técnico y económico. En total, se utilizaron 9 variables clave en el modelo de evaluación: "wind speed" (el criterio más pesado con un 38.6%), "water depth" (21.9%), "distance from shore" (12.3%), "distance from ports" (8.7%), "distance from the power grid" (6.1%), "shipping density" (4.1%), "seabed type" (3.2%), "proximity to existing pipelines and cables" (2.8%) y "proximity to protected areas" (2.3%).

Authors: Klok, C.W.; Kirkels, A.F.; Alkemade, F.
Title: Impacts, procedural processes, and local context: Rethinking the social acceptance of wind energy projects in the Netherlands
Abstract: A lack of social acceptance obstructs the diffusion of wind energy and jeopardises the fight against global warming and climate change. Academic literature has identified the leading causes of social acceptance, but falls short in providing workable methodologies to consider this in the planning and decision-making process. This paper attends to this issue by providing a novel holistic conceptualisation of community acceptance as an interplay between project impacts, the procedural process, and local context. This conceptualisation can help project developers, decision-makers and researchers to better understand the different dimensions of acceptance and identify potential blind spots in their current methods and approaches. Based on this conceptualisation, we construct and apply a planning tool based on geographical information systems and multi-criteria decision analysis. Using this method on a national scale, we estimate wind energy potential in the Netherlands from a social acceptance point of view. We find that a third of the theoretically available land can be exploited, and that roughly 26.5GW worth of wind energy can be installed. The case demonstrates that the approach can effectively raise acceptance by reducing impacts, facilitating participation, and improving transparency in the siting process. © 2023 The Authors
Author Keywords: Community acceptance; GIS; MCDA; NIMBY; Site selection; Societal acceptance
Metodología: El estudio realizado por Klok et al. (2023) en los Países Bajos aborda la selección de sitios para energía eólica terrestre (onshore) mediante un enfoque de toma de decisiones multicriterio (MCDM) que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP). El proceso se estructuró en tres pasos fundamentales: primero, se mapeó la idoneidad de cada ubicación para cada criterio individual utilizando puntuaciones de idoneidad; segundo, se emplearon las opiniones de expertos para evaluar la importancia relativa de cada criterio mediante el método AHP; y finalmente, estas puntuaciones se integraron en un mapa de idoneidad único, excluyendo los sitios legalmente prohibidos. Los autores justifican este procedimiento señalando que el modelado permite hacer explícitos conceptos y procesos complejos, transformando el problema en algo "tangible and debatable", lo que, sumado a su transparencia, lo hace intrínsecamente útil para procesos participativos. En cuanto a la configuración del modelo, se utilizaron 8 variables (o criterios) de decisión que abarcan aspectos económicos (proximidad a carreteras y líneas eléctricas), sociales (parpadeo de sombras, impacto visual y molestias por ruido), ecológicos (uso del suelo y tierras naturales protegidas) y técnicos (densidad de potencia eólica).

Authors: Mokarram, M.; Pourghasemi, H.R.; Mokarram, M.J.
Title: A multi-criteria GIS-based model for wind farm site selection with the least impact on environmental pollution using the OWA-ANP method
Abstract: Wind energy is considered one of the most efficient and cost-effective ways to generate electricity, since it has a low environmental impact. So, it is essential to identify the best places to build wind farms that have the lowest impact on human health and the highest performance. In order to determine the appropriate locations for the construction of wind power plants, in the study first, the interpolation maps of the most important parameters for the construction of wind power plants were created. Then, using the analytic network process (ANP) method due to higher accuracy than other weighting methods (the two-by-two comparison of external and internal data), the weight of each criterion was determined by establishing the external and internal relationships between the criteria and sub-criteria. In this study, since the objective was to prepare land suitability maps with different levels of risk in order to further manage the area, the OWA method was used to prepare land suitability maps. Based on the results of the ANP method for weighing each parameter, wind speed and protected areas were the most and least important parameters to build the power plant. According to the results of the OWA method, 0.78 and 0.1% of the area were suitable for building power plants at high and low risk levels, respectively. The study also found that the number of wind turbines that can be built in the region at both high and low risk levels was 422 and 75, respectively. Using the buffer function, the number of turbines for the construction of high-risk power plants was reduced to 284 by using the appropriate distance from residential areas. The ANP and OWA methods were used to prepare several maps for the evaluation of land suitability with different levels of risk, one of which could be used for the construction of a power plant. © 2022, The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature.
Author Keywords: Analytic network process (ANP) method; Environmental pollution; Ordered weighted averaging (OWA) method; Wind power plant
Index Keywords: building; interpolation; power plant; site selection; wind farm; wind power; wind velocity; atrial natriuretic factor; electric power plant; energy resource; geographic information system; human; pollution; wind; Atrial Natriuretic Factor; Energy-Generating Resources; Environmental Pollution; Geographic Information Systems; Humans; Power Plants; Wind
Metodología:
Como investigador experto en MCDM, presento el análisis técnico del artículo de Mokarram et al. (2022) sobre la selección de sitios para energía **eólica onshore** en Irán: El proceso de decisión multicriterio se estructuró mediante un modelo híbrido que combinó lógica difusa, el Proceso de Red Analítica (ANP) y el Operador de Media Ponderada Ordenada (OWA), justificando el uso de ANP porque, a diferencia del AHP tradicional, este permite "to address the dependencies between indicators and options" y realizar comparaciones tanto externas como internas entre criterios, lo que "leads to more accurate results". Metodológicamente, el proceso comenzó con la creación de mapas de interpolación de parámetros clave, seguidos por la transformación de estas capas en mapas "fuzzy" mediante funciones de membresía lineales para estandarizar las variables. Posteriormente, se determinaron los pesos de importancia utilizando ANP tras consultar a 30 expertos, y finalmente se aplicó el método OWA para generar múltiples mapas de aptitud bajo diferentes niveles de riesgo, permitiendo que "managers can use the results of the OWA method to determine suitable places for power plants with different risks" según las condiciones económicas y ambientales. Como innovación adicional, los autores integraron una función de "buffer" en GIS para garantizar una distancia mínima a zonas residenciales, con el fin de "maintain health and reduce noise pollution".
En cuanto a la configuración del modelo, se emplearon 12 variables de decisión, citadas explícitamente como "wind speed, wind density, wind stability, distance to urban area, distance to village, distance to road, land use, distance to fault, slope, distance to stream, altitude, and distance to protected areas"

Authors: Ajanaku, B.A.; Strager, M.P.; Collins, A.R.
Title: GIS-based multi-criteria decision analysis of utility-scale wind farm site suitability in West Virginia
Abstract: Worldwide, demand is increasing for renewable energy. While wind power is a proven, sustainable energy source, siting can be challenging. Identifying potential sites for wind turbines is a significant step in renewable energy resource planning. Wind turbine site suitability is primarily dependent upon location wind speed along with other economical, environmental and social factors. It is critical to arrive at robust wind farm decision to improve public acceptance, preserve the environment, and assure economic viability. This study provides a modelling approach that incorporates expert opinions of siting criteria in determining potential locations for wind farms in the state of West Virginia. The research involved a two-stage holistic approach: (1) a multi-criteria decision analysis—Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), and (2) Geographic Information System assessment of sites. This research builds upon previous studies and contributes to the literature by incorporating two important components: (1) inclusion of critical wildlife habitat for birds and bats as an elimination criterion within the AHP, and (2) the participation by wind power experts in the AHP decision-making process for the utility-scale wind farm suitability modeling. By incorporating expert opinions with weighing of ten siting factors, about 70,000 hectares of land are identified as 'highly suitable' for wind power development throughout the state of West Virginia. This area represents the potential to yield an estimated 29,000 MW of future utility-scale wind power capacity, which is larger than the current coal dominated electricity generation capacity in West Virginia. © 2021, The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature B.V.
Author Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; GIS-based model; Multiple criteria decision making (MCDM); Spatial analysis; Wind farm

Metodología: El estudio realizado por Ajanaku et al. (2022) evalúa la idoneidad para la ubicación de granjas **eólicas onshore** en Virginia Occidental, utilizando un enfoque holístico de dos etapas que combina el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) con Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con base en metodos fuzzy. La investigación se estructuró de manera que, en la primera etapa, "the AHP method was used to determine the weights of the criteria and sub-criteria", fundamentando esta elección en que el AHP permite descomponer un problema complejo en una estructura jerárquica y utilizar "pairwise comparisons" para derivar pesos basados en la importancia relativa. Posteriormente, en la etapa de GIS, se aplicaron dos procesos distintos: un análisis booleano para "constraint mapping" que excluye áreas donde el desarrollo es imposible, y una "Weighted Overlap Analysis" para las áreas restantes. Los autores justifican este procedimiento integrativo destacando que "the use of GIS and MCDA methods provides a powerful tool for the evaluation of site suitability" al permitir la inclusión de opiniones de expertos y criterios espaciales complejos como la protección de hábitats de vida silvestre. Para este modelo, se utilizaron un total de **14 variables** (sub-criterios), organizadas en tres categorías principales, citadas como: "The criteria used in this study include three main categories: technical/economical, environmental, and social. There are 14 sub-criteria used in the model", los cuales incluyen parámetros específicos como velocidad del viento, proximidad a líneas de transmisión, elevación, pendiente, uso del suelo, y áreas de importancia ecológica para aves y murciélagos.

Authors: Rehman, S.; Mohammed, A.B.; Alhems, L.
Title: GIS-Based Multi-Criteria Wind Farm Site Selection Methodology
Abstract: It is prevalent that by 2050, the global energy demand would increase by 50% to the current demand. The projected demand for electricity in Saudi Arabia is expected to exceed 120 GW/year by 2032. The Saudi government has set a goal of generating 9.5 GW of wind energy by 2023. In this study, firstly, a spatial interpolation technique is used to build a first accurate wind map of the entire country, then a GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making model is developed to select suitable wind farm sites considering various ecological, environmental and socio-economic criteria. All the identified criteria are given equal weightage in the model. Therefore, with noncompliance with even a single criterion, the associated complete area is considered unsuitable. As a case study, this model is independently applied to four regions covering the entire of Saudi Arabia. In the northern region, three wind farm sites are identified. The central area has a more suitable area compared to other regions mainly due to good wind resources and road and grid connectivity. The suitable sites in the eastern region are located between Dammam and Hafr AlBatin city. The southern region has a scarcest suitable area for wind power exploitation located near the province of Madinah. © 2020
Author Keywords: GIS; Multi-criteria decision making; Site suitability analysis; Wind energy; Wind Farm; Wind map
Metodología: En el artículo "GIS-Based Multi-Criteria Wind Farm Site Selection Methodology" , los autores explican que el proceso MCDM se realizó siguiendo un modelo conceptual que integra restricciones económicas, de seguridad, de planificación, ecológicas y geográficas a través de un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS). El procedimiento paso a paso comenzó con la creación de un mapa de vientos preciso mediante interpolación espacial (Inverse Distance Weighted) basado en datos históricos de 39 años ; posteriormente, se aplicó un análisis en el que "all the identified criteria are given equal weightage in the model" , justificando este enfoque simplificado al establecer que "with noncompliance with even a single criterion, the associated complete area is considered unsuitable" para asegurar que solo los sitios que cumplen estrictamente con todas las normativas y políticas nacionales sean seleccionados. En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 10 variables (o criterios de restricción), las cuales incluyen: "High mean wind speed", "Proximity to national grid", "Safe distance away from aerodromes", "Distance from roads/highways", "Distance from settlements", "Safe distance from birding locations and flying paths", "Distance from lakes, rivers, and springs", "Distance from parks, forests and public places", "Terrain slope" y "Minimum suitable continuous area". Finalmente, el estudio se enfoca exclusivamente en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre), ya que se aplica a cuatro regiones que cubren la totalidad del territorio continental de Arabia Saudita.

Authors: Yousefi, H.; Moradi, S.; Zahedi, R.; Ranjbar, Z.
Title: Developed analytic hierarchy process and multi criteria decision support system for wind farm site selection using GIS: A regional-scale application with environmental responsibility
Abstract: As determined by empirical analysis, renewable energy sources account for less than 1.73 percent of the developing countries energy market. The western region of Iran, or province of Kermanshah, presents significant opportunities for power exports to neighboring country (Iraq). In this research, an appropriate location for wind farms in Western Iran is determined with the assistance of a combined method of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) description. Electrical, techno-economic, environmental, and geo-infrastructure aspects, as well as their respective sub-criteria, were subjected to a comprehensive analysis and weighting in this study. The Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) was utilized to achieve this result. The aforementioned conclusions were reached through an extensive examination of insightful interviews conducted with esteemed experts in the domain of wind energy. Significant suitability for the potential installation of large-scale wind turbines or wind farms is evident in six critical areas, encompassing more than 20 % of the evaluated region, according to the findings of this paper. Conversely, it was deduced that precisely 80 % of the areas examined were deemed inappropriate for such endeavors. The assessment yielded four locations that were deemed suitable, denoted as A, B, C, and D. The southern, western, and central regions of the province contain these locations. The proposed total capacity is 216 MW, which is determined by the combined surface areas of these regions (19 km2, 55 km2, 64 km2, and 40 km2, respectively). An anticipated reduction of 1,262,471 tons in greenhouse gas emissions is anticipated as a result of the implementation and substitution of the proposed wind farms for the gas turbine power plants that presently predominate in Iran's thermal power plant sector. The method used in this article can be extended to all cases of other faculties to measure the potential of wind farm resources. © 2024 The Authors
Author Keywords: AHP; Decision Support Systems; MCDM; Renewable Energy; Wind farm
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Artificial intelligence; Decision making; Developing countries; Electric utilities; Gas emissions; Gas plants; Geographic information systems; Greenhouse gases; Hierarchical systems; Location; Site selection; Thermoelectric power plants; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Farm sites; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-criteria decision support systems; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Process criterion; Regional scale; Renewable energies; Wind farm; Decision support systems
Metodología: presento el análisis técnico del artículo "Developed analytic hierarchy process and multi criteria decision support system for wind farm site selection using GIS". El proceso MCDM se desarrolló integrando el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) con Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS), estructurándose de la siguiente manera: primero, se definieron los criterios principales que incluyen factores "environmental, geographical-infrastructure, electrical, and techno-economic" , justificando esta selección mediante "expert judgments and associated concerns" para asegurar la viabilidad técnica y el cumplimiento normativo. Posteriormente, se implementó un diagrama jerárquico donde "The rationale behind the establishment of a decision tree hierarchy is to classify the process of decision-making into four discrete levels, namely goals, criteria, sub-criteria, and possibilities" , seguido por la fase final de "utilizing pair-wise comparison matrices to ascertain the relative importance or weight of each criterion based on its priority". Para validar la precisión del juicio humano, se calculó un "Consistency Ratio (CR)" , asegurando que fuera inferior al 10% para evitar inconsistencias en la toma de decisiones. En total, se utilizaron 20 variables (4 criterios principales y 16 sub-criterios restrictivos o de clasificación) , destacando entre ellas "Wind energy potential", "Distance from electricity grids", "Distance from highways and roads", y diversos "Restrictive criteria buffer zones" como aeropuertos y áreas protegidas. El estudio se centra exclusivamente en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la provincia de Kermanshah, Irán , aunque menciona que investigaciones previas han evaluado la factibilidad de instalaciones "offshore wind turbine" en el país.

Authors: Benti, N.E.; Alemu, Y.B.; Balta, M.M.; Gunta, S.; Chaka, M.D.; Semie, A.G.; Mekonnen, Y.S.; Yohannes, H.
Title: Site suitability assessment for the development of wind power plant in Wolaita area, Southern Ethiopia: an AHP-GIS model
Abstract: The primary driver of economic growth is energy, predominantly derived from fossil fuels, the demand for which has experienced a significant increase since the advent of the Industrial Revolution. The emissions of hazardous gases resulting from the utilization of these fuels have been well acknowledged, therefore exerting a notable impact on the environment. In the context of Ethiopia, it is observed that despite the presence of ample renewable resources, the accessibility to power continues to be constrained. In order to effectively tackle this issue, it is imperative to redirect attention towards the utilization of renewable sources, such as wind energy, as a means of enhancing the existing power grid infrastructure. The present study used geospatial tools to evaluate the appropriateness of the Wolayita region for the establishment of a wind power facility. The process of site selection is guided by multiple factors, and a multi-criteria approach is facilitated through the utilization of Geographic Information System (GIS). The evaluation of seven characteristics was conducted utilizing the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) methodology, which involved pairwise comparisons and weighted scoring. The process of suitability mapping involves the classification of locations into four distinct categories, which range from the most suitable to the least suitable. The findings demonstrate that the area of 0.628% (28.00 km2) is deemed the most suitable, while 54.61% (2433.96 km2) is considered somewhat acceptable. Additionally, 0.85% (37.85 km2) is identified as the least suitable, leaving a remaining 43.91% (1060.00 km2) that is deemed unsuitable. The central, northwestern, and southern regions are identified as optimal geographic areas. The results of this study facilitate the process of investing in renewable energy, thereby assisting Ethiopian authorities and organizations in promoting sustainable development. This report serves as a crucial reference point for the wind energy industry. © 2023, The Author(s).
Index Keywords: analytic hierarchy process; article; attention; Ethiopia; geographic information system; organization; renewable energy; sustainable development; wind farm; economic development; renewable resource; wind power; fossil fuel; fuel
Metodología: analizo el artículo "Site suitability assessment for the development of wind power plant in Wolaita area, Southern Ethiopia: an AHP-GIS model". El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) con un Sistema de Información Geográfica (GIS), siguiendo un flujo de trabajo estructurado: primero, se realizó la recopilación de datos de múltiples fuentes; luego, se procedió a la "identification and classification of criteria", donde se aplicaron "Boolean and fuzzy membership logic" para transformar los datos en mapas de idoneidad. El paso crucial fue el uso de AHP para determinar los pesos de los criterios, justificando que "the AHP is widely used for making decisions in a variety of contexts, particularly in the assessment of suitability for different sites" debido a su capacidad para manejar la subjetividad de los expertos y estructurar problemas complejos. Finalmente, se realizó una "Weighted overlay analysis" para combinar las capas y un "Sensitivity analysis" para validar la estabilidad de los resultados, argumentando que este último "identifies the factors that significantly impact the suitability model". En este estudio se utilizaron un total de 7 variables ("evaluation of seven characteristics was conducted"), específicamente: "Wind speed", "Slope", "Elevation", "Distance from road", "Distance from grid", "Distance from town" y "Land use/Land cover". El proyecto se centra en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la zona de Wolaita, Etiopía.

Authors: Spyridonidou, S.; Vagiona, D.G.
Title: Spatial energy planning of offshore wind farms in Greece using GIS and a hybrid MCDM methodological approach
Abstract: The present paper focuses on the efficient development of offshore wind farms in shallow waters in Greece. An integrated spatial energy planning approach is introduced. The proposed methodology is implemented using geographic information system and Statistical Design Institute software and applied at the national spatial planning scale. It consists of four distinct stages: (1) identifying appropriate areas to site offshore wind farms (the macro-siting configuration), (2) determining the principal technical specifications of the offshore wind turbines as well as the micro-siting configurations of offshore wind projects, (3) estimating the total investment cost of each potential offshore wind project, and (4) prioritizing the most suitable potential sites for offshore wind farms based on specific assessment criteria. The final outcomes of the proposed methodology are the development of a marine site suitability index and an assessment of the effects of different policy orientations for offshore wind farm siting on that index. Four ‘what-if’ critical scenarios are applied to test the robustness of the overall suitability index to the subjectivity of expert judgments of potential sites and thus assess the reliability of the proposed methodology. The implementation of the proposed methodology could facilitate the fulfillment of national targets for the energy sector and encourage energy interdependence among many geographic areas in Greece, since the total estimated production capacity of all the proposed offshore wind projects is expected to reach 1,185 MW. © 2020, Springer Nature Switzerland AG.
Author Keywords: Geographic information systems; Multicriteria decision-making; Offshore wind farms; Sensitivity analysis; Site selection process; Spatial energy planning
Metodología: en el artículo "Spatial energy planning of offshore wind farms in Greece using GIS and a hybrid MCDM methodological approach". El proceso MCDM se desarrolló a través de un enfoque híbrido de cuatro etapas diseñado para la planificación espacial nacional: primero, se realizó el "macro-siting" mediante GIS para identificar áreas de exclusión basadas en legislación y limitaciones técnicas; luego, se determinaron las especificaciones técnicas y el "micro-siting" de los aerogeneradores. El núcleo de la decisión se ejecutó en la etapa de priorización mediante la combinación de los métodos AHP y TOPSIS, justificando que "the AHP is used for calculating the weights of the assessment criteria, whereas the TOPSIS method is used for the final ranking of the potential OWF [Offshore Wind Farm] sites", lo cual permite una evaluación robusta que equilibra la importancia relativa de los factores con el desempeño de cada alternativa frente a una solución ideal. En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 10 variables de evaluación (además de los criterios de exclusión), citando criterios como "Wind velocity", "Water depth", "Distance from the shore", "Distance from the electrical grid", "Distance from ports", "Distance from main settlements", "Distance from submarine cables", "Shipping density", "Visual impact" y "Power density". Este trabajo se dedica exclusivamente a la energía eólica de tipo offshore (marina) en las aguas territoriales de Grecia.

Authors: Bertsiou, M.M.; Theochari, A.P.; Baltas, E.
Title: Multi-criteria analysis and Geographic Information Systems methods for wind turbine siting in a North Aegean island
Abstract: The need for energy independence of small remoted islands, promote the exploration of renewable energy sources. Greek islands have rich wind potential and subsequently, the attention is being drawn to the use of wind turbines to meet their energy needs. However, the optimal location of wind turbines installation is the basis for the exploitation of high wind potential in combination with the current legislation framework. This paper presents a combined use of Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) and Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for the evaluation of eligible sites for wind turbine installation in North Aegean island, in Greece. Criteria weights are estimated for three scenarios using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for the illustration of the impact on the final design. The minimum distance from the main settlement of the island constitutes an extra constraint for the investigation of the capability of installing wind farms with the lowest possible energy losses due to power transmission via cables. In all scenarios, the prohibited zone corresponds to 27.9% of the total area of the island. Also, sites with high wind potential can be combined with lower altitudes and can give even more suitable locations for installation, reducing, thus, the installation cost. Economic-oriented scenario appears to combine both low cost and larger surface area for installation. A sensitivity analysis is performed, for determining the impact of the criteria on the suitability of a site. It seems that new suitability maps are affected greater by the type of the chosen criterion rather than the weight. © 2020 The Authors. Energy Science & Engineering published by the Society of Chemical Industry and John Wiley & Sons Ltd.
Author Keywords: AHP; GIS–MCDM; wind energy; wind turbine siting
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Costs; Decision making; Energy dissipation; Geographic information systems; Information systems; Information use; Installation; Sensitivity analysis; Wind power; Analytic hierarchy process (ahp); Energy independence; Installation costs; Multi Criteria Analysis; Multi-criteria decision making; Renewable energy source; Turbine installation; Wind turbine siting; Wind turbines
Metodología: analizo el artículo "Multi-criteria analysis and Geographic Information Systems methods for wind turbine siting in a North Aegean island". El proceso MCDM se desarrolló integrando GIS con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) en una metodología de dos fases: primero, se aplicó un análisis de exclusión basado en criterios de restricción (Boolean logic) donde "exclusion areas are defined based on the current national legislation and restrictions arise from technical or environmental factors", justificando que este paso garantiza el cumplimiento legal y la viabilidad técnica inicial. En la segunda fase, se realizó la evaluación de las áreas elegibles mediante AHP, donde "the weights of the criteria are determined through the Analytic Hierarchy Process, which is based on the decomposition of a complex problem into a hierarchy", justificando su uso porque permite "the evaluation of the relative importance of each criterion by making pair-wise comparisons". Finalmente, se utilizó la técnica de Combinación Lineal Ponderada (WLC) para generar el mapa final de idoneidad. En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 13 variables (8 criterios de exclusión y 5 criterios de evaluación), citando factores como "Wind speed", "Distance from the power grid", "Distance from the road network", "Slope", y "Land use". El estudio se enfoca en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la isla de Samotracia, Grecia.

Authors: Yaman, A.
Title: A GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making approach (GIS-MCDM) for determination of the most appropriate site selection of onshore wind farm in Adana, Turkey
Abstract: Energy stands as an indispensable global need, yet the finite nature of rapidly depleting fossil fuel reserves poses environmental challenges. To address this, numerous nations are shifting toward sustainable energy sources to foster environmental well-being while satisfying their energy requirements. Among these alternatives, wind energy emerges as a particularly efficient option. This study focuses on identifying optimal sites for installing wind power plants in Adana province through the integration of the analytic hierarchy process (AHP) and geographic information system (GIS). Within this framework, a comprehensive set of 15 criteria was delineated along with their respective sub-criteria. It is thought that the criteria used in the study will be useful not only for Adana province but also for other provinces that are suitable for wind turbine placement and planned to be built. The outcome of this investigation delineates potential areas in Adana province deemed suitable for establishing wind power plants. As a result, analysis reveals that 9.94% of the study area is conducive to establishing a wind farm (WF), while 51.66% is deemed moderately suitable. Notably, the districts of Feke, Tufanbeyli, Saimbeyli, and Pozantı emerge as particularly well-suited for this purpose. This study aims to contribute to the existing literature by identifying precise and suitable areas by combining GIS and AHP in the WF site selection process. In the study, a new research perspective is presented by taking into account the uncertainty in the site selection process and the concept of sustainability in three different dimensions: technical, economic, and environmental, thus aiming to guide decision-makers for future WF projects. In addition, it is thought that the methods and criteria used in the study will also be guiding for future WF site selection studies. © The Author(s) 2024.
Author Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process (AHP); Energy; Geographic information systems (GIS); Multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM); Wind farm
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric utilities; Fossil fuels; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Information systems; Information use; Proven reserves; Site selection; Sustainable development; Analytic hierarchy process; Energy; Farm sites; Geographic information system; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Wind farm; Wind power plants
Metodología: "A GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making approach (GIS-MCDM) for determination of the most appropriate site selection of onshore wind farm in Adana, Turkey". El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) con Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS), siguiendo una metodología estructurada en la que primero se definieron los criterios y subcriterios relevantes para la región; posteriormente, se utilizó el método AHP para determinar los pesos de importancia relativa, justificando que "AHP is a widely used multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) method that helps to solve complex decision-making problems by breaking them down into a hierarchy of criteria and sub-criteria" y permitiendo así "a more systematic and objective evaluation of the importance of each factor". Tras calcular los pesos y verificar el índice de consistencia, se procedió a la "standardization and overlaying of layers" en el entorno GIS para generar el mapa final de idoneidad, argumentando que esta integración es fundamental para manejar "the spatial dimension of the decision-making process". En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 15 variables (criterios), entre los que se encuentran: "Wind Speed", "Slope", "Aspect", "Elevation", "Distance to Road", "Distance to Transmission Lines", "Distance to Settlement", "Distance to Airport", "Distance to Railway", "Distance to Lake/River", "Distance to Protected Areas", "Distance to Cultural Heritage", "Distance to Fault Lines", "Distance to Bird Migration Routes" y "Land Use". Este trabajo se centra en la energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la provincia de Adana, Turquía.

Authors: Moltames, R.; Naghavi, M.S.; Silakhori, M.; Noorollahi, Y.; Yousefi, H.; Hajiaghei-Keshteli, M.; Azizimehr, B.
Title: Multi-Criteria Decision Methods for Selecting a Wind Farm Site Using a Geographic Information System (GIS)
Abstract: Wind energy is an economically, technically, and environmentally attractive option due to its cheapness and availability in different regions. The most important obstacle to developing renewable resources in Iran is subsidies for fossil fuels. The Iranian government has recently decided to reduce subsidies for electricity and fossil fuels, which has led to an increase in the prices of fossil fuels and electricity and makes renewable technologies more attractive to use. This study uses a multi-criteria decision method to identify wind energy potential in Khuzestan province. A GIS is used to determine the wind energy potential in this province. The technical, environmental, and economic criteria, which are a total of 14 layers of information, were examined by considering different values for each and from a Boolean point of view. The results show that, from the economic point of view, Shadegan city has the most potential and, from the technical point of view, Khorramshahr city has the highest amount of electricity production through wind energy. Furthermore, Dasht-e Azadegan city, due to its population, can supply the maximum amount of electricity it needs through wind energy.
