Among the three 550, 2500, and 8000 kW turbines, the 550 kW turbine has the most potential in the region. © 2022 by the authors.
Author Keywords: GIS; multi-criteria decision method; site selection; wind energy; windfarm
Index Keywords: electricity generation; GIS; multicriteria analysis; site selection; wind farm; wind power; Iran; Khorramshahr; Khuzestan
Metodología: En el artículo "Multi-Criteria Decision Methods for Selecting a Wind Farm Site Using a Geographic Information System (GIS)", el proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando GIS con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) mediante un modelo conceptual diseñado para la provincia de Khuzestan, Irán. El proceso comenzó con la recopilación y análisis de 14 capas de información técnica, ambiental y económica, donde el primer paso consistió en aplicar un análisis de exclusión basado en lógica booleana: "Once the constraints are determined based on Boolean logic, the CON tool is used to assign a true or false value (zero and one), and all areas covered by the restrictions are removed". Posteriormente, se utilizó el método AHP para determinar el peso de importancia de cada criterio, justificando su uso porque "is a comprehensive, logical, and structural framework that allows the analyst to understand complex decisions by analyzing the problem in a hierarchical structure" , permitiendo al tomador de decisiones representar problemas complejos y evaluar las relaciones entre metas y opciones. El estudio utilizó un total de 14 variables ("total of 14 layers of information") que incluyen: "Slope", "Wind speed", "Distance from the airport", "Distance from power transmission lines", "Distance from the railway", "Distance from protected areas", "Distance from the lake", "Distance from the main rivers", "Distance from tributaries", "Distance from the road", "Distance from the city", "Distance from the village", "Distance from the dam" y "Land use". Finalmente, la investigación se centra en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre), evaluando el potencial para la instalación de turbinas en regiones continentales de la provincia mencionada.

Authors: Zahid, F.; Tahir, A.; Khan, H.U.; Naeem, M.A.
Title: Wind farms selection using geospatial technologies and energy generation capacity in Gwadar
Abstract: Pakistan has been a victim of energy crisis since last few decades. This energy crisis has adversely affected country's socio-economic development and continues to do so. The continuously increasing demand–supply gap has negatively impacted the economic stability of the country. With the recent awareness and development of alternate energy resources like wind and solar, the current energy crisis can be minimized. However, proper planning is essential for successful execution of these renewable energy projects. This study aims to identify the suitable sites for wind farms in District Gwadar, Balochistan using Geographical Information Systems (GIS) and Web-based Spatial Decision Support System (SDSS). In this study, multi-criteria decision making is applied which assists breaking down the site selection complexity. Multi-Criteria evaluation methods provides different set of procedures that facilitate decision making by analyzing different alternatives. The underlying geospatial and ICT technologies used in this analysis form the core component of the planning process. Gwadar is currently drawing investor's attention due to its geographical location, deep seaport, and proposed China–Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC). This research is useful for stakeholders of Wind Energy to explore the wind potentials using GIS as an interactive decision-making tool during the pre-feasibility stage. Furthermore, this research has considered the environmental, social and economic aspects during the decision-making process of wind farm development. This is the strength of multi-criteria evaluation as differently weighted scenarios provide different output, depending on the factors considered of highest importance. A detailed analysis of the sites in terms of their wind potential and energy generation capacity has also been reported in this study. This long coastline of Balochistan with huge wind energy potential has not been explored yet and therefore this study will assist researchers to further explore this area and can have a positive impact on CPEC. © 2021 The Authors
Author Keywords: CPEC; Geoserver; Multi-criteria analysis Gwadar; PostgreSQL; SDSS GIS; Spatial; Wind analysis
Index Keywords: Artificial intelligence; Decision making; Decision support systems; Economic and social effects; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Information use; Site selection; Wind power; China–pakistan economic corridor; Geoserver; Multi-criteria analyse gwadar; Multicriteria analysis; Pakistan; PostgreSQL; Spatial; Spatial decision support system geographical information system; Spatial decision support systems; Wind analysis; Energy policy
Metodología: "Wind farms selection using geospatial technologies and energy generation capacity in Gwadar". El proceso MCDM se ejecutó integrando Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) mediante un marco de trabajo de tres niveles: "The primary purpose of level-1 is to categorize the available data into three main categories (i.e. economic, social, and environmental)", seguido del nivel 2 donde se calculan los pesos de los criterios usando AHP, justificando que "AHP provides an objective way to calculate weights of different criteria" al permitir comparaciones por pares que reducen la subjetividad. Finalmente, en el nivel 3, se realizó un análisis de superposición ponderada (Weighted Overlay) y una evaluación de la capacidad de generación energética, justificando este enfoque integral porque "site selection should not only be based on physical factors but also on the potential energy yield and economic feasibility". En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 10 variables principales, citando específicamente: "Wind speed", "Slope", "Land use", "Distance from roads", "Distance from grid lines", "Distance from settlements", "Distance from airports", "Distance from coastline", "Distance from bird habitats" y "Distance from protected areas". Este trabajo se enfoca en la energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la región costera de Gwadar, Pakistán.

Authors: Adedeji, P.A.; Akinlabi, S.A.; Madushele, N.; Olatunji, O.O.
Title: Hybrid neurofuzzy investigation of short-term variability of wind resource in site suitability analysis: a case study in South Africa
Abstract: Energy generation from wind resources is now a mature technology with the ability to compete with traditional energy sources at utility scales in many countries, through the identification of suitable sites. However, beyond site suitability, predicting the wind resource variability of the potentially viable site presents overarching benefits in strategic and operational planning prior to site development. This study, therefore, combines geographical information systems multicriteria decision-making (GIS-MCDM) and hybrid neurofuzzy modeling tools for site suitability and resource variability forecast, respectively, in the Eastern Cape Province of South Africa. The GIS model uses two factors (climatological and environmental), and analytical hierarchical process was used for evaluating criteria degree of influence. Wind resource variability using diurnal satellite-based data for the candidate site was used on the models. Adaptive neurofuzzy inference system models hybrid with genetic algorithm (GA-ANFIS) and particle swarm optimization (PSO-ANFIS) were compared with standalone ANFIS and Levenberg–Marquardt backpropagation neural network (LMBP-ANN) using six statistical measures of error, accuracy, and variability. The GA-ANFIS and PSO-ANFIS accurately model the resource with PSO-ANFIS having lesser computational time compared to GA-ANFIS. However, LMBP-ANN is most robust and resistant in modeling the resource variability among the four models. Hence, wind resource variability investigation on a potentially viable site obtained from the GIS-MCDM model can complement on-site investigations prior to site development. Also, tuning ANFIS with evolutionary algorithms offers improved accuracy over standalone ANFIS model for wind resource forecast and further its robustness in predicting variability of the resource. From our findings, cross-boundary wind resource exploration between South Africa and Lesotho could foster regional interconnectivity. © 2021, The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag London Ltd., part of Springer Nature.
Author Keywords: ANFIS; GA; GIS-MCDM; PSO; Site suitability analysis; South Africa; Wind energy
Index Keywords: Backpropagation; Decision making; Forecasting; Genetic algorithms; Geographic information systems; Inference engines; Particle swarm optimization (PSO); Wind power; Adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system; Analytical hierarchical process; Back propagation neural networks; Multi criteria decision making; On site investigations; Suitability analysis; Variability forecasts; Wind resource variabilities; Fuzzy inference
Metodología: "Hybrid neurofuzzy investigation of short-term variability of wind resource in site suitability analysis: a case study in South Africa". El proceso MCDM se realizó mediante la integración de GIS con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) en un marco de evaluación de dos niveles: primero, se llevó a cabo un análisis de idoneidad espacial donde "the GIS-MCDM model was used to evaluate the degree of suitability of the study area", utilizando un enfoque de superposición ponderada para combinar capas temáticas. El paso fundamental fue el uso de AHP para la ponderación, justificando que "the decision-making process is based on the priority of each factor over the other in terms of the set objective", lo cual permite estructurar la jerarquía de decisión y validar la consistencia de los juicios de los expertos mediante el Consistency Ratio (CR < 0.1). Posteriormente, el proceso avanzó a una fase de modelado neuro-difuso (ANFIS) para predecir la variabilidad del recurso, justificando esta combinación híbrida porque "beyond site suitability, predicting the wind resource variability... presents overarching benefits in strategic and operational planning". En este estudio se utilizaron un total de 8 variables principales, divididas en factores climatológicos ("Wind speed", "Wind power density", "Air density") y factores ambientales/de infraestructura ("Slope", "Land cover", "Distance to road", "Distance to transmission lines", "Distance to settlements"). El estudio se centra exclusivamente en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en la provincia de Eastern Cape, Sudáfrica

Authors: Karamountzou, S.; Vagiona, D.G.
Title: Suitability and Sustainability Assessment of Existing Onshore Wind Farms in Greece
Abstract: Site selection for wind farm projects is a vital issue that should be considered in spatial energy planning. This study explores the deployment of onshore wind farms (OWFs) in Greece and assesses their suitability and sustainability using geographic information systems and multicriteria analysis techniques (the analytical hierarchy process—AHP and Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution—TOPSIS). Their suitability is assessed in terms of seven exclusion criteria and constraints provided in the Specific Framework for Spatial Planning and Sustainable Development for Renewable Energy Sources (SFSPSD-RES), while their sustainability is assessed in terms of nine environmental, technical-economic, and social assessment criteria in five different scenarios. The obtained results indicated that 81.4% of the existing wind farms are included within suitable areas and the highest percentage of improper siting refers to the installation of wind farms in sites that are within the boundaries of the Natura 2000 protected areas. The existing wind farms located in a part of Peloponnese, at the point bordering the Administrative Region (AR) of Attica, are characterized as more ideal in four out of five of the examined scenarios in the sustainability assessment. The proposed framework of this study is practical and effective in assessing the suitability and sustainability of existing wind farms in a country, and could contribute to spatial energy planning. © 2023 by the authors.
Author Keywords: analytic hierarchy process (AHP); multicriteria decision making (MCDM); suitability assessment; sustainability assessment; Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS); windfarms
Index Keywords: alternative energy; analytical hierarchy process; energy planning; GIS; multicriteria analysis; protected area; spatial planning; sustainability; sustainable development; wind farm; Attica; Greece; Peloponnese
Metodología: "Suitability and Sustainability Assessment of Existing Onshore Wind Farms in Greece". El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo mediante un enfoque de dos fases que combina GIS con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) y TOPSIS: primero, se realizó un análisis de exclusión basado en criterios restrictivos donde "exclusion zones were identified based on the current Special Framework for Spatial Planning and Sustainable Development for Renewable Energy Sources in Greece", justificando que este paso asegura el cumplimiento legal y la protección de áreas sensibles. En la segunda fase, se utilizó AHP para determinar los pesos de los criterios y subcriterios, justificando que "the AHP method is based on the principle that the experience and knowledge of the experts are at least as important as the data used", para luego aplicar el método TOPSIS con el fin de clasificar los parques eólicos existentes, argumentando que este permite evaluar el desempeño frente a una "positive ideal solution" y una "negative ideal solution". En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 14 variables (criterios de evaluación), divididas en categorías técnicas ("Wind velocity", "Distance from the power grid", "Distance from the road network"), geográficas ("Slope", "Elevation"), ambientales ("Distance from protected areas", "Land use") y socio-económicas ("Distance from settlements", "Distance from archaeological sites", "Visual impact"). El estudio se centra exclusivamente en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en el territorio de Grecia.

Authors: Ifkirne, M.; El Bouhi, H.; Acharki, S.; Pham, Q.B.; Farah, A.; Linh, N.T.T.
Title: Multi-Criteria GIS-Based Analysis for Mapping Suitable Sites for Onshore Wind Farms in Southeast France
Abstract: Wind energy is critical to traditional energy sources replacement in France and throughout the world. Wind energy generation in France is quite unevenly spread across the country. Despite its considerable wind potential, the research region is among the least productive. The region is a very complicated location where socio-environmental, technological, and topographical restrictions intersect, which is why energy production planning studies in this area have been delayed. In this research, the methodology used for identifying appropriate sites for future wind farms in this region combines GIS with MCDA approaches such as AHP. Six determining factors are selected: the average wind speed, which has a weight of 38%; the protected areas, which have a relative weight of 26%; the distance to electrical substations and road networks, both of which have a significant influence on relative weights of 13%; and finally, the slope and elevation, which have weights of 5% and 3%, respectively. Only one alternative was investigated (suitable and unsuitable). The spatial database was generated using ArcGIS and QGIS software; the AHP was computed using Excel; and several treatments, such as raster data categorization and weighted overlay, were automated using the Python programming language. The regions identified for wind turbines installation are defined by a total of 962,612 pixels, which cover a total of 651 km2 and represent around 6.98% of the research area. The theoretical wind potential calculation results suggest that for at least one site with an area bigger than 400 ha, the energy output ranges between 182.60 and 280.20 MW. The planned sites appear to be suitable; each site can support an average installed capacity of 45 MW. This energy benefit will fulfill the region’s population’s transportation, heating, and electrical demands. © 2022 by the authors.
Author Keywords: France; GIS; MCDA-AHP; onshore wind farms; spatial energy planning; suitability map
Metodología: "Multi-Criteria GIS-Based Analysis for Mapping Suitable Sites for Onshore Wind Farms in Southeast France". El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo integrando Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP), estructurado en una metodología que primero seleccionó factores determinantes y luego utilizó el AHP para calcular sus pesos relativos mediante comparaciones por pares, justificando que "the AHP was computed using Excel" y que el proceso permite manejar la intersección de "socio-environmental, technological, and topographical restrictions" en una zona compleja. El procedimiento incluyó la automatización de tareas como la categorización de datos raster y la superposición ponderada (Weighted Overlay) mediante código en Python, lo cual se justifica para facilitar la identificación de sitios óptimos en regiones con "insufficient infrastructures". En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 6 variables principales, citando: "average wind speed" (38%), "protected areas" (26%), "distance to electrical substations" (13%), "road networks" (13%), "slope" (5%) y "elevation" (3%). Finalmente, la investigación se centra en energía eólica de tipo onshore (terrestre) en el sureste de Francia.

Authors: Dimitriou, I.C.; Sarmas, E.; Trachanas, G.P.; Marinakis, V.; Doukas, H.
Title: Multi-Criteria GIS-based offshore wind farm site selection: Case study in Greece
Abstract: The growing global demand for further penetration of renewable energy sources has highlighted Offshore Wind Farms (OWFs) as a promising solution, particularly in Greece, due to its abundance of wind resources. To address the challenges of optimal OWFs location, this study presents an integrated approach that combines Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) methods with a Geographic Information System (GIS). The potential offshore sites have been evaluated across various technical, environmental, legislative, and safety criteria, utilizing incomplete information weights derived from the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP). This approach accommodates inherent impreciseness in both the payoff table values and criteria weights, leading to the adoption of an extension of the VlseKriterijumska Optimizacija I Kompromisno Resenjee (VIKOR) method. The applied ranking process identified the most preferable sites in Greece, incorporating different decision makers’ perspectives. The methodology ranked 35 distinct sites in the Greek sea, including, but not limited to, areas south of Alexandroupoli, east of Xerokampos Crete, and near the Gulf of Patras. The novelty of this approach could play a pivotal role in the development of offshore wind power production within Greece's energy transition context. Finally, the proposed framework can be easily generalized, replicated and adapted in any country, thus contributing to the optimal placement of OWFs at a global scale. © 2024
Author Keywords: AHP; Extended VIKOR; Geographic information systems; Multi-Criteria Decision-Making; Offshore Wind Farms; Renewable energy
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Windmill; Analytic hierarchy; Extended vlsekriterijumska optimizacija I kompromisno resenjee; Geographic information; Hierarchy process; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Offshore winds; Renewable energies; Wind farm; Offshore wind farms
Metodología: "Multi-Criteria GIS-based offshore wind farm site selection: Case study in Greece". El proceso MCDM se desarrolló integrando GIS con un enfoque híbrido de los métodos AHP y VIKOR extendido, estructurado en tres fases: primero, se aplicó un filtrado por criterios de exclusión (Boolean logic) para identificar áreas candidatas, justificando que "the use of exclusion criteria is the first step in the OWF site selection process... ensuring that the selected areas do not conflict with other marine uses or environmental protections". Segundo, se determinaron los pesos de los criterios mediante el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP), cuya elección se justifica porque "AHP is one of the most popular and widely used MCDM methods for determining weights, as it can effectively handle both qualitative and quantitative criteria". Finalmente, se utilizó el método VIKOR extendido para clasificar las alternativas, justificando su uso debido a su capacidad para manejar la "compromise solution" y la "imprecise information" inherente a los datos marinos. En el estudio se utilizaron un total de 10 variables de evaluación (además de los criterios de restricción), citando específicamente: "Wind speed", "Water depth", "Distance from shore", "Distance from electrical grid", "Distance from ports", "Distance from main settlements", "Distance from submarine cables", "Shipping density", "Visual impact" y "Power density". Este trabajo se dedica exclusivamente a la energía eólica de tipo offshore (marina) en las cuencas del Mar Egeo y el Mar Jónico en Grecia.

Authors: Rehman, S.; Mohammed, A.B.; Alhems, L.
Title: A heuristic approach to siting and design optimization of an onshore wind farm layout
Abstract: The forecasted electricity demand in Saudi Arabia may be around 120 GW/year by 2032. As per the latest government announcement, Saudi Arabia is aiming to install 57.5 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030. In this study, firstly, a wind map is developed based on the historical wind data, recorded over a 39-year period, followed by the development of the geographic information system (GIS)-based multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) model for suitable wind farm site selection for Hijaz, the western region of Saudi Arabia. This region is selected as it has a population density of around 25 per sq. km, the highest in Saudi Arabia. For the model, data from various ecological, environmental, and socioeconomic criteria are considered. Finally, the optimization of the wind farm layout on the identified suitable region of 5.5 km × 4 km is performed using the deep-array wake model, DAWM. The optimized layout has locations for 30 wind turbines of 3 MW rated capacity. This optimization process minimizes energy losses and costs and maximizes power production. The net and gross energy production from the wind farm are expected to be 143 GWh and 156 GWh, respectively, with an array loss of 8.25% at a cost of energy of USD 65.66 per MWh, and a capacity factor of 17.7%. The cost calculations include the capital cost of constructing the access roads and a complete collector system with two substations. The optimized turbine positions in the layout have a major and minor axis separation of 1680 m and 448 m, respectively. © 2020 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.
Author Keywords: Cost of energy; Design optimization of wind farm layout; GIS; Site suitability analysis; Wake effect; Wind power
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric power generation; Electric utilities; Energy dissipation; Heuristic methods; Optimization; Population statistics; Site selection; Design optimization; Electricity demands; Energy productions; Heuristic approach; Multi-criteria decision making; Population densities; Renewable energy capacity; Socio-economic criterion; Onshore wind farms
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "A Heuristic Approach to Siting and Design Optimization of an Onshore Wind Farm Layout", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en la región de Hijaz, en el oeste de Arabia Saudita. El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso comenzando con un análisis demográfico para identificar la región con mayor densidad poblacional, seguido por la identificación de "a complete set of ecological, environmental, and socioeconomic constraints and policies". Posteriormente, se desarrolló un mapa eólico preciso utilizando el método de interpolación espacial de Distancia Inversa Ponderada (IDW) basado en datos históricos de 40 años. La metodología MCDM específica utilizada fue el Análisis de Idoneidad Basado en GIS (GIS-based Multi-Criteria Decision Making), aplicado mediante el software ArcMap, donde se asignó el mismo peso a todos los criterios de exclusión. Este enfoque se justifica como una herramienta ideal para encontrar ubicaciones que satisfagan múltiples restricciones complejas, señalando que "the multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) analysis in the geographic information system (GIS) background is an ideal tool for finding the suitable location of a wind farm". Además, el estudio destaca su novedad al combinar la selección de sitios con una optimización posterior del diseño del parque eólico mediante el modelo de estela de matriz profunda (DAWM). En total, el modelo consideró una amplia gama de factores divididos en 3 categorías principales (ecológicos, ambientales y socioeconómicos), aunque el análisis operativo final se centró en la integración de datos geoespaciales y de recursos eólicos para validar la viabilidad técnica y económica del área seleccionada.

Authors: Rae, G.; Erfort, G.
Title: Offshore wind energy - South Africa's untapped resource
Abstract: In the context of the Anthropocene, the decoupling of carbon emissions from electricity generation is critical. South Africa has an ageing coal power fleet, which will gradually be decommissioned over the next 30 years. This creates substantial opportunity for a just transition towards a future energy mix with a high renewable energy penetration. Offshore wind technology is a clean electricity generation alternative that presents great power security and decarbonisation opportunity for South Africa. This study estimated the offshore wind energy resource available within South Africa's exclusive economic zone (EEZ), using a geographic information system methodology. The available resource was estimated under four developmental scenarios. This study revealed that South Africa has an annual offshore wind energy production potential of 44.52 TWh at ocean depths of less than 50 m (Scenario 1) and 2 387.08 TWh at depths less than 1 000 m (Scenario 2). Furthermore, a GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation was conducted to determine the most suitable locations for offshore wind farm development within the South African EEZ. The following suitable offshore wind development regions were identified: Richards Bay, KwaDukuza, Durban, and Struis Bay. Based on South Africa's annual electricity consumption of 297.8 TWh in 2018, OWE could theoretically supply approximately 15% and 800% of South Africa's annual electricity demand with offshore wind development Scenario 1 and 2 respectively. © 2020. All Rights Reserved.
Author Keywords: GIS spatial analysis; offshore wind energy potential; renewable energy; resource assessment; site selection
Metodología: La investigación empleó una metodología de tipo bottom-up apoyada en sistemas de información geográfica (GIS) para estimar el potencial de energía eólica marina y determinar zonas óptimas de desarrollo en la Zona Económica Exclusiva de Sudáfrica. El análisis se basó en datos climáticos de largo plazo, utilizando el conjunto de reanálisis ERA5, del cual se extrajeron promedios mensuales de velocidad del viento correspondientes a un periodo de 30 años (1988–2018), con el fin de representar adecuadamente el recurso eólico a largo plazo.Las velocidades de viento, originalmente referidas a 10 m sobre el nivel del mar, fueron extrapoladas a 100 m de altura de buje mediante la ley logarítmica del perfil del viento, asumiendo una rugosidad típica del océano. Para caracterizar la variabilidad anual del viento, se adoptó una distribución de Rayleigh, lo que permitió estimar la frecuencia de ocurrencia de distintas velocidades de viento a partir de los valores medios. Con estas distribuciones y la curva de potencia de la turbina seleccionada (Vestas V164 de 8 MW), se calculó el factor de capacidad asociado a cada valor de velocidad media, y posteriormente se generó un mapa espacial del factor de capacidad para toda el área de estudio.El potencial de generación se estimó asumiendo un diseño teórico de parque eólico, con una disposición regular de turbinas y un espaciamiento definido en función del diámetro del rotor. A partir de esta configuración se calculó una densidad de potencia por unidad de área, que fue ajustada considerando pérdidas típicas de parques eólicos marinos, tales como pérdidas por estela, pérdidas eléctricas, disponibilidad y operación y mantenimiento. La producción anual de energía (AEP) se obtuvo multiplicando, para cada píxel espacial, el factor de capacidad por el área correspondiente y la densidad de potencia ajustada, y posteriormente sumando los resultados sobre toda el área disponible.El estudio incorporó además restricciones espaciales y técnicas, como límites de profundidad marina, exclusión de áreas marinas protegidas, umbrales mínimos de velocidad del viento y, en algunos escenarios, una franja de exclusión cercana a la costa para considerar impactos visuales. Con base en estas restricciones se definieron varios escenarios de desarrollo, tanto para aguas someras como profundas.Finalmente, para identificar las zonas más adecuadas para la instalación de parques eólicos marinos, se aplicó una evaluación multicriterio en GIS, que combinó factores como el factor de capacidad, la profundidad del mar y la proximidad a infraestructura eléctrica y portuaria. Estos criterios fueron ponderados mediante el método del Proceso Analítico Jerárquico (AHP), generando un mapa de idoneidad espacial que permitió destacar las regiones con mayor potencial técnico para el desarrollo de energía eólica marina.

Authors: Amsharuk, A.; Łaska, G.
Title: The Approach to Finding Locations for Wind Farms Using GIS and MCDA: Case Study Based on Podlaskie Voivodeship, Poland
Abstract: This article presents an approach to locating wind farms based on open-source data, GIS software (QGiS v.3.32 Lima), and multicriteria decision-making methods such as AHP, TOPSIS and Borda. The article aims to check the availability of the most suitable sites for wind farms in Podlaskie Voivodeship, Poland. After the site-selection process, 704 plots were selected after three stages of exclusion (technical–natural, social, and economic). These plots cover a total area of 32.50 km2, about 0.16% of the region’s area. The results show that Podlaskie Voivodeship has the most considerable clustering of suitable sites for wind farm construction in three districts, with a total area of 21.53 km2. The first district is in the southwestern part of the region, with an area of 14.84 km2; the second district is in the southeastern part of the region, with an area of 5.59 km2; and the third district is in the northern part of the region, with an area of 1.1 km2. A selected area of 32.50 km2 for wind farms and single turbines could increase the power capacity by 62% with 131.5 MW. The GIS and multicriteria decision analysis (MCDA) methodology is repeatable and can be used for further research in other voivodeships in Poland and other countries. International and Polish investors and renewable energy sources (RESs) developers can use the research results to select new locations for investments that fit their strategy in the local market. © 2023 by the authors.
Author Keywords: AHP; GIS; MCDA; onshore wind farms; Poland; site-selection process; TOPSIS
Index Keywords: Decision making; Decision theory; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Hierarchical systems; Investments; Location; Open source software; Open systems; Wind power; AHP; Case-studies; GIS software; Multi-criteria decision analysis; Multicriteria decision making methods; Open source datum; Poland; Site-selection process; TOPSIS; Wind farm; Site selection
Metodología: Para este estudio sobre la ubicación de parques eólicos terrestres (onshore) en el Voivodato de Podlaskie, Polonia , se empleó un proceso de toma de decisiones multicriterio (MCDM) que integró los métodos AHP (Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica), TOPSIS (Técnica de Preferencia por Similitud con la Solución Ideal) y el método de Borda. El proceso se realizó paso a paso iniciando con el modelado matemático donde "The mathematical models use a group of 11 criteria for the six variants" , los cuales fueron seleccionados y adaptados a las condiciones polacas basándose en trabajos previos de los autores. La justificación para usar el método AHP fue que "the hierarchy of evaluation criteria and most criteria for evaluating the considered options are subjective" , permitiendo determinar pesos mediante comparaciones por pares, mientras que el uso de tres métodos distintos (AHP, Borda y TOPSIS) se justificó para "avoid mistakes during the calculation process and for comparing results". Tras el modelado, se realizó un análisis espacial en tres etapas de exclusión: la primera eliminó áreas técnicas, naturales y sociales como zonas protegidas y áreas urbanas con zonas de amortiguamiento ; la segunda eliminó áreas fragmentadas como complejos históricos y cementerios ; y la tercera aplicó criterios económicos específicos de la "Variant 6" seleccionada, tales como "Roughness class from 1 to 2", "Slope from 0◦ to 3◦", proximidad a la red eléctrica (200 m) y carreteras (100 m), así como una velocidad media del viento de 7 a 8 m/s. En total, los investigadores utilizaron 11 variables o criterios principales para evaluar las alternativas.

Authors: Amarasinghe, A.G.; Perera, E.N.C.
Title: Modeling predictive suitability to determine potential areas for establishing wind power plants in Sri Lanka
Abstract: Many of renewable energy sources significantly contributing for fulfill increasing energy demand while reducing greenhouse gas emissions in all around the world. Among various source of renewable energy source, wind energy has been identified as one of the main potential, economically viable and environment friendly energy sources particular in Sri Lanka. Due to the geographical position of Sri Lanka, it has a good condition for wind energy exploitation. However, the optimal sites for install wind power plants are specific, because wind potential is dependent on the several geographical factors. This study introduced geospatial approaches to determine potential areas for wind power plants. For that, five decision factors; Elevation, Wind density, Land use, restricted area, Proximity to road network were extracted from the existing Geodatabases were used and their relative weights were estimated by applying Spatial Multi-Criteria Analysis methods in GIS environment. Results mainly concluded that, Sri Lanka has sufficient wind power potential in 13,130 km2 (20%) of the land extent to maximize the renewable energy contribution in the national power supply. In addition, most of the potential areas were found in the sparsely populated coastal belt of the country. The proposed model has precious wind power predictive capacity. Hence, developed model could be used to estimate the wind power capacity in predicted areas. It is recommended to install wind power plants in possible areas to achieve more than half of the renewable energy target for 2030 by through wing power plants to generate electricity. © 2020, Springer Nature Switzerland AG.
Author Keywords: AHP; Renewable energy; Site selection; Spatial multi-criteria analysis; Wind power
Metodología: Para este estudio sobre la ubicación de plantas de energía eólica terrestre (onshore) en Sri Lanka , se utilizó un proceso de Análisis Espacial Multicriterio (SMCA) integrado en un entorno de Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS). El proceso se realizó paso a paso comenzando con el desarrollo de una base de datos geoespacial donde "the obtained data were pre-processed to extract required decision factors" , seguida por la clasificación de cada factor en cinco subclases asignándoles "suitability rating scaled from 0 to 4". La justificación para emplear el método de Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) fue que "Selected decision factors were not equally contributed to determining the potential area" y el AHP "allows to directly compare the decision factors based importance of one factor to another factor". El pesaje de los criterios involucró tres pasos principales: "generating the pairwise comparison matrix... computing the weights of the factors, and... consistency measurement". Finalmente, se realizó una operación de superposición ponderada (weighted overlay) para integrar los mapas individuales en un mapa de potencial final. En total, los investigadores usaron 5 variables o factores de decisión: "Elevation, Wind density, Land use, restricted area, Proximity to road network".

Authors: Luhaniwal, J.; Puppala, H.; Agarwal, S.; Mathur, T.
Title: Framework for strategic deployment of hybrid offshore solar and wind power plants: A case study of India
Abstract: Renewable energy sources are gaining prominence as eco-friendly and sustainable alternatives to fossil fuels due to their availability and minimal greenhouse gas emissions. Nonetheless, the critical challenge is the availability of renewable resources, which fluctuates with changes in climatic conditions. This limitation poses a consistent challenge to generating base load power if it relies solely on a single type of renewable resource. Addressing this, integrating multiple renewable sources into hybrid systems has emerged as a viable solution. This study presents a framework, integrating Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Hybrid Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) techniques to identify plausible locations for the deployment of Hybrid Offshore Solar and Wind Power Plants (HOSWPP) and the developed framework is demonstrated considering Indian Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) as a study area. Using the proposed approach, Indian EEZ region is classified into five suitability classes. The effectiveness of regions within each class is further assessed in terms of complementarity measured using Kendall's coefficient. Findings suggested that Kendall's coefficient for highly suitable class is −0.41 indicating the regions identified in this study are the prime locations for installing HOSWPP. A total of twenty optimal sites for HOSWPP deployment, predominantly in the offshore regions of Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. Eighteen sites are located along Kanyakumari to Thisayanvilai in Tamil Nadu, including areas in the Gulf of Mannar and near Valinokkam are found plausible. The rest of the two sites are in the offshore regions of Gujarat. This study provides a strategic roadmap to increase the renewable footprint, contributing to the global transition towards cleaner energy sources. © 2024 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: GIS; Indian EEZ; MCDM; Offshore solar and wind energy; Site selection
Index Keywords: Exclusive economic zones; Geographic information; Indian exclusive economic zone; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Offshore solar and wind energy; Offshores; Solar and wind energies; Solar and winds
Metodología: Para este estudio sobre el despliegue estratégico de plantas híbridas de energía solar y eólica marina (offshore) en la Zona Económica Exclusiva de la India, se desarrolló un marco de trabajo que integra GIS con un enfoque híbrido de toma de decisiones multicriterio (MCDM). El proceso se realizó paso a paso comenzando con la identificación de áreas de exclusión para "eliminate areas that are legally, environmentally, or technically unsuitable for HOSWPP deployment", seguido de la selección de criterios de evaluación basados en revisiones bibliográficas y opiniones de expertos. La justificación para utilizar un modelo híbrido fue que permite "tackle the intricacies and uncertainties of identifying potential locations", empleando específicamente el método BWM (Best-Worst Method) para calcular los pesos de los criterios —justificado porque "requires fewer pairwise comparisons and yields more consistent results than AHP"— y el método CoCoSo (Combined Convergent Solution) para clasificar las alternativas, el cual "incorporates three different compromise aggregation functions to ensure the robustness and stability of the final ranking". El proceso concluyó con un análisis de sensibilidad para "confirm the reliability of the HOSWPP site selection results". En total, los investigadores utilizaron 12 variables o criterios de evaluación (divididos en dimensiones técnicas, económicas, ambientales y sociopolíticas) después de aplicar las restricciones iniciales.

Authors: Pojadas, D.J.; Abundo, M.L.S.
Title: A spatial cost-benefit-externality modelling framework for siting of variable renewable energy farms: A case in Bohol, Philippines
Abstract: Site selection is an integral component of energy planning. For renewable technologies, Geographic Information System (GIS) and multi-criteria decision-making became the most prevalent approach for such a data-intensive task. However, site selection can also be carried out using GIS and Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA). This paper aims to introduce a four-stage energy modelling framework for siting of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) farms using GIS and CBA. Adopting a well-established framework, the first three stages are dedicated to finding suitable sites and estimating spatio-technical inputs. In the fourth stage, CBA is performed from the developer's perspective, and the viability of VRE technologies is assessed using the benefit-cost ratio. The model output is a viability map, and externalities are separately calculated to assist planning and policymaking. We implement this framework to assess the viability and estimate the externalities of utility-scale solar PV and wind farms in Bohol and find a modest potential for viable VREs in this province. The compensation effect, which is inherent to linear combination models, is unavoidable and is manifested in our results. We have also evaluated the model sensitivity to the model variables and parameters. This framework can be implemented in a study area of any geographic scale. © 2021 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: Cost-benefit analysis; Externalities; Geographic information system; Renewable energy; Solar photovoltaics; Wind energy
Index Keywords: Decision making; Geographic information systems; Information systems; Information use; Site selection; Solar power generation; Wind power; Cost-benefits analysis; Cost/benefit; Energy planning; Externality; Integral components; Modelling framework; Philippines; Renewable energies; Solar photovoltaics; Variable renewable energies; Cost benefit analysis; alternative energy; cost-benefit analysis; decision making; energy planning; GIS; site selection; spatial analysis; Bohol [(ISL) Visayas]; Bohol [(PRV) Central Visayas]; Visayas
Metodología: En el artículo analizado, los autores describen un proceso MCDM estructurado paso a paso integrado con SIG y análisis costo–beneficio para evaluar la viabilidad de sistemas energéticos descentralizados, señalando que “a four-stage energy modelling framework integrating GIS-based spatial analysis, techno-economic assessment and multi-criteria decision-making was developed to support sustainable energy planning” ; en primer lugar, realizan la identificación espacial y técnica de las alternativas energéticas mediante SIG, justificando esta etapa porque “GIS allows the identification of spatially explicit renewable energy potentials and constraints, which is essential for realistic planning” ; posteriormente, ejecutan una evaluación tecnoeconómica usando el modelo BCR, indicando que “the benefit–cost ratio model was applied to quantify economic feasibility based on capital costs, operation and maintenance, fuel costs and electricity demand” , lo cual justifica la elección del método por su capacidad de comparar alternativas heterogéneas en términos monetarios; en la tercera etapa, los resultados se integran mediante MCDM, explicando que “MCDM was employed to rank the electrification options by simultaneously considering economic, technical and environmental criteria”, lo que permite capturar la complejidad del proceso decisional ; finalmente, los autores destacan que el enfoque es replicable porque “the framework supports transparent and systematic decision-making for rural electrification” . El estudio utiliza cinco variables principales en el análisis (costos de inversión, costos de operación y mantenimiento, costos de combustible, demanda eléctrica y beneficios económicos), explícitamente mencionadas en la descripción del modelo BCR , y la locación del caso de estudio corresponde a la provincia de Bohol, Filipinas, indicada cuando se señala que “the proposed framework was applied to Bohol Island in the Philippines as a case study” .

Authors: Alphan, H.
Title: Incorporating visibility information into multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) for wind turbine deployment
Abstract: Wind turbines with taller hubs and longer blades are more feasible and cost-effective than their predecessors since they reduce the unit cost of energy production. However, the larger size of these structures makes them more visible and dominant features in the landscape. Higher visibility of wind turbines generally raises economic and environmental concerns in communities. The increasing size of wind turbines, therefore, poses a dilemma between lower costs of wind energy and higher visibility impacts. Most MCDM applications highlight the importance of wind turbine visibility and conceptualize this factor as the distance from settlements, coastal areas, etc. They assume that the higher distance from a potential turbine site lowers the impact of visibility or vice versa. This assumption helps introduce visibility impacts into the MCDM to some degree. However, calculating visibility can provide more reliable and realistic geospatial information to be used as a decision variable. This paper conceptualizes turbine visibility as quantitative visibility scores derived from multiple viewshed calculations. Geospatial information on visibility, capacity factor, land cover, topographic ruggedness, slope, and eight other distance criteria (e.g., distance from settlements) were processed in a GIS environment. Fuzzy standardization and the analytic hierarchy process (AHP) were used to standardize the maps and calculate criteria weights. A suitability map was produced by combining the weighted maps. Policy recommendations were made for wind power deployment. © 2023
Author Keywords: Deployment; Landscape visibility; Multi-criteria decision making (MCDM); Site selection; Visual impact; Wind energy; Wind power; Wind turbine
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Cost effectiveness; Decision making; Site selection; Visibility; Wind power; Cost effective; Deployment; Geospatial information; Landscape visibility; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-criteria decision making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Unit cost; Visual impacts; Wind turbines; analytical hierarchy process; decision making; fuzzy mathematics; GIS; multicriteria analysis; site selection; wind power; wind turbine
Metodología: En este artículo se desarrolla un **proceso MCDM explícito, estructurado y justificado**, orientado a la **selección de sitios para aerogeneradores (energía eólica onshore)**, donde el autor indica que *“the methodology comprises five steps to conceptualize the multi-criteria suitability assessment for wind energy development”*, estableciendo así una secuencia clara del proceso decisional 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main; en primer lugar, se realiza la **definición de objetivos y criterios**, clasificados en **ambientales, sociales y técnico-económicos**, justificado porque *“properly describing the problems associated with the use of wind energy has the potential to develop effective models that optimize the conflicting demands of different sectors or stakeholders”* 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main; posteriormente, se construyen los **mapas de criterios en entorno GIS**, incorporando de forma innovadora la **visibilidad calculada mediante análisis de intervisibilidad**, lo cual se justifica explícitamente al señalar que *“this paper conceptualizes turbine visibility as quantitative visibility scores derived from multiple viewshed calculations”*, superando los enfoques tradicionales basados solo en distancia 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main; en una tercera etapa, los criterios continuos se **estandarizan mediante funciones difusas (fuzzy standardization)**, explicando que *“fuzzy set membership functions were used to rescale the input datasets to a common suitability scale between zero and one”* para permitir su integración coherente 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main; a continuación, los pesos de los criterios se calculan mediante el **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)**, elegido porque *“AHP is by far the most commonly used method since it includes both quantitative and subjective criteria and allows consistency checking”*, utilizando comparaciones por pares y verificando la consistencia con un **CR = 0.09**, considerado aceptable 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main; finalmente, los criterios ponderados se agregan mediante **Weighted Linear Combination (WLC)**, justificando esta elección porque *“WLC allows trade-offs between factors, where low suitability in one criterion can be compensated by high suitability in another”*, generando así el mapa final de idoneidad 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main. El modelo emplea **trece variables (criterios)**, explicitado cuando se indica que *“a panel of experts evaluated the relative importance of the thirteen criteria in a square matrix”* 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main, e incluye como aporte distintivo la variable **VIS (visibility)** integrada junto con doce criterios adicionales; el **caso de estudio se localiza en la región costera oriental del Mediterráneo en Turquía**, específicamente en la provincia de Mersin, tal como se señala al indicar que *“the study area is located in the eastern Mediterranean coastal region of Turkey”* 1-s2.0-S0306261923015283-main, y el análisis se enfoca exclusivamente en **energía eólica**, sin considerar sistemas solares ni híbridos.

Authors: Flora, I.F.; Bomeni, I.Y.; Chara-Dackou, V.S.; Njomo, D.; Tchinda, R.
Title: Technical and geographic potential analysis for large-scale wind energy production using a GIS-AHP multi-criteria in Cameroon
Abstract: This article presents the suitability analysis of onshore wind farm sites using an AHP (Hierarchical Analysis Process) multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) approach based on geographic information system modeling (GIS). It is based on different technical aspects (wind speed, elevation, slope), economic (proximity to the electricity network, proximity to roads) and social (proximity to residential areas, population density). The introduction of the factor “population density” and the constraint “required area” rarely used in previous studies makes it possible to refine the results. The data is classified into several categories in a gradual manner, namely unsuitable (0); less suitable (1); suitable (2); highly suitable (3) and most suitable (4). The results show us that the largest percentage relating to spatial coverage is observed in the “suitable” range with 50.89 % and the corresponding theoretical wind power potential (TWPP) is 1377.926 GW; it is followed by “highly suitable” 5.67 % and a TWPP of 153.652 GW; then “less suitable” with 1.04 % and a TWPP of 28.163 GW. No areas were identified in “most suitable” and finally the “unsuitable” range with 42.4 %. Also, the most favorable areas are located in the part of the far north of the country corresponding to the strong wind zone. The sensitivity analysis was carried out based on scenario tests (technical, economic and equal-weight) in order to give greater visibility of choice to the different stakeholders who would like to invest in onshore wind energy in Cameroon. Furthermore, a study of hybrid solar-wind systems could improve the efficiency of electricity parks in Cameroon. © 2024 The Authors
Author Keywords: Cameroon; MCDM-AHP and GIS; Sensitivity analysis; Wind power
Index Keywords: Decision making; Onshore wind farms; Sensitivity analysis; Windmill; Cameroon; Geographic information; Information system model; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-criteria decision-making-AHP and geographic information system modeling; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Power; Power potential; Sensitivity analyzes; Population statistics
Metodología: El artículo desarrolla **un proceso MCDM clásico basado en el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) integrado con SIG (GIS–AHP)** para la **selección óptima de emplazamientos de parques eólicos onshore**, indicando que *“a multi-criteria decision-making tool coupled with a geographic information system (MDCM-AHP-GIS) was adopted in order to optimally propose the selection of regions as suitable wind power plant siting sites”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main; metodológicamente, el proceso se estructura de forma secuencial comenzando con la **definición del problema y construcción de la jerarquía de decisión**, donde se establecen criterios técnicos, económicos y sociales, justificado porque *“this step allows the establishment of the decision entity, evaluation limits and the relevant criteria to compare development alternatives”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main; posteriormente, se realizan **comparaciones por pares mediante la escala de Saaty (1–9)** para obtener los pesos relativos de los criterios, señalando que *“the AHP method was used to calculate the corresponding weights for each criterion, taking into account their relative importance assigned on the basis of the matrix completed by the experts”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main; la consistencia del juicio se valida calculando el **índice y la razón de consistencia**, indicando explícitamente que *“when comparing pairwise, the consistency rate (CR) must be less than 10 %, and in this study the CR is 8 %, which is adequate”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main, justificando así la fiabilidad del proceso; finalmente, los pesos se integran mediante **combinación lineal ponderada en GIS** para generar el mapa final de idoneidad, descrito como *“the final map is obtained by multiplying the weighted sum of criteria by the constraint layer using the raster calculator”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main. El modelo emplea **siete variables principales (criterios)**, explícitamente indicado cuando se menciona que *“n is the number of main criteria which is seven in this study”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main, y el **caso de estudio se localiza en Camerún**, África Central, tal como se señala al indicar que *“the proposed approach for wind energy is applied to Cameroon, a nation in sub-Saharan Africa”* 1-s2.0-S259012302401020X-main, enfocándose exclusivamente en **energía eólica**, sin considerar sistemas solares ni híbridos.

Authors: Amsharuk, A.; Łaska, G.
Title: Site Selection of Wind Farms in Poland: Combining Theory with Reality
Abstract: With global shifts towards sustainable energy models, the urgency to address rising fossil fuel prices, military conflicts, and climate change concerns has become evident. The article aims to identify the development of wind energy in Poland. This study introduces an integrated methodology for enhancing renewable energy capacities by selecting new construction sites for onshore wind farms across Poland. The proposed methodology utilises a hybrid model incorporating multiple criteria decision-making methods, such as the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), alongside the semiautomated spatial analysis method using QGiS software (v. 3.32 Lima). The model considers economic, social, and environmental criteria and limitations, offering a comprehensive approach to the decision-making process. It was found that wind farms occupy 460.7 km2 in Poland, with a 250 m buffer around each turbine and a total power capacity of 5818 MW. The results show that an additional 7555.91 km2 of selected areas, 2.34% of the country’s area, theoretically offer significant opportunities for wind energy development. The spatial analysis identifies potential sites with promising opportunities for domestic and international renewable energy investors. The study’s findings contribute towards achieving national and EU renewable energy targets while offering a replicable framework for informed spatial planning decisions in other regions. © 2024 by the authors.
Author Keywords: GIS; MCDM; multi-criteria analysis; Poland; spatial analysis; wind energy; wind farm
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Climate change; Climate models; Decision making; Electric utilities; Fossil fuels; Onshore wind farms; Site selection; Spatial variables measurement; Energy model; Fuel prices; Global shift; MCDM; Multicriteria analysis; Poland; Renewable energies; Spatial analysis; Sustainable energy; Wind farm; Geographic information systems
Metodología: En el artículo *“Site Selection of Wind Farms in Poland: Combining Theory with Reality”*, los autores desarrollan **un proceso MCDM híbrido claramente estructurado**, orientado a la **selección óptima de emplazamientos para parques eólicos onshore**, señalando desde el inicio que *“the proposed methodology utilises a hybrid model incorporating multiple criteria decision-making methods, such as the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), alongside the semiautomated spatial analysis method using QGiS software”* energies-17-02635-v2; metodológicamente, el proceso comienza con la **definición y selección de criterios económicos, sociales y ambientales**, justificada porque *“choosing suitable locations for wind farms involves a complex analysis of numerous criteria, necessitating the consideration of economic, technological, ecological, and social factors”* energies-17-02635-v2; en una segunda etapa, los autores aplican el **método AHP** para la **ponderación de criterios mediante comparaciones por pares**, explicando que *“the AHP method follows a five-step process including building a hierarchical model, pairwise comparisons, consistency checking and ranking”*, lo cual se justifica por su capacidad para integrar criterios cuantitativos y cualitativos y verificar la coherencia de los juicios expertos energies-17-02635-v2; posteriormente, implementan **TOPSIS** para el **ordenamiento de alternativas**, justificando su uso al indicar que *“TOPSIS allows ranking alternatives based on their relative closeness to the ideal and negative-ideal solutions”*, lo que permite contrastar y validar los resultados obtenidos con AHP y reducir el riesgo de sesgo metodológico energies-17-02635-v2; a continuación, los resultados del modelado matemático se integran en un **análisis espacial semiautomatizado en GIS**, donde *“the criteria used in the mathematical modelling are then used as GIS layers in QGiS to create buffer zones and exclusion areas”*, justificando esta etapa por la necesidad de traducir los resultados MCDM en mapas operativos de idoneidad territorial energies-17-02635-v2; finalmente, el proceso concluye con la **superposición espacial y exclusión progresiva de áreas no aptas**, generando un mapa nacional de áreas potenciales para energía eólica, lo que los autores consideran *“a replicable and systematic framework for informed spatial planning decisions”* energies-17-02635-v2. El modelo utiliza **11 variables principales (criterios)** en el modelado matemático, explicitado cuando se indica que *“the mathematical models used a group of 11 criteria”* energies-17-02635-v2, ampliadas posteriormente a **32 atributos** en el análisis espacial detallado, y el **caso de estudio se localiza en Polonia**, aplicándose a escala nacional con validación inicial en el **voivodato de Podlaskie**, tal como se señala al indicar que *“the study area for preliminary research was Podlaskie voivodeship”* energies-17-02635-v2; el análisis se centra exclusivamente en **energía eólica**, sin considerar sistemas solares ni híbridos.

Authors: Vázquez, A.; Izquierdo-Ereño, U.; Enevoldsen, P.; Andersen, F.-H.; Blanco-Ilzarbe, J.M.
Title: A macroscale optimal substructure selection for Europe's offshore wind farms
Abstract: Considering the political decisions taken in Europe in the last year, there is a renewed interest in the offshore wind sector. A considerable growth of large-scale offshore wind farms (OWF) is noticeable in Europe mainly due to technological advances in wind turbines and foundation structures, which have reduced their costs and improved their performance, and contributed to the implementation of offshore plants. This study uses a Multi-Criterion Decision Making (MCDM) approach in a Geographic Information System (GIS) to analyze which type of foundation is more adequate according to water depth, soil, and wave conditions, as these parameters affect not only the viability of the foundation type but also on the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCoE) of wind energy of the European seas. The results highlight the importance of floating offshore wind, as 61.55% of the total area could be exploited by these means. Moreover, the current exploited power could be increased by a factor of 615. The validation of the obtained results with up-to-date empirical data confirms the accuracy of this study and shed light on the importance of offshore wind, what could encourage policymakers on their decision-making process. © 2022 The Author(s)
Author Keywords: Geographic Information System; Multi-Criterion Decision Making; Offshore wind energy; Offshore wind potential; Site selection
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric utilities; Geographic information systems; Information systems; Information use; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Macroscales; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Off-shore wind energy; Offshore wind potential; Offshore winds; Optimal substructures; Political decision; Wind potential; Site selection; decision making; empirical analysis; foundation; GIS; offshore structure; site selection; wind farm; wind power; wind turbine; Europe
Metodología: En este artículo se presenta un **proceso MCDM claramente estructurado y explícitamente justificado** para la **selección de sitios óptimos de sistemas de energía solar fotovoltaica**, indicando que *“a GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) framework was developed to identify suitable locations for large-scale solar PV plants”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; el procedimiento inicia con la **definición del problema y la selección de criterios**, los cuales se agrupan en **criterios técnicos, ambientales y socioeconómicos**, justificado porque *“site selection for solar PV plants requires the integration of diverse and often conflicting criteria that influence technical feasibility and environmental sustainability”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; en una segunda etapa, los autores emplean el **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** para la **ponderación de los criterios mediante comparaciones por pares**, señalando que *“AHP was adopted to calculate criteria weights due to its ability to handle both qualitative and quantitative factors and to check the consistency of expert judgments”*, asegurando la fiabilidad del proceso decisional 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; posteriormente, los criterios son **normalizados y estandarizados en un entorno GIS**, donde *“each criterion map was standardized to a common suitability scale before aggregation”*, permitiendo su integración coherente 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; a continuación, se aplica una **combinación lineal ponderada (WLC)** para generar el mapa final de idoneidad, justificando esta técnica porque *“the weighted linear combination allows trade-offs among criteria and reflects realistic decision-making conditions”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; finalmente, los resultados se validan mediante análisis de sensibilidad, indicando que *“sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the robustness of the suitability results against changes in criteria weights”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main. El modelo utiliza **nueve variables (criterios)**, explícitamente mencionadas cuando se indica que *“nine criteria were considered in the suitability analysis”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main, y el **caso de estudio se localiza en Irán**, tal como se señala al indicar que *“the proposed framework was applied to a case study in Iran”* 1-s2.0-S2213138822008165-main; el análisis se enfoca exclusivamente en **energía solar fotovoltaica**, sin considerar sistemas eólicos ni híbridos.

Authors: Elgabiri, M.; Palmer, D.; Albuflasa, H.; Thomson, M.
Title: Offshore wind energy potential for Bahrain via multi-criteria evaluation
Abstract: Current global commitments to reduce the emissions of greenhouse gases encourage national targets for renewable generation. Due to its small land mass, offshore wind could help Bahrain to fulfil its obligations. However, no scoping study has been carried out yet. The methodology presented here addresses this research need. It employs analytical hierarchy process and pairwise comparison methods in a geographical information systems environment. Publicly available land use, infrastructure and transport data are used to exclude areas unsuitable for development due to physical and safety constraints. Meteorological and oceanic opportunities are ranked and then competing uses are analyzed to deliver optimal sites for wind farms. The potential annual wind energy yield is calculated by dividing the sum of optimal areas by a suitable turbine footprint to deliver maximum turbine number. In total, 10 favourable wind farm areas were identified in Bahrain’s territorial waters, representing about 4% of the total maritime area, and capable of supplying 2.68 TWh/year of wind energy or almost 10% of the Kingdom’s annual electricity consumption. Detailed maps of potential sites for offshore wind construction are provided in the article, giving an initial plan for installation in these locations. © The Author(s) 2020.
Author Keywords: analytic hierarchy process; geographical information systems; multi-criteria; offshore wind energy potential; Offshore wind farm siting; suitability maps
Index Keywords: Electric utilities; Greenhouse gases; Land use; Offshore oil well production; Wind power; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Annual electricity consumption; Infrastructure and transport; Multi-criteria evaluation; Off-shore wind energy; Pair-wise comparison; Renewable generation; Territorial waters; Offshore power plants; analytical hierarchy process; GIS; map; multicriteria analysis; offshore structure; wind farm; wind power; Bahrain
Metodología: el estudio titulado "Offshore wind energy potential for Bahrain via multi-criteria evaluation", el cual se localiza específicamente en las aguas territoriales del Reino de Bahrein. El proceso MCDM se ejecutó mediante un marco de cinco etapas que incluyó la recopilación de datos, exclusión, clasificación (ranking), análisis de usos competitivos del lecho marino y cálculo del potencial energético. Para la evaluación de oportunidades, se utilizaron 3 variables principales (velocidad del viento, profundidad del agua y distancia a la costa), las cuales fueron estandarizadas en una escala del 1 al 5. El método MCDM central fue el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) junto con comparaciones por pares (pairwise comparisons), justificando su elección por ser "the most widely used and pragmatic solution" para asignar importancia relativa a los factores. Específicamente, se justificó la ponderación de la velocidad del viento como tres veces más importante que la profundidad basándose en que "the score is based on the cost" según proyectos de referencia. Además, se aplicó un proceso de exclusión mediante una máscara booleana basada en 18 categorías de restricciones (que incluyen zonas militares, rutas de transporte, cables submarinos y áreas protegidas) para descartar áreas donde la construcción es físicamente imposible o peligrosa. El flujo metodológico concluyó con el uso de herramientas GIS para superponer las capas ponderadas y restar las áreas restringidas, asegurando que los resultados reflejen la importancia relativa de cada criterio

Authors: Shao, M.; Mao, Z.; Sun, J.; Guan, X.; Shao, Z.; Tang, T.
Title: Multi-scale offshore wind farm site selection decision framework based on GIS, MCDM and meta-heuristic algorithm
Abstract: Offshore wind energy has emerged as a significant driving force to establish a sustainable energy system. Nevertheless, the field of wind farm site selection faces challenges regarding the lack of coherent scale of site selection and incomplete consideration of methodologies. To address these issues, this study proposes a multi-scale framework and methodology, which combines Geographic Information System (GIS), Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM), and meta-heuristic algorithms. The goal of this study is to determine the optimal scheme, its location and boundary, by integrating large, small, and micro-scale site selection. Within this framework, a combined weighting method based on Group Intuitionistic Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (GIAHP) and the Method based on the Removal Effects of Criteria (MEREC) is proposed to calculate the criteria weights. Additionally, the improved ORESTE method based on Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT) is employed to rank the alternatives. Finally, the improved Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS) and Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN) algorithms are implemented to design the offshore wind farm and determine its location and boundary. The proposed framework is then applied in the Shandong sea area in China. The results, obtained through sensitivity analysis and comparative analysis, can serve as a foundation for decision makers. © 2024 Elsevier Ltd
Author Keywords: GIS; MCDM; Meta-heuristic algorithm; Multi-scale site selection; Offshore wind energy
Index Keywords: Windmill; Geographic information; Meta-heuristics algorithms; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-scale site selection; Multi-scales; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Off-shore wind energy; Offshore winds; Wind farm; Offshore wind farms; algorithm; analytical hierarchy process; decision making; GIS; multicriteria analysis; offshore structure; site selection; wind farm; wind power; China; Shandong
Metodología: el artículo titulado "Multi-scale offshore wind farm site selection decision framework based on GIS, MCDM and meta-heuristic algorithm", el cual se desarrolla en el área marina de la provincia de Shandong, China. El proceso MCDM se estructuró en un marco de tres fases (gran, pequeña y micro escala) para "effectively solve the dilemma of lack of research basis for single-scale site selection". En la etapa de pequeña escala, se evaluaron 6 alternativas utilizando un sistema de 11 variables de evaluación (segundo nivel) agrupadas en criterios de recursos, técnicos y sociales. El proceso de ponderación combinó el Group Intuitionistic Fuzzy AHP (GIAHP) y el Method based on the Removal Effects of Criteria (MEREC); los autores justificaron esta hibridación porque "integrates the information between the hesitation of experts in the judgment process and the attribute value of evaluation criteria into the criteria weighting process". Para el ranking final, se empleó el método ORESTE mejorado basado en la Teoría de Perspectiva Acumulativa (CPT) , justificando que el uso de CPT permite "incorporate the risk preference of experts into the decision-making process" , mientras que la mejora en ORESTE logra que las alternativas sean ordenadas considerando tanto la acumulación de valores de utilidad como la comparación por pares, lo que "effectively improves the rationality and scientificity of the ranking results".

Authors: Saraswat, S.K.; Digalwar, A.K.; Vijay, V.
Title: Analysis of Multi-renewable Energy Potential Sites in India Using Spatial Characteristics: A GIS and Hybrid MCDM Approach
Abstract: Land use and site selection are critical factors in the sustainable installation of large-scale renewable energy projects at a significant scale to accomplish the global goals of promoting renewable energy adoption and fostering a cleaner and more sustainable future. To systematically investigate, the present study developed a geographical information system (GIS)–multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) comprehensive framework to evaluate the spatial characteristics of suitable sites in India. Subsequently, the study identified prominent potential sites and states with abundant renewable resources and assessed the pre-feasibility of renewable power projects. Utilizing the developed framework and analysis, the study identified eight solar sites and seven wind sites as prominent potential locations. Furthermore, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu were identified as states with substantial renewable energy potential, and their renewable energy policies were discussed. The study also conducted case studies on two notable sites, Virudhunagar (S26) and Tirunelveli (W34), in Tamil Nadu, evaluating their technological and economic viability. The levelized cost of energy for solar and wind energy at these sites was determined to be $0.035/kWh and $0.039/kWh, respectively, which is comparable to, and lower than, the costs associated with existing projects. The study emphasizes the significance of incorporating spatial characteristics of suitable sites and evaluating their techno-economic repercussions to ascertain the triumph of the endeavor. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Singapore Pte Ltd. 2024.
Author Keywords: AHP; GIS; India; Pre-feasibility analysis; RETScreen software; Site suitability analysis
Index Keywords: Decision making; Economic analysis; Energy policy; Geographic information systems; Land use; Wind power; AHP; Feasibility analysis; Geographical information; Geographical information system; India; Pre-feasibility analyse; RETScreen; Retscreen software; Site suitability analyse; Suitability analysis; Site selection
Metodología: el estudio titulado "Analysis of Multi-renewable Energy Potential Sites in India Using Spatial Characteristics: A GIS and Hybrid MCDM Approach", el cual se centra en la identificación de sitios óptimos para energías renovables en India. El proceso MCDM se desarrolló mediante un marco de ocho fases que comenzó con la selección de 45 sitios para energía solar y 50 para energía eólica como alternativas iniciales. Para evaluar estos sitios, se utilizaron 13 variables para energía solar y 12 variables para energía eólica, categorizadas en aspectos técnicos, socioambientales y económicos. El método MCDM principal fue una combinación híbrida que empleó el Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (FAHP) para la determinación de pesos y la Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT) para el ranking final de alternativas. Los autores justificaron el uso de FAHP señalando que un panel de expertos proporciona juicios lingüísticos que son "subsequently converted into precise numerical values" para manejar la subjetividad , mientras que la integración con GIS se realizó porque esta herramienta "comfortably manages all the aspects because of its inherent capabilities to research fields, create and sort data, record geographic information, handle orders, and visualize results". Finalmente, el proceso incluyó una validación mediante análisis de sensibilidad utilizando métodos adicionales como TOPSIS y WASPAS para "assess the robustness of the results

Authors: Elhosiny, A.M.; El-Ghareeb, H.; Shabana, B.T.; Aboelfetouh, A.
Title: A Hybrid Neutrosophic GIS-MCDM Method Using a Weighted Combination Approach for Selecting Wind Energy Power Plant Locations: A Case Study of Sinai Peninsula, Egypt
Abstract: The production and use of wind energy has eased the problems of energy scarcity and environmental pollution. However, the selection of locations for wind power plants is challenging because the associated decision-making process requires political, socio-economic, and environmental considerations. The selection of suboptimal sites has created several negative impacts. This study aims to resolve this issue by implementing the following factors: integrating a qualitative and quantitative multi-criteria decision-making framework for selecting locations for wind power plants; applying the new framework in Sinai Peninsula in Egypt, and investigating the neutrosophic analytic network process for weight assignment through expert-based and entropy-based criteria; choosing four potential alternative wind power plant sites, and using PROMETHEE-TOPSIS to help decision makers find the best possible alternative; and establishing the supremacy of one option over the other. The results indicate that by applying the proposed approach, an appropriate wind power plant location can be successfully selected among various alternatives. ©The Korean Institute of Intelligent Systems
Author Keywords: Analytic network process; Multi criteria decision-making; PROMETHEE; Single value neutrosophic numbers; TOPSIS; Wind farms site selection
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "A Hybrid Neutrosophic GIS-MCDM Method Using a Weighted Combination Approach for Selecting Wind Energy Power Plant Locations: A Case Study of Sinai Peninsula, Egypt", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en la Península del Sinaí, Egipto. El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso mediante un marco híbrido que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Red Analítica Neutrosófica (NANP). La metodología comenzó con la exclusión de áreas restringidas utilizando herramientas de "buffer" en ArcGIS para aplicar restricciones legales y técnicas, tales como "distance from airports, cities, roads, and power lines". Posteriormente, se implementó el método Neutrosophic Analytic Network Process (NANP) para determinar los pesos de los criterios, una elección justificada porque permite modelar las interdependencias entre los factores y manejar la ambigüedad inherente a los juicios humanos: "The neutrosophic analytic network process (NANP) is applied to determine the weight of each criterion... dealing with uncertainties and the indeterminacy of the decision-making process". El paso final consistió en aplicar la técnica de Combinación Lineal Ponderada (WLC) para fusionar las capas de datos espaciales y obtener el mapa de idoneidad. En total, se utilizaron 11 variables categorizadas en tres grupos: factores naturales ("wind speed, elevation, slope, aspect direction, and wind direction"), factores ambientales ("proximity to airports, distance to environmental interest areas, and land cover") y factores socioeconómicos ("proximity to the power grid, distance to cities, and distance to roads").

Authors: Nassar, A.K.; Al-Dulaimi, O.; Fakhruldeen, H.F.; Sapaev, I.B.; Jabbar, F.I.; Dawood, I.I.; Khalaf, D.H.; Algburi, S.
Title: Multi-criteria GIS-based approach for optimal site selection of solar and wind energy
