Abstract: This research introduces a comprehensive multi-criteria geographic information system-based approach designed to determine optimal locations for solar and wind energy development by integrating geographic information system, multi-criteria decision analysis, and the analytical hierarchy process. The methodology enables spatial analysis that considers a wide range of environmental, economic, and technical factors, producing suitability maps that prioritize locations for renewable energy infrastructure. Applied to the geographic and climatic context of Iraq, the approach addresses the countries’ growing energy demands and infrastructure constraints by identifying regions with high renewable energy potential. the analysis resulted in 38.33 % of the total study area being suitable for utility-scale solar photovoltaic installations, 12.44 % for large-scale wind farms, and 34.47 % for small-scale solar photovoltaic projects. site selection criteria included solar irradiance, wind speed, terrain slope, ambient temperature, land use, elevation, and proximity to infrastructure such as roads and power lines. the study demonstrates the critical role of spatial decision-making tools in promoting sustainable energy strategies, especially within developing countries experiencing energy deficits and environmental pressures. © 2025 The Authors
Author Keywords: Multi-criteria geographic information system-based approach; Renewable energy; Site selection; Solar and wind energy; Sustainability planning
Index Keywords: alternative energy; energy planning; GIS; site selection; solar power; spatial analysis; sustainability; wind power; Iraq
Metodología: el estudio titulado "Multi-criteria GIS-based approach for optimal site selection of solar and wind energy", el cual se desarrolla en Irak1111. El proceso MCDM se fundamentó en un marco de trabajo que integró Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) y el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) para evaluar la idoneidad de sitios en tres escenarios: energía solar fotovoltaica (PV) a gran escala, eólica a gran escala y solar PV a pequeña escala2222. El procedimiento comenzó con la identificación y estandarización de criterios, seguida de la asignación de pesos mediante matrices de comparación por pares de $8 \times 8$ para cada escenario, donde expertos utilizaron una escala lineal del 1 al 93333333333333333. Los autores justificaron el uso de AHP al señalar que ofrece "robust frameworks to holistically evaluate and rank the multifaceted criteria" 4y permite estructurar el problema en una jerarquía que incluye evaluaciones subjetivas de profesionales y académicos para "enhance the pairwise assessments"5. Se utilizaron 8 variables principales por escenario (incluyendo irradiación solar, velocidad del viento, temperatura ambiente, pendiente del terreno, elevación y proximidad a infraestructura como carreteras y líneas eléctricas), cuya importancia relativa se validó mediante un análisis de consistencia, asegurando que el Consistency Ratio (CR) fuera $\leq 0.10$ para garantizar la racionalidad de los juicios6666666666666666. Finalmente, se aplicó una herramienta de superposición de suma ponderada (weighted sum overlay) en ArcGIS para amalgamar los mapas de criterios y "evaluating every pixel in accordance with its weighted significance"7. Este proceso se complementó con la eliminación de barreras y restricciones (como zonas protegidas, áreas agrícolas y territorios en disputa) para asegurar que los proyectos se ubiquen en las localizaciones "most suitable and least contentious"

Authors: Yegizaw, E.S.; Mengistu, D.A.
Title: Multi-criteria decision analysis for wind farm location selection in Bahir Dar City and its surroundings, Northwestern Ethiopia
Abstract: While there is a consensus on the multifaceted advantages of wind farms, only a handful of developing countries harness it to the fullest potential. Among the various factors that contributed to the low development of wind farms in those countries, the dearth of methods for identifying a suitable site is the leading one. Studies conducted elsewhere on wind farm industry identifies criteria such as slope, wind speed, land use/land cover, geology, distance from electric transmission grids, roads, and settlements. However, the relative magnitude of each factor to the optimum location of the wind farm is inadequately examined. This study focuses on the combined use of geographic information systems (GIS) and spatial multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) for selecting the most appropriate sites for wind-farm development. Using a pairwise comparison method of the analytical hierarchical process, we computed the relative importance and weight of parameters affecting wind farm sites in a weighted overlay technique. Based on the analysis of pertinent data collected from Bahir Dar City and its surroundings, northwestern Ethiopia, we categorized the study area into five suitability classes ranging from extremely suitable to not suitable. Our result, revealed a spatial pattern of the potential sites suitable for wind farm investments that are economical, environmentally sound, and aesthetically amenable. We recommend replicating the method developed in this study in other areas of developing countries where transitioning to renewable energy sources through windfarm development is sought. © 2023, The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG.
Author Keywords: AHP; Ethiopia; Renewable energy; Site selection; Wind energy

Metodología: el estudio titulado "Multi-criteria decision analysis for wind farm location selection in Bahir Dar City and its surroundings, Northwestern Ethiopia", el cual se sitúa geográficamente en la ciudad de Bahir Dar y sus alrededores, en el noroeste de Etiopía. El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo mediante un enfoque de análisis de decisión multicriterio espacial (SMCDA) que comenzó con la identificación de 8 variables de evaluación (velocidad del viento, pendiente, uso/cobertura del suelo, geología y distancias a la red eléctrica, carreteras, asentamientos y aeropuertos). El método MCDM utilizado fue el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) mediante el "pairwise comparison method", justificando su uso porque "is a widely used and well-accepted method for weighting criteria in MCDA" y permite a los tomadores de decisiones "compare each pair of criteria to determine their relative importance". El proceso siguió un flujo de tres etapas: primero, la creación de una base de datos geoespacial y la reclasificación de criterios en capas de "suitability" y "constraint"; segundo, el cálculo de pesos mediante la matriz de comparación por pares, donde se justificó la prioridad de la velocidad del viento (38.4%) indicando que "the wind speed is the most critical factor in determining the energy production potential"; y tercero, la agregación mediante la técnica de Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) para generar el mapa final de aptitud. Los autores subrayaron que el uso de GIS fue esencial ya que "provides the tools for managing, analyzing, and visualizing spatial data", permitiendo transformar datos complejos en zonas clasificadas desde "unsuitable" hasta "highly suitable" para el desarrollo de granjas eólicas.

Authors: Zubir, M.A.M.; Jaafar Ibrahim, S.A.; Abdul Rasam, A.R.A.; Yusoff, Z.M.; Hashim, I.C.
Title: IDENTIFYING THE OPTIMAL PLACEMENT OF SPATIAL WIND ENERGY FARMS IN SELANGOR, MALAYSIA
Abstract: Electrical energy is indispensable for the economic growth and well-being of humanity. However, there is a growing concern with the conventional method of generating electricity which is by burning coal and other fossil fuels. In the coming decades, the world will experience a transition to cleaner energy, away from the use of fossil fuels. One of the clean and renewable energies that is getting more popular and advancing in technology is wind energy. Wind energy requires a proper location to operate in order to be profitable and have the desired impact on society. In search of a suitable location for wind energy, a Geographical Information System based Multi-Criteria Decision Making (GIS-MCDM) approach was utilized in this paper to explore the suitable areas for wind energy development in Selangor. Eight parameters were chosen to address this concern, including terrain elevation, terrain slope, road network, airport locations, forests, settlements, water bodies and average wind speed. All of these criteria were weighted using the pairwise comparison approach in MCDM, and the decisionmaking process was facilitated by the criteria utilizing the ArcGIS-weighted overlay tool. The study has identified seven potential sites, but only four sites that are practically located the wind energy site, which are Banting, Jeram, Ijok and Kerling. GIS-MCDM can assist the decision-maker in designing the optimal placement of wind farms in real scenarios. © 2022 by MIP.
Author Keywords: GIS; MCDM; Site Selection; Suitability Index Map; Wind Energy
MCDM method: proporcion de apariciones de cada variable
Metodología: The first step is the preliminary study in identifying the criteria and sub-criteria in locating the suitable location for a wind farm. Next is the geospatial data collections such as the DEM, wind speed data, land use map, and road networks. The third step is the data processing, which was carried out to map all the datasets and classify them according to the suitable criteria for wind energy generation. The weightage (pairwise comparison method) is decided based on previous study as mentioned on literature review.

Authors: Luhaniwal, J.; Agarwal, S.; Mathur, T.
Title: Assessment of offshore wind farm site suitability in India using GIS and MCDM methods
Abstract: The growing demand for sustainable energy sources has prompted investigation of offshore wind farms (OfWF) as viable options. This study proposes an innovative framework combining GIS with MCDM methods to identify highly suitable sites and efficiently prioritize alternative sites for OfWF, specifically focusing on regions viable for fixed foundations within the Indian EEZ. It takes into account technical, economic, and socio-environmental criteria to identify the optimal sites for OfWF. Two types of criteria are involved: exclusion criteria, refining study area by excluding areas unsuitable for renewable energy projects, and evaluation criteria, influencing the viability of specific sites in line with defined objective. The methodology involves screening criteria from existing literature, preprocessing data through GIS, and calculating criteria weights with AHP and CRITIC methods. This is followed by ranking potential sites using TOPSIS and VIKOR, offering structured decision support. The final phase of the analysis involved evaluating the consistency between individual site rankings using the Spearman rank correlation, ensuring reliability in the ranking outcomes across selected methods. The findings suggest the top fifteen sites for installing wind farms, ranging in offshore regions from Pallam town in Kanyakumari to Kooduthalai in Tirunelveli district, Tamil Nadu. This study’s insights on offshore renewable energy site selection in India are invaluable for policymakers, investors and sustainability initiatives, offering a roadmap for a cleaner and more sustainable energy future worldwide. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: GIS; Indian EEZ; MCDM; Offshore wind farm; Site selection

Authors: Sapkota, K.; Shabbiruddin, n.; Sherpa, K.S.
Title: Wind farm site suitability assessment & validation using geospatially explicit multi-criteria approach: A case study of South Sikkim, India
Abstract: Determination of optimal location for wind farm is a challenging task influenced by several decision criteria and factors subject to human. This case study done proposes an integrated decision support framework to investigate the spatial wind farm suitability based on F-AHP (Fuzzy-Analytical Hierarchy Process) together with Q-GIS (Quantum-Geographic Information System) tool. Firstly, spatial analysis and integration of nine-exclusion factors were included to identify the unsuitable sites. Next, F-AHP-based MCDM (Multi Criteria Decision Making) analysis was employed to obtain criteria weights of seven evaluation factors. Unlike traditional AHP method, the proposed F-AHP method is much flexible to deal with intermediate linguistic variable. F-AHP results reveal that wind speed is the most important criteria followed by power Tr. lines with weighted value of 33.05% and 20.95% respectively. The thematic suitability map was developed on Q-GIS platform to classify from “least suitable” to “most suitable” classes. The final suitable map indicates 26.09 km2 and 138.42 km2 area are most suitable and highly suitable respectively for wind farm establishment. This study is also supported with ground measurement wind resource data collected through a WMS (Weather Monitoring Station) installed at “most suitable” region of suitability indexed class. © 2023 Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

Authors: Qutaina, B.; Shehada, A.; Yasin, A.; Alsayed, M.
Title: Geographical information systems based site selection methodology for renewable energy systems in Palestinian territories
Abstract: Renewable energy is the key term for the energy industry sector in the world recently. Palestinian territories (PT) have good potential for multiple renewable energy applications. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the potential sites for solar photovoltaic systems, concentrated solar power systems, and wind farms in the West Bank (WB). The study was derived from geographical information systems (GIS) and multi-criteria decision making (MCDM). The criteria for each application were identified and weighted according to the analytical hierarchy process (AHP). All the resulting layers were multiplied to produce the final suitability map for each application. The results of the study depend on two scenarios, In the first scenario, where area C is included, the areas classified as highly suitable for photovoltaic (PV), concentrated solar power (CSP), and wind turbines are between 14.27 and 7.56 km2. The second scenario is excluding area C, the highly suitable areas were between 4.1 to 2.47 km2 © 2023 Institute of Advanced Engineering and Science. All rights reserved.
Author Keywords: Analytical hierarchy process; Geographical information; Multi-criteria decision-making; Palestinian territories; renewable energy; system
Metodología: Este estudio desarrolla una metodología de selección de sitios para sistemas de energía renovable en los territorios palestinos (Palestinian territories), específicamente en la Ribera Occidental (West Bank). El proceso MCDM se estructuró en tres fases —"intelligence, design, and choice"— donde primero se recolectaron datos y se definieron criterios, para luego formular el análisis multicriterio vinculando los objetivos del decisor con procedimientos precisos. La metodología utilizada integró Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP), justificando su uso debido a que "the MCDM method is a vital tool for site selection as it gives adequate solutions" y que el método AHP es "easy to understand and modify for any future adjustments". El paso a paso incluyó la creación de una matriz de comparación por pares basada en la escala de Saaty y entrevistas con expertos para determinar los pesos de cada factor, traduciendo esto en una matriz para "estimate the weight of each factor". Finalmente, las capas resultantes se multiplicaron en GIS para generar mapas de idoneidad bajo dos escenarios políticos. El estudio empleó un total de 13 variables (o criterios) distribuidas según la tecnología: 5 para sistemas fotovoltaicos (PV), 5 para energía solar de concentración (CSP) y 5 para granjas eólicas, con algunas variables compartidas entre aplicaciones. Específicamente, para los sistemas fotovoltaicos se citan "Grid Connection Point Proximity, Slope, Solar Resource, Road Accessibility" junto con el aspecto del terreno; para CSP se consideran "Slope, Aspect, Road Accessibility, Solar Resource" y proximidad a la red; y para energía eólica se utilizaron "Wind speed, Grid Connection Point Proximity, Road Accessibility, Slope" y distancia a áreas residenciales.

Authors: Bimenyimana, S.; Wang, C.; Asemota, G.N.O.; Ihirwe, J.P.; Tuyizere, M.N.; Mwizerwa, F.; Mo, Y.; Abiyese, M.
Title: Geospatial Analysis of Wind Energy Siting Suitability in the East African Community
Abstract: Site investigation is essential for developing and constructing a dependable and effective wind engineering project. Also, the kinetic energy of moving air, used to drive a wind turbine, produces electricity. Having seen the shortage of previous studies on wind energy sites’ suitability across Africa and having read about the abundance of untapped wind energy resources in the East African region, this paper used Geographical Information System (GIS), multi-criteria, and Analytic Hierarchy techniques to provide a geospatial analysis of wind energy technology siting suitability in Eastern African Community Countries. Different data were acquired and processed from numerous open-access databases (Global Wind atlas, Regional Center for Mapping of Resources for Development (RCMRD), African Geoportal, East African community website, and Energy data Info.org). The results reveal Kenya has large parts of its land areas highly appropriate for wind energy siting (15.26%) and 1.55% of its land classified as unsuitable for wind energy generation. The rates of suitability and unsuitability were respectively 26.57% and 4.87% for Burundi, 20.6% and 10.21% for Rwanda, 20.39% and 10.44% for Tanzania, and 4.65% and 27.15% for South Sudan. The findings also show that East Africa exhibits moderate levels of wind energy siting suitability, with an estimated average of around 37.27% of its land area moderately suitable for wind energy technology installation, covering thousands of square kilometers. The study is advantageous to academia and industry-related personnel engaged in renewable energy-related activities in other African countries with similar topographies. © 2024 by the authors.
Author Keywords: climate change; East Africa; geographical information system; spatial analysis; wind energy
Index Keywords: GIS; kinetic energy; spatial analysis; wind power; wind turbine; Africa
Metodología: Este estudio realiza un análisis geoespacial para la ubicación de energía eólica en la East African Community (EAC), cubriendo específicamente seis países: Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, South Sudan, Kenya, y Tanzania. El proceso MCDM se llevó a cabo paso a paso comenzando con la selección de criterios técnicos y ambientales, donde "wind speed, slope, elevation, land use land cover, distance from power lines, and distance from major roads are among the factors that underwent assessment" mediante el uso de software GIS. La metodología aplicada fue el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) combinado con Geographic Information Systems (GIS). Los autores justifican esta elección indicando que "the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) plays a pivotal role in deriving these final weights" y que el uso de GIS permite "to swiftly and precisely collect data over wider areas". El proceso continuó con la estandarización de criterios en una escala del 1 al 5 y la asignación de pesos de importancia relativa, donde se determinó que "wind speed held the highest priority weight (40%)". Finalmente, se utilizó el método de superposición ponderada (weighted overlay) para generar mapas de idoneidad, calculando un "suitability index (SI)" para cada punto del mapa. El estudio utilizó un total de 6 variables principales para el análisis de idoneidad, identificadas explícitamente como "six criteria (C0) are integrated into the research’s GIS platform" , las cuales fueron "C1: wind speed, C2: slope, C3: elevation, C4: distance from power lines, C5: land use land cover, and C6: distance from roads"

Authors: Tumelienė, E.; Sužiedelytė Visockienė, J.; Malienė, V.
Title: Evaluating the Eligibility of Abandoned Agricultural Land for the Development of Wind Energy in Lithuania
Abstract: Land requirements of wind power (WP) are often seen as a constraint to future broad-scale deployment. The aim of the study is to evaluate the eligibility of abandoned agricultural land (AAL) areas, covered with woody plants, for the development of wind energy (WE) in Lithuania. Agricultural land abandonment (ALA) has numerous negative aspects and its use for WE must be a profitable choice for the landowner, as for the energy developers also. A newly developed methodological approach, a multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) method known as TOPSIS (the Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution) was applied to select suitable areas for wind power plants (WPP). The authors have used various data sets, as follows: protected areas (reserves, parks) combined into one common geographic information system (GIS) layer, forest cadaster data, water (lakes, rivers) area layer; abandoned land area layer; buildings layer, taken from the Lithuania Georeferenced Data Base. The results were generated for the entire territory of Lithuania and separately for AAL using the algorithms of the open source QGIS program. The results showed that the central part of Lithuania is most suitable for the development of WE. However, ALA in this part is low, because of the high soil yield potential and suitable conditions for farming. According to the selection criteria, about 7% of AAL are suitable for WE, and more than 18% of AAL have an average suitability. © 2022 by the authors.
Author Keywords: abandoned agricultural land; MCDA method; renewable energy; wind energy
Index Keywords: abandoned land; agricultural land; alternative energy; decision making; GIS; power plant; protected area; wind power; woody plant; Lithuania
Metodología: Este estudio evalúa la idoneidad de las tierras agrícolas abandonadas (abandoned agricultural land - AAL) para el desarrollo de energía eólica en Lituania. El proceso MCDM se realizó siguiendo una metodología que combinó el uso de Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método TOPSIS (Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution), el cual fue seleccionado por ser "computationally efficient" y proporcionar un mecanismo efectivo para manejar múltiples criterios. El proceso paso a paso comenzó con la preparación de datos vectoriales (áreas protegidas, bosques, agua, edificios y tierras abandonadas) y su transformación a imágenes ráster donde "pixels acquired values of 1 (for objects) and values of 0 (no objects)". Posteriormente, se realizó una reclasificación basada en la proximidad a estos objetos, asignando valores de 10, 50 y 100 para indicar si el área era "not suitable", de "average suitability" o "fully suitable". La justificación técnica para este procedimiento radica en que el método TOPSIS permite compensaciones entre criterios, donde un mal resultado en uno puede ser mitigado por uno bueno en otro, basándose en la idea de que la mejor alternativa es la que tiene la "shortest distance to the ideal solution". Finalmente, se aplicó un análisis de superposición ponderada (overlay analysis) mediante una calculadora ráster para generar los mapas finales de idoneidad. El estudio utilizó un total de 4 variables (o criterios) principales de restricción espacial, citados como "1. distance from forests; 2. distance from buildings; 3. distance from water reservoirs (lakes, rivers); 4. land does not belong to a protected area", además de considerar el potencial de velocidad del viento (mean wind speed) como factor de contexto.

Authors: Subramani, S.; Rao, R.; Shekar, S.; Dsilva Winfred Rufuss, D.; Rajan, A.J.; Rusho, M.A.; Navas, R.K.B.
Title: Application of multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) for site selection of offshore wind farms in India
Abstract: Wind energy is one of the promising alternatives to a clean and sustainable source of energy that guarantees a better tomorrow. This research work proposes a new Multi-Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) integrated with Geographical Information System (GIS) approach to determine the optimal locations for the installation of offshore wind farms in Gujarat, India, under the country's renewable energy program. Four major criteria, viz., wind speed, optimum depth, distance from the shore, and relative humidity, have been considered during the site selection. An analytical hierarchy process (AHP) has been applied to determine the relative weights of each of the criteria. Pairwise comparisons of the criteria were obtained through a thorough review of literature. The weights obtained through the AHP were then applied in QGIS®, an open-source Geographic Information System (GIS) software. The final wind farm suitability map was obtained using a Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) method. The suitability map has been divided into five zones, and a second-level AHP was conducted with respect to the criteria to determine the most optimal zone. The results of this research work will be a useful tool for policymakers, energy commissions, and other stakeholders to determine the most optimal location to install wind farms for maximum efficiency. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Analytical Hierarchy process; Geographical information systems; Multi-criteria decision making; Offshore wind farms; Wind energy
Metodología: Este estudio propone un enfoque integrado de Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) y Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP) para determinar las ubicaciones óptimas de parques eólicos marinos en Gujarat, India. El proceso MCDM se realizó paso a paso iniciando con la identificación de factores críticos mediante una revisión de literatura y opiniones de expertos, seguido de la construcción de una matriz de comparación por pares utilizando la escala de Saaty para "determine the relative weights of each of the criteria". Los autores justifican esta metodología señalando que "AHP allows pairwise comparison of the criteria, whereas GIS allows spatial visualization and weighted overlay analysis", lo que lo convierte en un sistema de soporte de decisiones robusto para capturar la experiencia en la selección de sitios. Una innovación clave fue la aplicación de un "two-level AHP procedure" que, tras generar un mapa de idoneidad inicial mediante el método de Combinación Lineal Ponderada (WLC), realizó una segunda validación para priorizar las zonas identificadas. Para el análisis se utilizaron 4 variables principales, citadas explícitamente como "four major criteria, viz., wind speed, optimum depth, distance from the shore, and relative humidity". Estos criterios fueron reclasificados en una escala de 1 a 9 y procesados en la plataforma de código abierto QGIS® para obtener el mapa final de idoneidad dividido en cinco niveles

Authors: Flora, I.F.
Title: “Monte Carlo and Fuzzy AHP with GIS for ranking hybrid solar-wind sites for electricity and hydrogen production in Cameroon”
Abstract: This study evaluates the potential for hybrid renewable energy production in Cameroon by integrating photovoltaic (PV), concentrated solar power (CSP), and wind energy systems. Advanced decision-making methodologies, including Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (FAHP), Monte Carlo Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (MFAHP), and Geographic Information Systems (GIS), were applied to identify optimal sites for electricity and hydrogen production, accounting for technical, economic, and social factors. The analysis highlights the critical role of Global Horizontal Irradiation (GHI), Direct Normal Irradiation (DNI), and wind speed in determining site suitability. Results show that 42.33% of the area is classified as “Not Suitable” across all models, while “Most Suitable” zones differ by method, with 4.62% in AHP, 4.72% in FAHP, and 4.76% in MFAHP. Significant differences in energy potential were observed, with hybrid systems in highly suitable areas potentially generating up to 20,379.67 TWh/year of electricity and 387,937,880.29 tonnes/year of hydrogen. The methodology was validated by comparing predicted “highly suitable” areas with existing solar plants in Maroua and Guider, confirming the reliability of the model. A sensitivity analysis with four scenarios emphasizes the impact of criteria weighting on site selection. Scenario S0 prioritizes technical potential, S1 economic feasibility, S2 social considerations, and S3 balances productivity with social acceptance. This research improves renewable energy site evaluation by addressing uncertainties and optimizing multi-criteria decision-making tools. The findings provide a comprehensive framework for sustainable energy planning in Cameroon and similar regions. Future research should expand to include additional environmental and socio-economic factors to further refine site selection and facilitate the development of hybrid renewable energy systems. © 2025
Author Keywords: Cameroon; Electricity production; Geographic information system (GIS); Hybrid solar-wind system; Hydrogen production; Monte Carlo; Multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Decision making; Hierarchical systems; Solar power plants; Windmill; Cameroon; Electricity production; Geographic information; Geographic information system; Hybrid solar-wind system; Monte carlo; Multi criteria decision-making; Multi-criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Sensitivity analysis
Metodología: Este estudio se centra en Cameroon, país ubicado en África Central. La investigación emplea un método MCDM híbrido avanzado denominado "Monte Carlo Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (MFAHP)" integrado con "Geographic Information Systems (GIS)". El proceso se realizó paso a paso comenzando con el "Step 1: Define the Problem and Criteria" para identificar factores económicos, ambientales y sociales, seguido del "Step 2: Develop the Hierarchical Structure". Posteriormente, se ejecutó el "Step 3: Perform Fuzzy Pairwise Comparisons" utilizando una escala lingüística del 1 al 9 para evaluar la importancia relativa, lo que permitió el "Step 4: Construct the Fuzzy Comparison Matrix" y el "Step 5: Calculate Fuzzy Weights" mediante la media geométrica. Tras la "Step 6: Defuzzification of Weights", se integró el proceso en una "Step 7: Monte Carlo Simulation" con 1000 iteraciones para generar escenarios aleatorios que reflejen posibles resultados. Esta metodología se justifica porque "the Monte Carlo Fuzzy AHP method integrates the strengths of fuzzy AHP with the probabilistic framework of Monte Carlo simulations" , lo cual permite "overcomes the limitations of traditional AHP by considering both data uncertainty and human judgment". El estudio utilizó un total de 13 variables o criterios principales, descritos como "Thirteenth factors GHI solar irradiation, DNI solar irradiation, PVOUT, wind speed, temperature, slope, aspect, elevation, distance to power lines, distance to residential areas, distance to waterways and bodies, distance to main road and railways, and population density"

Authors: Placide, G.; Lollchund, M.R.
Title: Wind farm site selection using GIS-based mathematical modeling and fuzzy logic tools: a case study of Burundi
Abstract: Introduction: The electricity generated from nuclear plants and petroleum-based products has a negative influence on the environment as a whole. It has shown the utility to search out and promote the utilization of renewable, environmentally friendly, and sustainable energy sources such as solar, wind, and geothermal. Nowadays, Wind energy resource has quickly emerged as the world’s fastest-growing energy source. Methods: However, the selection of the most suitable places for developing a wind farm is a crucial challenge that can be seen as a problem of site selection, which involves numerous conflicting variables. Therefore, it is classified as an MCDM (multi-criteria decision-making) problem. The main objective of this research is to determine the best locations in Burundi for the installation of wind farms. The Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (FAHP) was used to weigh the criteria considering their relative importance. This study considers several key factors when determining the optimal location for a wind farm. These factors include wind speed, slope, proximity to the grid network, distance to roads, and land use/land cover (LULC). Furthermore, a geographic information system (GIS) is utilized to generate the final suitability wind farm locations map. Results and Discussion: The obtained results indicate that 20.91% of the whole study area is suitable nevertheless, only 1.96% is tremendously suitable for wind turbine placement. The western part of Burundi is the optimal area for constructing a wind farm, and the most is in Lake Tanganyika. © © 2024 Placide and Lollchund.
Author Keywords: decision-making; fuzzy theory; fuzzy-analytic hierarchy process; restriction factors; wind farm location
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Decision making; Electric utilities; Fuzzy logic; Geographic information systems; Geothermal energy; Land use; Site selection; Wind; Wind power; Decisions makings; Farm sites; Fuzzy analytic hierarchy; Fuzzy theory; Fuzzy-analytic hierarchy process; Fuzzy-Logic; Model logic; Restriction factor; Wind farm; Wind farm location; Location
Metodología: Este estudio propone una metodología avanzada para la selección de sitios óptimos para parques eólicos en Burundi, abordando el vacío de investigación sobre el potencial eólico en este país sin litoral. El proceso MCDM se realizó siguiendo un enfoque integrado que combina Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica Difuso (FAHP), una elección justificada por la capacidad del método para "handle intricate decision-making situations and yield dependable outcomes" y su efectividad para gestionar la incertidumbre y la ambigüedad de los juicios expertos mediante el uso de números difusos en lugar de valores reales. El proceso se desarrolló paso a paso en tres fases principales: primero, se definieron y priorizaron los criterios mediante una matriz de comparación por pares basada en la escala de Saaty, donde "The Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (FAHP) was used to weigh the criteria considering their relative importance" ; en segundo lugar, se realizó un análisis geoespacial exhaustivo para crear capas de mapas digitales clasificados para cada criterio ; y finalmente, se generó el mapa de idoneidad utilizando una técnica de superposición ponderada (weighted overlay analysis) para identificar las ubicaciones más favorables. La justificación técnica de este flujo de trabajo es que el FAHP "seamlessly integrates with GIS" y permite incorporar juicios subjetivos manteniendo la verificación de la consistencia. El estudio empleó un total de 7 variables o factores de evaluación, citados explícitamente como "seven criteria" que incluyen "C1(Wind potential), C2(Slope), C3 (Distance to roads), C4 (Distance to grids), C5(Aspect), C6 (LULC), C7 (Elevation)". Los resultados finales destacaron que la parte occidental de Burundi, especialmente cerca del Lago Tanganyika, es el área óptima, concluyendo que "the western part of Burundi is the optimal area for constructing a wind farm"

Authors: Chaïbi, M.; Ben Ghoulam, E.M.; Khallouk, N.; Tarik, L.; El Yousfi, Y.; El Hmaidi, A.; Berrada, M.; Mabrouki, J.
Title: A novel fuzzy-multi-criteria-GIS-machine learning approach for onshore wind power plant site selection
Abstract: Selecting proper sites for onshore wind power plants (OWPPs) is a challenging task due to the inherent uncertainty in the decision-making process. This paper proposes a novel hybrid methodology that combines fuzzy logic, multi-criteria decision making (MCDM), and machine learning (ML) techniques, based on geographic information system (GIS). First, we standardized seven criteria for selecting OWPP sites using fuzzy logic membership functions. Then, we employed the spherical fuzzy-analytical hierarchy process (SF-AHP) to calculate the weights of the criteria. Subsequently, the weighted aggregated sum product assessment (WASPAS) method was utilized to generate a suitability map for OWPPs in Morocco. The final suitability map’s consistency was validated by comparison with existing or planned wind farms and using two ML models namely, XGBoost and multiple linear regression (MLR). SF-AHP produced a balanced weight distribution, with wind speed (23.40%) being the most significant factor, and soil texture (9.4%) being the least significant. The WASPAS method clearly identified the highly suitable areas for OWPPs in Morocco. XGBoost outperformed MLR and provided excellent testing results (R2 = 0.9982, RMSE = 0.0043, MAE = 0.0016, and MAPE = 0.5486) demonstrating a good relationship between the seven criteria and the generated wind farm suitability map. The framework validity and stability were further confirmed through comparative and sensitivity analyses. © Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2024.
Author Keywords: Geographic information system; Machine learning; Morocco; Multi-criteria decision making; Onshore wind power plants; Spherical fuzzy-analytical hierarchy process; Weighted aggregated sum product assessment
Metodología: Este estudio se desarrolla en Marruecos, país ubicado en la zona subtropical del noroeste de África. La investigación propone una metodología híbrida que integra Geographic Information Systems (GIS), un método MCDM compuesto por Spherical Fuzzy-Analytical Hierarchy Process (SF-AHP) y Weighted Aggregated Sum Product Assessment (WASPAS), junto con modelos de Machine Learning (ML). El proceso se realizó siguiendo un enfoque de cuatro etapas: primero, "fuzzy membership functions are used to standardize the criteria"; en segundo lugar, "each criterion is assigned a relative weight based on the SF-AHP method"; en tercer lugar, el método "WASPAS is then utilized to rank the locations of OWPPs" (plantas eólicas terrestres); y finalmente, se validaron los resultados comparándolos con parques eólicos existentes y mediante el uso de modelos de aprendizaje automático. La elección de esta metodología se justifica por la necesidad de "handle uncertainty and vagueness associated with experts' judgment" , destacando que el uso de conjuntos difusos esféricos permite recopilar juicios de expertos de manera más "informatively and explicitly" al incluir funciones de membresía, no membresía y duda de forma independiente. El modelo utilizó un total de 7 variables o criterios relevantes, identificados explícitamente como "Wind speed at 100 m, Slope, Distance to transmission lines, Distance to roads, Distance to residential areas, Land cover, and Soil texture".

Authors: AlBraheem, L.; Almutlaq, F.
Title: A Geographic Information System-Based Model and Analytic Hierarchy Process for Wind Farm Site Selection in the Red Sea
Abstract: The wind is one of the most important sources of renewable energy. However, it is associated with many challenges, with one of the most notable being determining suitable locations for wind power farms based on different evaluation criteria. In this study, we investigated the suitability of wind farm sites in the Red Sea off the coast of Saudi Arabia using the analytical hierarchy process (AHP) and a Geographic Information System (GIS). We assessed the suitability of offshore locations for wind energy projects, differentiating between fixed and floating turbines, and identified a 4180 km2 area as less suitable, whereas the 33,094 km2, 20,618 km2, and 11,077 km2 areas were deemed suitable, very suitable, and extremely suitable, respectively. These findings highlight the differences in suitability levels based on specific geographical features. Moreover, the extremely suitable location, which has the largest area of 3032 km2, has the capacity to generate an annual energy output of 56,965,410 MWh/year. © 2024 by the authors.
Author Keywords: AHP; GIS; MCDM; offshore renewable energy; site suitability analysis; wind energy
Metodología: Este estudio se localiza en la costa del Mar Rojo de Arabia Saudita (Red Sea), un área estratégica por sus condiciones climáticas. La investigación utiliza una metodología de GIS-based Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) integrada con el Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP). El proceso se realizó siguiendo un flujo de trabajo riguroso que comenzó con la "Selection of Criteria" (técnicos, económicos y ambientales), seguido de la "Data Collection and Pre-processing" para estandarizar capas de información. El paso central fue la aplicación del método AHP, donde se realizó la "Construction of a Pairwise Comparison Matrix" basada en juicios de expertos para determinar la importancia relativa de cada factor, asegurando la validez mediante el "Consistency Ratio (CR)". Finalmente, se ejecutó la "Weighted Overlay Analysis" en un entorno GIS para generar el mapa de idoneidad. La metodología se justifica porque el enfoque GIS-AHP "is considered one of the most effective and efficient approaches for spatial decision-making" y permite "incorporating the opinions of several specialists in various disciplines" para resolver la complejidad de la ubicación de parques eólicos. El lugar de estudio es la región del Red Sea y el análisis integró un total de 8 variables fundamentales, citadas como "wind speed, elevation, slope, distance to the electrical grid, distance to roads, distance to cities, distance to airports, and distance to protected areas".

Authors: Gacu, J.G.; Garcia, J.D.; Fetalvero, E.G.; Catajay-Mani, M.P.; Monjardin, C.E.F.; Power, C.
Title: A Comprehensive Resource Assessment for Wind Power Generation on the Rural Island of Sibuyan, Philippines
Abstract: Amid rising energy demands in rural areas, thorough resource assessments for initiatives such as wind power are crucial. This study involves a land resource assessment for wind power generation on the rustic Sibuyan Island in the Philippines, which is currently experiencing an electricity shortage. A comprehensive overview of the island’s suitability for wind energy projects is performed via evaluation and analysis using geospatial data and multi-criteria decision making (MCDM). The research results indicate that 50.44% (220.68 km2) of the island’s land area is categorized as ‘poorly suitable’ since it considers protected areas where developments are not allowed. Only 0.08% (0.35 km2) of the island can be classified as ‘marginally suitable’, while 9.15% (40.73 km2), 36.64% (176.39 km2), and 0.69% (3.05 km2) are labeled as ‘moderately suitable’, ‘suitable’, and ‘highly suitable’, respectively. This confirms the potential for wind energy exploration on the island. Delineating the suitability levels provides a foundational framework for stakeholders that enables them to identify optimal sites for wind power, sustain the island’s resources, and contribute to the renewable energy landscape of this rural location. Overall, this study, underpinned by data analysis, offers invaluable insights for decision making in wind power development, with the presented framework adaptable to other areas of interest. © 2024 by the authors.
Author Keywords: analytical hierarchy process; GIS; multi-criteria decision making; renewable energy; resource assessment; rural energy resources
Index Keywords: Conservation; Decision making; Energy policy; Geographic information systems; Power generation; Rural areas; Analytical Hierarchy Process; Energy demands; Multi criteria decision-making; Multicriteria decision-making; Multicriterion decision makings; Philippines; Renewable energies; Resource assessments; Rural energy resources; Wind power generation; Wind power
Metodología: Este estudio se desarrolla en la Sibuyan Island, Romblon (Filipinas), una zona rural que depende actualmente de generadores diésel. La investigación utiliza una metodología de "GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)" que integra el "Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)" para la evaluación de idoneidad. El proceso se realizó de forma sistemática en cuatro etapas: primero, la "Selection of evaluation criteria" basada en literatura previa; segundo, el "Weighting of criteria using AHP", donde se empleó una matriz de comparación por pares para asignar importancia relativa; tercero, el "Data layers and standardization" para convertir todos los datos a un formato ráster común; y finalmente, la "Overlay analysis" para producir el mapa de idoneidad final. La metodología se justifica indicando que el uso de GIS-AHP permite "provide a scientific basis for decision-makers to prioritize investment" y es fundamental porque "AHP helps in reducing the complexity of decision-making by breaking down the problem into a hierarchy", lo que garantiza que se consideren factores técnicos, económicos y ambientales de manera equilibrada. El lugar de estudio es la Sibuyan Island y el análisis utilizó un total de 8 variables críticas, citadas como "wind speed, elevation, slope, land cover, distance to roads, distance to transmission lines, distance to residential areas, and distance to protected areas"

Authors: Richards, D.; Yabar, H.; Mizunoya, T.; Koon-Koon, R.; Tran, G.H.; Esopere, Y.
Title: Balancing Stakeholders’ Perspectives for Sustainability: GIS-MCDM for Onshore Wind Energy Planning
Abstract: This study supports Jamaica’s renewable energy implementation strategies by providing updated wind atlases and identifying suitable locations for future wind farms. Using a GIS-based Analytic Hierarchy Process with multi-criteria decision-making (AHP-MCDM), this research integrates stakeholders’ opinions, environmental considerations, and technical factors to assess land suitability for wind energy development. The analysis reveals that Jamaica has the potential to increase its wind power output by 8.99% compared to the current production of 99 MW. This expansion could significantly contribute to offsetting fossil fuel-based energy consumption and reducing carbon dioxide emissions. It identifies sites across several parishes, including Westmoreland, Clarendon, St. Mary, and St. James, as highly suitable for utility-scale wind farm development. By providing detailed spatial information and estimated energy outputs, this research offers valuable insights for energy planners, investors, and policymakers to create sustainable energy policies and advance Jamaica’s 50% renewable energy goal by 2030. © 2024 by the authors.
Author Keywords: carbon dioxide emission reduction; environmental evaluation; geographic information system (GIS); multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM); spatial energy systems planning
Index Keywords: alternative energy; carbon dioxide; decision making; emission control; GIS; multicriteria analysis; policy making; spatial planning; wind farm; wind power; Jamaica
Metodología: Este estudio se localiza en Jamaica, específicamente enfocado en la isla principal para el desarrollo de energía eólica onshore. La investigación emplea un modelo híbrido de "GIS-MCDM" que utiliza el método Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) bajo tres escenarios de pesaje distintos (Conservador, Equilibrado y Agresivo). El proceso se realizó siguiendo un flujo de trabajo de cuatro fases: primero, la "Identification and Selection of Criteria" basada en literatura y disponibilidad de datos; segundo, la "Data Acquisition and Pre-processing" para estandarizar capas espaciales; tercero, la aplicación del "MCDM-AHP" para el cálculo de pesos mediante comparaciones pareadas; y finalmente, la "GIS-Overlay and Suitability Mapping" utilizando la técnica de combinación lineal ponderada (WLC). Esta metodología se justifica debido a que "AHP is widely used in wind energy planning because it simplifies complex decisions by breaking them into a hierarchical structure" y permite "balancing multiple conflicting criteria such as technical, environmental, and social factors". El lugar de estudio es Jamaica y el análisis integró un total de 10 variables (4 criterios técnicos, 4 ambientales y 2 socioeconómicos), citadas explícitamente como "wind speed, elevation, slope, proximity to grid infrastructure, land cover, distance to protected areas, distance to water bodies, distance to bird migratory routes, distance to roads, and distance to settlement areas"

Authors: Nassif, W.G.; Elhmaidi, D.; Al-Timimi, Y.K.
Title: Selecting Suitable Sites for Wind Energy Harvesting in Iraq using GIS Techniques
Abstract: Wind energy harnesses the kinetic force of the wind through turbines to produce electricity. As a critical renewable energy source, it presents a sustainable alternative to fossil fuels. The availability of wind energy is geographically contingent. This paper aims to pinpoint optimal sites for wind energy development in Iraq and to secure future energy needs from renewable sources to achieve this, a multi-criteria analysis utilizing Geographic Information Systems (GIS) was employed to determine the prime locations for wind energy extraction. Vital climatic data incorporated into this analysis included a RASTER file of the study area's annual wind speed, temperature, precipitation, soil moisture, and NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) roads, urban centers, terrain gradients, and land utilization patterns were instrumental in constructing a GIS-based model. This model harnessed the weighted overlay tool within ArcGIS 10.7.1's Spatial Analysis Tools to classify and integrate various raster datasets. Findings reveal that the most favorable locations for wind energy exploitation are in Iraq's southeastern sectors. These zones are not only marked by vigorous winds averaging speeds over 6.2 meters per second but also benefit from their proximity to power grids, accessible roads, and gentle slopes, making them prime candidates for wind farm installation. The research also notes that regions with a high suitability index for wind energy development constitute 5.3% of the total examined area. This is a robust indicator of Iraq's capacity for wind energy utilization. Meanwhile, 79.7% of the territory received a moderate suitability rating, 9.5% was categorized as poorly suitable, and 5.4% was considered unsuitable for wind energy projects. These insights underscore the significant potential for harvesting wind energy in Iraq and guide strategic planning for its implementation. © 2024 University of Baghdad-College of Science. All rights reserved.
Author Keywords: GIS; Iraq; Multi-criteria; Wind Energy (WE)
Metodología: Para el estudio titulado "Selecting Suitable Sites for Wind Energy Harvesting in Iraq using GIS Techniques", el proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se llevó a cabo mediante un enfoque estructurado que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) y una evaluación multicriterio secuencial para identificar las ubicaciones óptimas de energía eólica en Irak. El procedimiento comenzó con la recopilación de datos climáticos de 40 años, incluyendo velocidad del viento, temperatura y precipitación, junto con parámetros topográficos de un Modelo de Elevación Digital (DEM). La metodología paso a paso se ejecutó de la siguiente manera: primero, se realizó una evaluación técnica centrada en la velocidad del viento a 50 metros ("thorough assessment of technical factors, specifically emphasizing the annual wind speed at a standardized height of about 50 meters") ; segundo, se analizaron criterios económicos y topográficos como la elevación y la pendiente ("elevation data obtained from the Digital Elevation Model playing a crucial role in assessing the topographical suitability") ; tercero, se evaluaron factores ambientales y de infraestructura, como la proximidad a redes eléctricas y carreteras mediante cálculos de distancia euclidiana ("proximity of candidate sites to existing infrastructure... was estimated using Euclidean distance computations") ; y finalmente, se aplicó un filtrado de exclusión para eliminar cuerpos de agua y zonas protegidas. El método MCDM específico utilizado fue el Weighted Overlay Analysis (Análisis de Superposición Ponderada), justificado por la necesidad de estandarizar y combinar capas de objetivos no uniformes para obtener un índice de idoneidad cohesivo ("This model is needed to standardize values across diverse inputs for a cohesive analysis... the analysis components are non-uniform"). En total, se utilizaron 6 variables clave para la ponderación final: "Annual Wind Speed (50 m) high" (40%), "Slope" (20%), "Land cover/use" (10%), "Distance from Cities" (10%), "Distance from Roads" (10%) y "Distance from power line" (10%). El estudio concluye que el uso sistemático de estas herramientas fue "crucial in identifying the most suitable regions for wind energy projects", destacando el sureste de Irak como la zona de mayor potencial.

Authors: Bhasuru, B.A.; Shah, M.; Varshney, S.
Title: A comprehensive GIS-driven multi-criteria framework for offshore floating hybrid wind-solar site selection and performance analysis
Abstract: The increasing global demand for sustainable energy has prompted the exploration of hybrid renewable systems, particularly in offshore environments. This study presents an integrated, Geographic Information System (GIS)-enabled multi-criteria decision framework for the optimal selection of offshore sites suitable for floating hybrid wind–solar platforms. Candidate sites are first filtered using Pareto-based analysis, incorporating environmental, technical, and infrastructural constraints. For the shortlisted sites, a physics-based wind simulation is conducted using the FLoating Offshore wind farm RISer model (FLORIS) model configured with realistic National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) 5-MW reference turbine parameters, while solar resource data from European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) is converted to power output using established efficiency and area assumptions. Additionally, a comprehensive sensitivity analysis is performed using both local and global (Sobol) methods to quantify the influence of key parameters, such as turbulence intensity, wind shear, air density, and turbine spacing, on wind farm performance. The results demonstrate hybrid capacity factors exceeding 45 %, identify air density as accounting for over 60 % of the variance in hybrid yield, and achieve an 80 % reduction in computational screening by clustering 55 741 candidates into 1 000 representative sites. Spatial visualizations in Quantum GIS (QGIS) identify high-potential sites and validate the robustness of the proposed methodology. This integrated approach provides a novel framework for hybrid energy development in offshore settings and offers valuable insights for future large-scale renewable energy deployments. © 2025 The Authors
Author Keywords: Capacity factor; Floating solar; Hybrid energy; Offshore wind; Pareto optimization
Index Keywords: Geographic information systems; Multiobjective optimization; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Offshore wind turbines; Pareto principle; Site selection; Solar energy; Air density; Capacity factors; Floating solar; Geographic information; Hybrid energy; Hybrid wind; Offshore winds; Offshores; Pareto-optimization; Wind farm; Sensitivity analysis
Metodología: Para el estudio de la selección de sitios para plataformas híbridas eólicas y solares flotantes en la Zona Económica Exclusiva (ZEE) de la India 1111, se utilizó un marco de trabajo integrado que combina Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el método de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) basado en el filtrado de Pareto2222. El proceso se realizó paso a paso comenzando con la adquisición y preprocesamiento de datos de 55,741 sitios candidatos, los cuales fueron discretizados en celdas de $0.05^{\circ}\times0.05^{\circ}$3. Posteriormente, se aplicó un análisis de optimización de Pareto para identificar soluciones no dominadas, una metodología elegida específicamente para evitar la necesidad de ponderaciones subjetivas (como AHP o métodos de entropía), ya que "it equally treats all seven criteria and identifies non-dominated solutions without aggregating objectives into a single index"4444. Para manejar la gran cantidad de resultados, se implementó un refinamiento basado en clústeres (K-means) que redujo los candidatos a 1,000 sitios representativos para simulaciones de alta fidelidad5555. En total, se utilizaron 7 variables (criterios) divididas en tres categorías: calidad del recurso (velocidad del viento y radiación solar), viabilidad técnica (idoneidad batimétrica) e infraestructura y restricciones ambientales (distancia a líneas eléctricas, subestaciones, puertos y áreas protegidas)6666. El estudio justifica este enfoque integral señalando que "integrating these datasets with geospatial information and applying multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) techniques can lead to more robust site selection" 7, permitiendo un equilibrio entre los objetivos ambientales, técnicos y económicos de manera objetiva y transparente8888

Authors: El-Hosiny, A.M.; El-Ghareeb, H.; Shabana, B.T.; Aboelfetouh, A.
Title: Sustainable Energy Production’s Spatial Determination Framework, Based on Multi Criteria Decision Making and Geographic Information System Under Neutrosophic Environment: A Case Study in Egypt
Abstract: The development of wind energy projects (WEP) have been encouraged, since the last decade. Therefore, WEP grows exponentially, which makes wind energy the trend of energy production for many countries. The success of wind energy project relies on the choice of the appropriate site for wind power plant, often decided by the application of Multi Criteria Decision Making (MCDM). The MCDM methodologies for location selection have a range of shortcomings: (1) the incomplete use of knowledge, (2) the lack of evidence in the decision-making process; and (3) the problem of ignoring the interaction between parameters. This paper presents a new framework for the location selection of wind power stations, based on the incorporating of geographic information system (GIS) and analytical network process (ANP) through neutrosophic environment to cover MCDM's shortcoming. First, an assessment model is built for wind farm site selection. Then, in the specialist committee decision, the bipolar neutrosophic set is used to express missing knowledge. In addition, we take the relationship problem into account by collecting the opinions of experts. Finally, the GIS is used to determine the wind farm potential zones. The suggested framework for the identification of wind farm sites is validated by the use of a case study from Egypt. © 2021. All Rights Reserved.
Author Keywords: GIS; MCDM; neutrosophic; WEP
Index Keywords: Decision making; Electric utilities; Information systems; Information use; Power plants; Site selection; Wind power; Analytical network processes (ANP); Decision making process; Energy productions; Location selection; Multi-criteria decision making; Sustainable energy; Wind energy projects; Wind power stations; Geographic information systems
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "Sustainable Energy Production's Spatial Determination Framework, Based on Multi Criteria Decision Making and Geographic Information System Under Neutrosophic Environment: A Case Study in Egypt" , el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en la Península del Sinaí, Egipto. El proceso MCDM se realizó paso a paso integrando el Proceso de Red Analítica (ANP) con Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) bajo un entorno neutrosófico bipolar. La metodología comenzó con una etapa preliminar de adquisición de datos y preprocesamiento , seguida por la identificación de áreas restringidas mediante lógica booleana y herramientas de "buffer" en ArcGIS para excluir zonas no aptas como áreas residenciales o protegidas. Posteriormente, se realizó la estandarización de criterios en una escala común de idoneidad y se aplicó el método Bipolar Neutrosophic ANP para determinar los pesos de los criterios. Este proceso incluyó la consulta a expertos mediante expresiones lingüísticas transformadas en números neutrosóficos bipolares, la construcción de matrices de comparación por pares y la generación de una supermatriz para manejar las interdependencias entre factores. Finalmente, se utilizó la Combinación Lineal Ponderada (WLC) para agregar los criterios y generar el mapa de idoneidad final. El uso de este enfoque específico se justifica porque permite manejar la incertidumbre y la información incompleta de manera más efectiva que los métodos difusos tradicionales: "BNS is applied with ANP method and GIS to add to the field of wind power station literature... based on bipolar neutrosophic set (BNS) that can handle vagueness, indeterminacy and improve reliability". En total, se utilizaron 11 variables (criterios) clasificadas en tres grupos: factores naturales (velocidad del viento, elevación, pendiente, dirección del aspecto y dirección del viento), factores ambientales (proximidad a aeropuertos, distancia a áreas de interés ambiental y cobertura del suelo) y factores socioeconómicos (proximidad a la red eléctrica, ciudades y carreteras).

Authors: Cheng, C.; Silalahi, D.F.; Roberts, L.; Nadolny, A.; Weber, T.; Blakers, A.; Catchpole, K.
Title: Heatmaps to Guide Siting of Solar and Wind Farms
Abstract: The decarbonization of the electricity system coupled with the electrification of transport, heat, and industry represents a practical and cost-effective approach to deep decarbonization. A key question is as follows: where to build new solar and wind farms? This study presents a cost-based approach to evaluate land parcels for solar and wind farm suitability using colour-coded heatmaps that visually depict favourable locations. An indicative cost of electricity is calculated and classified for each pixel by focusing on key factors including the resource availability, proximity to transmission infrastructure and load centres, and exclusion of sensitive areas. The proposed approach mitigates the subjectivity associated with traditional multi-criteria decision-making methods, in which both the selection of siting factors and the assignment of their associated weightings rely highly on the subjective judgements of experts. The methodology is applied to Australia, South Korea, and Indonesia, and the results show that proximity to high-voltage transmission and load centres is a key factor affecting site selection in Australia and Indonesia, while connection costs are less critical in South Korea due to its smaller land area and extensive infrastructure. The outcomes of this study, including heatmaps and detailed statistics, are made publicly available to provide both qualitative and quantitative information that allows comparisons between regions and within a region. This study aims to empower policymakers, developers, communities, and individual landholders to make informed decisions and, ultimately, to facilitate strategic renewable energy deployment and contribute to global decarbonization. © 2025 by the authors.
Author Keywords: GIS analysis; heatmap; resource assessment; solar photovoltaics; wind energy
Index Keywords: Site selection; Solar power generation; Australia; Decarbonisation; Energy; GIS analysis; Heatmaps; Key factors; Load centers; Resource assessments; Solar and wind farms; Solar photovoltaics; Decision making
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "Heatmaps to Guide Siting of Solar and Wind Farms", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en Australia, Indonesia y Corea del Sur 1111utilizando un enfoque de Análisis de Superposición Ponderada (Weighted Overlay Analysis) basado en costos, implementado mediante Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS)2222. El proceso se realizó paso a paso de la siguiente manera: primero, se calcularon las distancias euclidianas a los centros de carga y a la red de transmisión existente para cada píxel de $1 \text{ km} \times 1 \text{ km}$ (solar) o $250 \text{ m} \times 250 \text{ m}$ (eólica) 3333; segundo, se determinaron los factores de capacidad basados en recursos solares y eólicos globales 4; tercero, se aplicó un filtrado binario para excluir áreas sensibles como parques nacionales, zonas urbanas, bosques nativos y pendientes excesivas para paneles solares 55555555; y finalmente, se calculó un "indicative cost of electricity (USD/MWh)" para cada celda utilizando una fórmula que integra CAPEX, OPEX y costos de transmisión666666666. La metodología se justifica explícitamente para evitar la subjetividad de otros métodos MCDM tradicionales, señalando que "The proposed approach mitigates the subjectivity associated with traditional multi-criteria decision-making methods, in which both the selection of siting factors and the assignment of their associated weightings rely highly on the subjective judgements of experts"7777. En este estudio se utilizaron 7 variables principales integradas en el modelo final: "solar and wind resources", "proximity to high-voltage transmission infrastructure", "proximity to load centres", "protected areas", "urban areas", "native forests" y "slope (for solar only)"8888.
país: Australia, Indonesia y Corea del Sur

Authors: Khan, F.; Fuinhas, J.A.; Rapposelli, A.; Talpur, S.A.
Title: A comprehensive spatial decision support system for future floating offshore wind farm development in Italy
Abstract: To be more energy autonomous and engage in sustainable energy production, Italy is also shifting towards deploying its first floating-based offshore wind farms in the country. However, the whole process is impeded by a lack of technical frameworks and complex and lengthy regulatory procedures. The viability of such projects depends on site suitability analysis which needs to accommodate various aspects of socio-economic, environmental, and technical aspects. The novel model developed in this study is based on employing the Geographic Information System (GIS) to manage the spatial criteria and the non-linear multi-criteria optimization approach known as fuzzy Full Consistency Method (FUCOM) to evaluate the spatial evaluation criteria. The spatial data is based on the Italian regulatory frameworks and best practices in Europe. Finally, the weighted overlay tool in ArcGIS software is employed to yield the most suitable zones for the deployment of future first floating-based offshore wind turbines in the Sicilian Strait. The highly suitable zones cover an area of 5951.290 Km2 which is found to be very encouraging for the future offshore wind farm industry in the country. © 2025
Author Keywords: Floating-based offshore wind farms; Fuzzy FUCOM; GIS; Sicily; Weighted overlay analysis
Index Keywords: Economics; Multiobjective optimization; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Offshore wind turbines; Sustainable development; Floating-based offshore wind farm; Fuzzy full consistency method; Geographic information; Offshore winds; Overlay analysis; Sicily; Spatial decision support systems; Weighted overlay analyze; Wind farm; Wind farm development; Geographic information systems; alternative energy; decision support system; floating offshore structure; fuzzy mathematics; GIS; optimization; spatial data; wind farm; Italy
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "A comprehensive spatial decision support system for future floating offshore wind farm development in Italy", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en el Estrecho de Sicilia, Italia. El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso mediante un marco de apoyo a la decisión espacial que integra el Geographic Information System (GIS) con un enfoque de optimización no lineal denominado fuzzy Full Consistency Method (FUCOM). La metodología comenzó con la identificación del problema y la determinación de criterios de evaluación y exclusión basados en regulaciones italianas y mejores prácticas de la Unión Europea para "minimize subjectivity". Seguidamente, se recolectaron capas de datos espaciales procesadas en ArcGIS y se aplicó el método fuzzy FUCOM para asignar pesos a los criterios de evaluación. Este proceso MCDM específico se justifica porque, a diferencia de métodos tradicionales como AHP o BWM, el fuzzy FUCOM "ensures higher consistency and requires fewer pairwise comparisons" y es "more reliable and robust as opposed to conventional AHP and BWM approaches". El paso final consistió en un Análisis de Superposición Ponderada (Weighted Overlay Analysis) en ArcGIS para generar el mapa de idoneidad final, multiplicando los valores de cada celda por los pesos derivados del modelo MCDM. En total, se utilizaron 15 variables (criterios) para evaluar el área, incluyendo factores técnicos, ambientales, económicos y sociales, destacándose la inclusión novedosa de infraestructuras existentes de petróleo y gas como criterio de exclusión ("inclusion of existing offshore oil and gas platforms as exclusion criteria—an aspect overlooked in previous literature"). Entre las variables citadas se encuentran: "wind speed", "water depth", "sediment thickness", "distance from airports", "vessel density", "offshore oil and gas rigs", "distance from submarine cables" y "bird migratory routes".

Authors: Chalastani, V.I.; Feloni, E.; Duarte, C.M.; Tsoukala, V.K.
Title: Identifying Sustainable Offshore Wind Farm Sites in Greece Under Climate Change
Abstract: Wind power has gained attention as a vital renewable energy source capable of reducing emissions and serving as an effective alternative to fossil fuels. Floating wind farms could significantly enhance the energy capacities of Mediterranean countries. However, location selection for offshore wind farms (OWFs) is a challenge for renewable energy policy and marine spatial planning (MSP). To address these issues, this study considers the marine space of Greece to propose a GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) framework employing the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to identify suitable sites for OWFs. The approach assesses 19 exclusion criteria encompassing legislative, environmental, safety, and technical constraints to determine the eligible areas. Subsequently, 10 evaluation criteria are weighted to determine the selected areas’ level of suitability. The study considers baseline conditions (1981–2010) and future climate scenarios based on RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 for two horizons (2011–2040 and 2041–2070), integrating projected wind velocities and sea level rise to evaluate potential shifts in suitable areas. Results indicate the central and southeastern Aegean Sea as the most suitable areas for OWF deployment. Climate projections indicate a modest increase in suitable areas. The findings serve as input for climate-resilient MSP seeking to promote sustainable energy development. © 2025 by the authors.
Author Keywords: analytic hierarchy process (AHP); climate change; floating offshore wind farms; Greece; marine spatial planning; sustainable planning
Index Keywords: Analytic hierarchy process; Decision making; Electric utilities; Energy policy; Hierarchical systems; Offshore oil well production; Offshore wind farms; Sustainable development; Analytic hierarchy; Farm sites; Floating offshore wind farm; Greece; Hierarchy process; Marine Spatial Planning; Offshore winds; Sustainable planning; Wind farm; Climate change
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "Identifying Sustainable Offshore Wind Farm Sites in Greece Under Climate Change", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en la zona marina de Grecia, abarcando su Zona Económica Exclusiva (ZEE). El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso siguiendo un marco estructurado de seis etapas que integra Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS) con el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica (AHP). La metodología comenzó con la definición de los límites del área de estudio y la recopilación de datos espaciales públicos. Posteriormente, se aplicó una etapa de exclusión utilizando lógica booleana para eliminar áreas inviables según 19 criterios técnicos, legales y ambientales (como profundidades inadecuadas o zonas militares), lo cual se justifica para "ensure the selection of only the most favorable areas... by comprehensively accounting for legislative, environmental, techno-economic, and safety constraints". En la fase de evaluación, las áreas restantes se analizaron mediante el método de Combinación Lineal Ponderada (WLC), donde los pesos de importancia se determinaron a través de comparaciones por pares realizadas por un panel de 15 expertos, asegurando un "systematic, and participatory weighting process". El estudio justifica la elección de AHP debido a su popularidad y fiabilidad derivadas de su "simplicity and ease of application". Una característica distintiva de este proceso fue la integración de escenarios de cambio climático (RCP 4.5 y 8.5) para evaluar la resiliencia de los sitios a largo plazo, ya que "by considering future climate scenarios, planners can assess future conditions, identify areas that will remain viable... and reduce potential damages". En total, se utilizaron 29 variables en el modelo: 19 criterios de exclusión (ExC) —incluyendo "water depth", "average wind velocity", "military exercise areas", "marine routes" y diversas zonas protegidas (Natura 2000, IBAs, praderas de posidonia)— y 10 criterios de evaluación (EvC) para determinar el nivel de idoneidad, tales como "wind velocity" (que recibió el mayor peso con 23.20%), "distance from ports" y "proximity to energy infrastructure".

Authors: Sotto, P.J.T.; Abuan, B.E.; Danao, L.A.M.
Title: Suitability Investigation and Technical Assessment Using Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis for Offshore Wind Farms in the Philippines
Abstract: This study aims to identify the potential sites based on a set of decision criteria and employ a Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) method. Geographical data were processed into standardized layers by utilizing the Geographic Information System (GIS) capability of ArcGIS Desktop. The final suitability maps were generated from the product of the weighted analysis for factors layers, Boolean Raster from the constraints model, and wind speed datasets. Eight scenarios were produced in which Scenario 3A and Scenario 4A were selected as the most preferred set-up for bottom-fixed set-up and floatingtype platforms, respectively. These two scenarios adopted the averaged factors and 200 m altitude datasets as they provided the highest technical resource potential for wind energy extraction of 710.78 GW for shallow bathymetric maps and 4,054.44 GW for deep waters. Locations with a suitability score index of 0.6 -1.0 were observed as having good quality wind resources ranging from 9.07 m/s to 11.67 m/s. They were in the north of Ilocos Norte, the southeast portion of Oriental Mindoro, the southern part of Iloilo province, and some sections in Guimaras Strait. Finally, Wind Turbine #1 (Enercon E-126 7.580) was favored as it showed the largest Annual Energy Production (AEP) of 30,336,475 MWh/y for Scenario 3A and 172,894,869 MWh/y for Scenario 4A. © 2023, AIDIC Servizi S.r.l.
Metodología: El estudio se localiza en "the Philippines" y utiliza como metodología principal el "Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) method" integrado con un "Geographic Information System (GIS)". El proceso se realizó paso a paso comenzando con la definición de criterios donde "Decision criteria were pre-determined and extrapolated from previous research" , seguido de la recolección de juicios de expertos donde "Responses from various experts in the field were gathered as necessary input in ranking the pairwise elements in order of importance" , y finalizando con una validación técnica mediante un "decision loop acted as a filter to check the consistency of the responses". La justificación para utilizar AHP se basa en que "was used to systematically generate weights of intangible elements and rank relevant items in order of priority during decision-making" , fundamentándose en que es un modelo "based on a mathematical model of aggregated individual judgments and is considered neutral and objective". Se utilizaron un total de 13 variables, divididas específicamente en "eight factors (wind resource, bathymetry, physical oceanography, geological hazards, distance to shore, substations, logistical access point, and shipwreck) and five constraints (marine protected areas, marine habitat, biological behavior, submarine assets, and aviation)"

Authors: Rekik, S.; Khabbouchi, I.; El Alimi, S.
Title: A Spatial Analysis for Optimal Wind Site Selection from a Sustainable Supply-Chain-Management Perspective
Abstract: Finding optimal locations for wind farms requires a delicate balance between maximizing energy generation potential and addressing the socio-economic implications for local communities, particularly in regions facing socio-economic challenges. While existing research often focuses on technical and economic aspects of wind farm siting, this study addresses a crucial research gap by integrating sustainable supply-chain-management principles into a comprehensive site-selection framework. We present a novel approach that combines Geographic-Information-System-based spatial analysis, the Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process, and multi-criteria decision-making techniques to identify and prioritize optimal wind farm locations in Tunisia. Our framework considers not only traditional factors, like wind speed, terrain slope, and road and grid infrastructure, but also crucial socio-economic indicators, such as unemployment rates, population density, skilled workforce availability, and land cost. Based on the spatial analysis, it was revealed that 33,138 km2 was appropriate for deploying large-scale wind systems, of which 6912 km2 (4.39% of the total available area) was categorized as “most suitable”. Considering the SSCM evaluation criteria, despite the minor variations, the ARAS, COPRAS, EDAS, MOORA, VIKOR, and WASPAS techniques showcased that Kasserine, Kebili, and Bizerte stood as ideal locations for hosting large-scale wind systems. These rankings were further validated by the Averaging, Borda, and Copeland methods. By incorporating this framework, the study identifies locations where wind energy development can be a catalyst for economic growth, social upliftment, and improved livelihoods. This holistic approach facilitates informed decision making for policymakers and investors, thus ensuring that wind energy projects contribute to a more sustainable and equitable future for all stakeholders. © 2025 by the authors.
Author Keywords: fuzzy AHP; GIS; MCDM; onshore wind systems; optimal locations; sustainable development; sustainable supply chain management (SSCM); Tunisia
Index Keywords: decision making; fuzzy mathematics; GIS; holistic approach; spatial analysis; stakeholder; supply chain management; sustainable development; wind farm; Bizerte; Kasserine; Kebili; Tunisia
MCDM method: FAHP, ARAS, COPRAS, EDAS, MOORA, VIKOR, WASPAS
Metodología: Para el artículo titulado "A Spatial Analysis for Optimal Wind Site Selection from a Sustainable Supply-Chain-Management Perspective", el proceso de selección de sitios se llevó a cabo en Túnez. El proceso de Toma de Decisiones Multicriterio (MCDM) se realizó paso a paso integrando Sistemas de Información Geográfica (GIS), el Proceso de Jerarquía Analítica Difuso (Fuzzy AHP) y seis técnicas adicionales de MCDM para el ranking final.
